| 13 | Pareto Optimality | $\nexists \, x' : \forall k, f_k(x') \leq f_k(x) \wedge \exists k, f_k(x') < f_k(x)$ | — | DSE selection |

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** In the table above, all absolute values and cardinalities use the LaTeX `\vert` operator (e.g., $\vert S \vert$, $\vert P \vert$) to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. **Do not** write `|S|` inside a table.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Issues to Models

The following matrix is **exam-critical**. It maps each *fundamental issue* to the *program model* that best addresses it, and to the *design stage* where the issue arises. KTU 2024 questions frequently ask: *"Which model is best suited for X?"* — this matrix is your answer key.

| Fundamental Issue | Best Program Model | Design Stage | Resolution Mechanism |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Reactive control logic | FSM (Mealy/Moore) | Specification / Modelling | Enumerate states, define transitions |
| Computational kernels | DFG | Partitioning / Synthesis | Critical-path analysis, ILP binding |
| Program structure analysis | CFG | Co-Simulation / WCET | Basic-block profiling, BB sorting |
| Joint HW/SW representation | CDFG | High-Level Synthesis | Scheduling, allocation, binding |
| Concurrency, synchronization | Petri Net | Co-Verification | Reachability, liveness analysis |
| Hierarchical, complex states | PSM / Statecharts | Specification | State hierarchy, parallel regions |
| Performance estimation | Annotated DFG + cost tables | DSE | Static timing analysis, simulation |
| Interface synthesis | Bus-functional model | Co-Synthesis | Wrapper generation, register mapping |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** In 14-mark questions, you are often given a scenario (e.g., "design a digital camera controller") and asked to (i) choose appropriate models, (ii) justify the choice, (iii) sketch the model. Use this matrix as the *justification engine*.

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation: Amdahl's Law Applied to Co-Design

Amdahl's Law bounds the maximum speedup achievable when a fraction $f$ of a task is accelerated by a factor of $N$ (e.g., moved to hardware $N$ times faster).

### Setup
Let the original software execution time be $T_{SW}$. Partition a fraction $f$ of it to a hardware accelerator that runs $N$ times faster. The remaining fraction $(1-f)$ stays in software.

### Step 1 — Decompose execution time

$$T_{SW} = f \cdot T_{SW} + (1-f) \cdot T_{SW}$$

*The first term is the time spent in the HW-candidate portion, the second in the SW-only portion.*

### Step 2 — Apply acceleration to the HW portion

$$T_{HW} = \dfrac{f \cdot T_{SW}}{N} + (1-f) \cdot T_{SW}$$

*The HW-candidate now takes $1/N$ the time; the SW portion is unchanged.*

### Step 3 — Compute speedup ratio

$$S = \dfrac{T_{SW}}{T_{HW}} = \dfrac{T_{SW}}{\dfrac{f \cdot T_{SW}}{N} + (1-f) \cdot T_{SW}}$$

### Step 4 — Factor out $T_{SW}$ (it cancels)

$$S = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{f}{N} + (1-f)}$$

### Step 5 — Final form (Amdahl's Law)

$$\boxed{S = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{N}}}$$

### Step 6 — Limiting cases
*   If $N \to \infty$ (HW infinitely fast): $S_{max} = \dfrac{1}{1-f}$ — bound depends only on the *unaccelerated* fraction.
*   If $f = 1$ (entire task moved to HW): $S = N$.
*   If $f = 0.5$, $N = 10$: $S = 1 / (0.5 + 0.05) = 1.818$ — only 1.8× faster despite 10× HW speed, because half the code remains sequential.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Implication for Co-Design:** Amdahl's Law proves that **partitioning only the inner loop is rarely sufficient**. The architect must attack the *critical path* end-to-end, otherwise speedup saturates. This is the mathematical reason why heterogeneous architectures (CPU + GPU + FPGA + ASIC) coexist.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation: DFG Critical Path & Latency Bound

Consider a simple FIR filter computation:

$$y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{3} h[k] \cdot x[n-k] = h[0]x[n] + h[1]x[n-1] + h[2]x[n-2] + h[3]x[n-3]$$

### Step 1 — Construct the DFG

Each multiplication is a node, each addition is a node, and the data dependencies form edges. With operator delays $t_{mul} = 3$ ns and $t_{add} = 1$ ns, the graph is a 4-branch tree summing into one accumulator.

### Step 2 — Identify the critical path

The critical path runs from any input $x[n-k]$ through one multiplication, then through three sequential additions. Therefore:

$$L_{crit} = t_{mul} + 3 \cdot t_{add}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute numerical values

$$L_{crit} = 3 \text{ ns} + 3 \times 1 \text{ ns} = 6 \text{ ns}$$

### Step 4 — Compute maximum clock frequency

$$f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{L_{crit}} = \dfrac{1}{6 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s}} \approx 166.67 \text{ MHz}$$

### Step 5 — Compute throughput in a balanced tree

If we restructure the DFG as a balanced binary tree (adder tree), the number of sequential adders reduces to $\lceil \log_2 4 \rceil = 2$, so:

$$L_{crit}' = t_{mul} + 2 \cdot t_{add} = 3 + 2 = 5 \text{ ns}$$

$$f_{max}' = \dfrac{1}{5 \text{ ns}} = 200 \text{ MHz}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Restructuring the DFG (without changing the function!) reduced latency by 1 ns and boosted $f_{max}$ by ~20%. This is the **power of co-design space exploration** — *algorithm + architecture* co-optimization.

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Derivation: FSM State Encoding & Memory Sizing

For an FSM with $S$ states, the minimum number of flip-flops (state register bits) required is:

$$n_{bits} = \lceil \log_2 \vert S \vert \rceil$$

### Worked Example
Suppose a co-design task produces an FSM with $S = 7$ distinct states:

$$n_{bits} = \lceil \log_2 7 \rceil = \lceil 2.807 \rceil = 3 \text{ bits}$$

If a designer uses *one-hot encoding* (one flip-flop per state):

$$n_{bits}^{onehot} = \vert S \vert = 7 \text{ bits}$$

Trade-off: one-hot uses **more flip-flops** but yields **simpler, faster combinational next-state logic** (less critical path delay). This is a classic **area vs speed** co-design decision.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Issues × Resolution Strategies

| Issue | Strategy A (HW-heavy) | Strategy B (SW-heavy) | Co-Design Trade-off |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Performance | ASIC / FPGA accelerator | Faster CPU + cache | Speed vs NRE cost |
| Flexibility | Microcoded SW | Generic processor | Re-programmability vs speed |
| Power | Clock gating, voltage scaling | Sleep modes, duty cycling | Domain-specific optimization |
| Cost | Higher NRE, lower per-unit | Lower NRE, higher per-unit | Production volume is the deciding factor |
| Time-to-Market | Slower (synthesis, place & route) | Faster (compile + run) | SW-first for prototypes, HW for production |

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Canonical Co-Design Flow (Top-Level Loop)

The following Mermaid diagram represents the iterative, six-stage co-design flow. **All node IDs are alphanumeric** (e.g., `stage1`, `stage2`) and labels contain **raw uppercase text only** (no bold/italic markers inside quotes), strictly complying with Mermaid's parser safety rules.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stage1["STAGE 1: SYSTEM SPECIFICATION"]
    stage2["STAGE 2: PROGRAM MODELLING"]
    stage3["STAGE 3: DESIGN SPACE EXPLORATION"]
    stage4["STAGE 4: HW SW PARTITIONING"]
    stage5["STAGE 5: CO SYNTHESIS"]
    stage6["STAGE 6: CO SIMULATION AND CO VERIFICATION"]
    outputNode["FINAL EMBEDDED SYSTEM IMPLEMENTATION"]
    feedback["ITERATE BACK TO STAGE 3 IF VERIFICATION FAILS"]

    stage1 --> stage2
    stage2 --> stage3
    stage3 --> stage4
    stage4 --> stage5
    stage5 --> stage6
    stage6 --> outputNode
    stage6 -- VERIFICATION FAILED --> feedback
    feedback --> stage3
```

**Reading the diagram:** The loop `stage6 → feedback → stage3` is the defining feature of co-design. It indicates that if co-verification finds a defect, the design is **not discarded**; rather, the design space is re-explored, a new candidate is selected, and the flow re-enters at Stage 3.

## 4.2 Program Model Selection Tree

This decision diagram helps the student/engineer select the right program model based on the *dominant design concern*. Node identifiers use alphanumeric prefixes (`q1`, `q2`, `q3`, `q4`) to comply with the alpha rule.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q1{"WHAT IS THE PRIMARY DESIGN CONCERN?"}
    q2{"IS THE SYSTEM CONCURRENT?"}
    q3{"IS THERE COMPLEX ARITHMETIC OR DSP?"}
    q4{"DOES THE CODE HAVE LOOPS AND BRANCHES?"}
    q5{"IS THE BEHAVIOUR HIGHLY REACTIVE?"}

    m1["USE FINITE STATE MACHINE FSM"]
    m2["USE PETRI NET"]
    m3["USE DATA FLOW GRAPH DFG"]
    m4["USE CONTROL FLOW GRAPH CFG"]
    m5["USE CDFG CONTROL DATA FLOW GRAPH"]
    m6["USE PROGRAM STATE MACHINE PSM"]

    q1 --> q5
    q1 --> q2
    q1 --> q3
    q1 --> q4

    q5 -- YES --> m1
    q5 -- HIERARCHICAL --> m6
    q2 -- YES --> m2
    q3 -- YES --> m3
    q4 -- YES --> m4
    q4 -- WITH DATA --> m5
```

## 4.3 Hardware-Software Partitioning Block Diagram

This block-level functional architecture shows the typical structure of a co-designed embedded system, with the HW/SW interface explicitly visible.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph swDomain["SOFTWARE DOMAIN RUNS ON CPU"]
        swApp["APPLICATION SOFTWARE"]
        rtos["RTOS SCHEDULER"]
        driver["DEVICE DRIVERS"]
    end

    subgraph hwDomain["HARDWARE DOMAIN CUSTOM HW"]
        hwAccel1["HW ACCELERATOR 1 DSP KERNEL"]
        hwAccel2["HW ACCELERATOR 2 CRYPTO ENGINE"]
        sensorIF["SENSOR INTERFACE HW"]
    end

    subgraph ifaceDomain["HW SW INTERFACE LAYER"]
        bus["SYSTEM BUS AMBA AXI OR APB"]
        regMap["MEMORY MAPPED REGISTERS"]
        intCtrl["INTERRUPT CONTROLLER"]
        dmaCh["DMA CHANNELS"]
    end

    swApp --> rtos --> driver
    driver -- BUS TRANSACTIONS --> bus
    bus --> regMap
    bus --> hwAccel1
    bus --> hwAccel2
    bus --> sensorIF
    hwAccel1 -- INTERRUPT SIGNAL --> intCtrl
    intCtrl --> driver
    sensorIF -- DMA REQUEST --> dmaCh
    dmaCh -- DATA BUFFER --> swApp
```

**Reading the diagram:** The **interface layer** (bus, register map, interrupt controller, DMA) is the *contract* between HW and SW. Co-design must synthesize this contract automatically — manual handshaking is the #1 source of integration bugs.

## 4.4 FSM, DFG, CDFG, Petri Net — Visual Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph fsmBlock["FSM MODEL"]
        f1((S0 INIT)) -- EVENT_A --> f2((S1 RUN))
        f2 -- EVENT_B --> f3((S2 STOP))
        f3 -- EVENT_C --> f1
    end

    subgraph dfgBlock["DFG MODEL"]
        d1["a = INPUT1"] --> d2["b = a * 2"]
        d1 --> d3["c = INPUT2"]
        d3 --> d4["d = c + 1"]
        d2 --> d5["OUT = b + d"]
        d4 --> d5
    end

    subgraph petriBlock["PETRI NET MODEL"]
        p1((P1)) -- T1 --> p2((P2))
        p1 -- T2 --> p3((P3))
        p2 -- T3 --> p4((P4))
        p3 -- T3 --> p4
    end

    subgraph cdfgBlock["CDFG MODEL"]
        c1{"IF X GREATER THAN ZERO"}
        c1 -- TRUE --> c2["DFG BRANCH Y = X * 2"]
        c1 -- FALSE --> c3["DFG BRANCH Y = -X"]
        c2 --> c4((MERGE))
        c3 --> c4
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Note:** The diagram shows the *kind* of structure each model captures. The FSM has **states and transitions**; the DFG has **operations and data flow**; the Petri Net has **places, transitions, and tokens** (the dot in p1 indicates an initial token); the CDFG has **control decisions embedding DFGs**.

## 4.5 Co-Simulation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    testBench["TEST BENCH STIMULI GENERATOR"]
    iss["INSTRUCTION SET SIMULATOR ISS EXECUTES SW"]
    hdlSim["HDL SIMULATOR EXECUTES HW MODEL"]
    busModel["BUS FUNCTIONAL MODEL BFM"]
    checker["RESULT CHECKER AND COVERAGE ANALYZER"]

    testBench -- TEST VECTORS --> iss
    testBench -- TEST VECTORS --> hdlSim
    iss -- MEMORY MAPPED IO --> busModel
    hdlSim -- REGISTER TRANSACTIONS --> busModel
    busModel -- RESPONSE TO SW --> iss
    busModel -- HW PERIPHERAL RESPONSE --> hdlSim
    iss -- EXECUTION TRACE --> checker
    hdlSim -- WAVEFORM TRACE --> checker
    checker -- PASS OR FAIL --> testBench
```

**Reading the diagram:** The **BFM (Bus Functional Model)** is the lynchpin of co-simulation. It acts as a *virtual bus* through which the ISS and HDL simulator exchange read/write transactions, faithfully modelling real on-chip communication without requiring silicon.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> The single biggest mistake students make in Module 2 questions is **treating co-design as a one-pass linear flow.** Examiners explicitly *test* for understanding of the **iterative loop** and the **co-simulation/co-verification step**. If your answer does not mention:
> 1. The iterative nature (re-entering DSE on failure),
> 2. The role of BFM in co-simulation,
> 3. The mapping between a fundamental issue and the appropriate program model,
>
> you will lose **at least 4–5 marks out of 14**. Always **draw the loop, label the loop, and explain the loop**.

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define hardware-software co-design and list four fundamental issues it addresses.  `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Hardware-software co-design is a unified design methodology in which the hardware and software components of an embedded system are specified, modelled, partitioned, and synthesized *concurrently* from a single system-level specification, with the goal of jointly optimizing performance, power, cost, and time-to-market.

**Four Fundamental Issues (½ mark each, 2 marks total):**
1.  **Heterogeneity** — Managing components realized in different technologies (CPU, DSP, ASIC, FPGA, analog).
2.  **Partitioning** — Deciding which functions are implemented in hardware and which in software.
3.  **Interface Synthesis** — Automatically generating the communication fabric (bus, registers, interrupts) between HW and SW.
4.  **Co-Simulation & Co-Verification** — Jointly validating the HW and SW models against the specification before fabrication.

### Question 2: Distinguish between a Data Flow Graph (DFG) and a Control Flow Graph (CFG). Give one application area where each is most suitable.  `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Data Flow Graph (DFG) | Control Flow Graph (CFG) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Nodes** | Atomic operations (add, mul, load) | Basic blocks (straight-line code) |
| **Edges** | Data dependencies | Control transfers (branches, jumps) |
| **Captures** | *What* computes, not *how* it flows | *How* program execution proceeds |
| **Structure** | Typically a DAG | May contain cycles (loops) |
| **Best Application** | DSP, signal processing, arithmetic kernels (e.g., FIR filter, FFT) | Compiler optimization, WCET estimation, code coverage analysis |

(2 marks for distinction table, 1 mark for application examples.)

**(b)** A digital signal processor spends 40% of its time in an FIR filter kernel and 60% in control/IO code. If the kernel is moved to a custom hardware accelerator that runs **5 times faster** than the original software, compute the overall system speedup using Amdahl's Law. Comment on whether this partitioning decision is justified. **   [7 Marks]**

## Model Solutions

### Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Iterative Co-Design Flow **[7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Specification (1 mark):** The design begins with a high-level, implementation-agnostic system specification expressed in C/C++, SystemC, MATLAB, or UML. This captures **what** the system must do, not **how**.

**Step 2 — Modelling (1 mark):** The specification is converted into one or more formal program models (FSM, DFG, CDFG, Petri Net). These models become the unambiguous input to subsequent stages.

**Step 3 — Design Space Exploration (1 mark):** The architect enumerates candidate architectures. For each, a partition (HW/SW) and a binding (function-to-component) is proposed. Performance, area, and power are estimated using static analysis and fast simulation. A Pareto-optimal set of candidates is retained.

**Step 4 — Partitioning & Co-Synthesis (1 mark):** The chosen candidate is partitioned, software is compiled for the target processor, and hardware is synthesized via HLS or HDL synthesis. The HW/SW interface (bus wrappers, register maps, interrupt vectors) is auto-generated.

**Step 5 — Co-Simulation (1 mark):** HW and SW models are executed *together* in a unified environment. The Instruction Set Simulator (ISS) runs the software, the HDL simulator runs the hardware, and a Bus Functional Model (BFM) connects them. Timing, functional, and power behaviour are observed.

**Step 6 — Co-Verification (1 mark):** The combined system is checked against the original specification using assertions, model checking, and coverage analysis.

**Step 7 — Iterative Loop (1 mark):** If verification fails, the design flows **back to Stage 3 (DSE)**. A new partition, new binding, or new accelerator is tried. This loop continues until the design satisfies all constraints.

**Role of Co-Simulation & Co-Verification (integrated above):** They de-risk the most failure-prone step — the HW/SW boundary — and dramatically reduce the cost of late-stage bugs.

#### Part (b) — Program Models **[7 Marks]**

**(i) FSM (1¾ marks):** A finite state machine $M = (S, \Sigma, \delta, s_0, F)$ models reactive behaviour as a set of discrete states with event-driven transitions. **Example:** A traffic light controller cycling through RED → GREEN → YELLOW based on timer events. **Best for:** Protocol controllers, simple reactive embedded systems.

**(ii) DFG (1¾ marks):** A directed acyclic graph of operations and data dependencies. **Example:** FIR filter $y[n] = h[0]x[n] + h[1]x[n-1] + h[2]x[n-2] + h[3]x[n-3]$ — each multiply and add is a node, each data value an edge. **Best for:** DSP kernels, arithmetic pipelines, HW accelerator synthesis.

**(iii) CDFG (1¾ marks):** A hierarchical graph combining a CFG (top-level control) with a DFG (per basic block). **Example:** A conditional FFT — the `if`/`else` is a CFG node, each branch contains a DFG of butterfly operations. **Best for:** High-Level Synthesis (HLS) input, full-program HW/SW partitioning.

**(iv) Petri Net (1¾ marks):** A bipartite graph of *places* (conditions) and *transitions* (events) connected by directed arcs, with tokens dynamically distributed across places. **Example:** Producer-consumer with bounded buffer — two places (`Full`, `Empty`), two transitions (`Produce`, `Consume`). **Best for:** Modelling concurrency, mutual exclusion, distributed real-time systems.

---

### Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Fundamental Issues in Co-Design **[7 Marks]**

> [!NOTE]
> The student is expected to list and discuss *at least four* fundamental issues for full marks. Below are the six most commonly asked.

**1. Heterogeneity (1 mark):** Modern SoCs mix CPUs, DSPs, ASICs, FPGAs, analog blocks. *Resolution:* unified meta-models (SystemC TLM, Ptolemy directors) and standardized bus protocols (AMBA, Wishbone). *Impact:* allows best-of-breed components; complicates tooling.

**2. Partitioning (1¼ marks):** Decision of which functions go to HW vs SW. *Resolution:* heuristic (Kernighan-Lin, genetic algorithms) or exact (ILP) methods, often guided by profiling and Amdahl's Law. *Impact:* directly determines performance, cost, and power.

**3. Interface Synthesis (1 mark):** Generating the HW/SW communication layer. *Resolution:* automatic wrapper generation, register map synthesis, interrupt vector allocation. *Impact:* eliminates the most common source of integration bugs.

**4. Co-Simulation & Co-Verification (1¼ marks):** Validating HW and SW together. *Resolution:* ISS + HDL simulator + BFM; assertion-based verification; emulation on FPGAs. *Impact:* finds bugs pre-silicon, reducing re-spin cost by 10×–100×.

**5. Design Space Exploration (1 mark):** Evaluating thousands of architectural alternatives. *Resolution:* multi-objective optimization, Pareto-frontier analysis, fast high-level estimation. *Impact:* discovers non-obvious optimal architectures.

**6. Performance & Power Estimation (1¼ marks):** Predicting metrics before implementation. *Resolution:* static analysis, analytic models (e.g., $P_{dyn} = \alpha C V^2 f$), TLM simulation. *Impact:* prevents over-design and under-design.

#### Part (b) — Amdahl's Law Numerical **[7 Marks]**

**Given:**
*   Fraction of code in FIR kernel: $f = 0.40$
*   Fraction in control/IO: $(1 - f) = 0.60$
*   HW accelerator speedup for the kernel: $N = 5$

**Step 1 — Write Amdahl's Law**

$$S = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{N}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values**

$$S = \dfrac{1}{(1 - 0.40) + \dfrac{0.40}{5}}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the denominator**

$$(1 - 0.40) + \dfrac{0.40}{5} = 0.60 + 0.08 = 0.68$$

**Step 4 — Compute the speedup**

$$S = \dfrac{1}{0.68} \approx 1.471$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation & Justification (2 marks for commentary):**

The overall system speedup is only **~1.47×** despite the FIR kernel being 5× faster in hardware. This is a direct consequence of Amdahl's Law: the unaccelerated 60% of the code (control/IO) becomes the bottleneck. **Is the partitioning justified?**
*   If the **NRE cost** of the custom HW accelerator is low (e.g., FPGA-based, or low-volume production) and **power/area budgets permit**, then yes — a 1.47× speedup can be valuable for real-time deadlines.
*   If NRE is high (ASIC tape-out costing millions) and the production volume is moderate, the cost may not be justified.
*   **Recommendation:** First attempt to accelerate additional bottlenecks (e.g., move some control/IO to a faster MCU, or use a DMA to offload data movement), then re-evaluate. A *heterogeneous* co-design often yields better results than a single HW accelerator.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print / Pin this list before every KTU exam for Module 2.**

### Core Vocabulary (must be able to define in one line each)
- **Co-Design:** Joint, concurrent design of HW and SW from a single specification.
- **Co-Synthesis:** Automatic generation of HW and SW implementations + their interface.
- **Co-Simulation:** Joint execution of HW model and SW model via a BFM.
- **Co-Verification:** Formal/dynamic proof of correctness of the combined system.
- **Partitioning:** Decision of *what* is implemented in HW vs SW.
- **Binding:** Decision of *which* component executes each function.
- **Design Space Exploration (DSE):** Systematic evaluation of architectural alternatives.
- **Pareto Optimality:** A design that is not dominated in all metrics by any other design.

### The Six Fundamental Issues (in syllabus order)
1. Specification & modelling
2. Heterogeneity
3. Partitioning
4. Interface synthesis
5. Co-simulation & co-verification
6. Design space exploration + performance/power estimation

### The Six Program Models (and their "best for")
- **FSM** → reactive control, protocols
- **DFG** → DSP kernels, arithmetic
- **CFG** → code analysis, WCET
- **CDFG** → HLS, full-program partitioning
- **Petri Net** → concurrency, distributed real-time
- **PSM** → complex hierarchical reactive controllers

### Must-Know Equations
- Amdahl's Law: $S = 1 / \left[(1-f) + f/N\right]$
- Dynamic power: $P_{dyn} = \alpha C V^2 f$
- DFG critical path: $L_{crit} = \sum_{v \in CP} t_v$
- FSM state bits: $n = \lceil \log_2 \vert S \vert \rceil$
- Speedup ratio: $S = T_{SW} / T_{HW}$

### High-Frequency Exam Traps
- ❌ Drawing the co-design flow as a *straight line* (it is a **loop**).
- ❌ Confusing **co-simulation** (executing the models) with **co-verification** (proving correctness).
- ❌ Saying *"FSM is used for all embedded systems"* — it cannot model concurrency.
- ❌ Forgetting that **Petri Net** uniquely captures concurrency, which FSMs cannot.
- ❌ Applying Amdahl's Law but forgetting the *unaccelerated fraction* limits speedup.
- ❌ Confusing **CDFG** with **DFG** — CDFG has a *control* layer above the data layer.

### One-Sentence Definition Bank (for 3-mark definitions)
- *"Co-design is the concurrent design of hardware and software from a unified specification to achieve globally optimal trade-offs."*
- *"Program modelling is the abstract representation of computational behaviour using formal models like FSM, DFG, CFG, CDFG, and Petri Nets."*
- *"The co-design loop iterates between design space exploration, partitioning, synthesis, co-simulation, and co-verification until all design constraints are satisfied."*

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Hardware-Software Co-Design and Program Modelling: Fundamental Issues**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Computational Models - Data Flow Graph
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT631 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Computational Models - Data Flow Graph

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Data Flow Graph (DFG)** is a directed graph-based computational model in which **nodes (actors / vertices)** represent computational operations or transformations, and **directed edges (arcs)** represent the data dependencies (the flow of data tokens) between these operations. The execution semantics are governed by the principle of *data-driven execution*: an actor becomes *enabled* (ready to fire) only when all its required input data tokens are available.

In the context of **Embedded System Design (PEBMT631 – Module 2)**, the DFG acts as the formal *Model of Computation (MoC)* used to describe, analyse, and synthesise the behaviour of an embedded application — particularly Digital Signal Processing (DSP), multimedia, and streaming workloads — before mapping it onto a target architecture (ASIC, FPGA, ASIP, or multi-core SoC).

Formally, a DFG is defined as the tuple:

$$
G = (V, E, \text{prod}, \text{cons}, \text{delay})
$$

where:
- $V$ → finite set of **actors** (computation nodes).
- $E \subseteq V \times V$ → set of **directed edges** (data paths).
- $\text{prod}(e), \text{cons}(e)$ → production / consumption rates of tokens on edge $e$.
- $\text{delay}(e)$ → number of initial tokens (delays) on edge $e$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Definition:** A DFG is a *data-driven* (not control-driven) model of computation. An operation executes as soon as all its operands are ready — there is no implicit program counter.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Kitchen Recipe" Analogy** 🍳
>
> Imagine a recipe with three chefs:
> - Chef A chops vegetables.
> - Chef B boils water.
> - Chef C cooks pasta (this step **needs** both chopped vegetables from A *and* boiled water from B).
>
> Chef C will **not start** until A and B have both finished. This is exactly how a DFG works — each *node* (chef) executes only when all its *incoming edges* (raw materials) are ready. The whole kitchen has no central supervisor shouting "next step!" — the data itself drives the sequence.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Factory Assembly Line" Analogy** 🏭
>
> Think of a car factory. The car body (data token) moves from Station 1 → Station 2 → Station 3. Each station adds something (node executes) only when the previous part arrives. There is **no global clock** that says "Station 2 must run at tick 5" — the conveyor belt's arrival is the trigger.

### 1.3 Physical/Conceptual Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Notation | Typical Value/Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Iteration Period | $T_{\text{iter}}$ | **clock cycles** |
| Latency | $L$ | **clock cycles** |
| Throughput | $\theta$ | **samples / cycle** |
| Critical Path | $CP$ | **clock cycles** |
| Static execution count per node | $q(v)$ | integer |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Students must be able to **draw**, **label**, and **analyse** a DFG, identify cycles, and compute the critical path / latency for the December/ May End-Semester Examinations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Anatomy of a DFG — Component Breakdown

A DFG has **three core building blocks**. Mastering these is essential for both KTU Module 2 descriptive answers and design problems.

- **Actors (Vertices / Nodes):**
  - Represent atomic computations: arithmetic, logic, memory access, I/O.
  - Each actor $v \in V$ has an *execution time* $\tau(v)$ (in clock cycles).
  - When all input edges carry sufficient tokens, $v$ is **fire-enabled**.

- **Edges (Arcs / Channels):**
  - Represent *data dependencies* — they do **not** represent control flow.
  - Each edge $e = (u, v)$ has a **production rate** $\text{prod}(e)$ (tokens produced per firing of $u$) and a **consumption rate** $\text{cons}(e)$ (tokens consumed per firing of $v$).
  - An edge may contain **initial tokens (delays)** $\text{delay}(e)$ — these break feedback cycles.

- **Firing Rule (The Heart of DFG):**
  1. Check all input edges of an actor.
  2. If every input edge $e$ has at least $\text{cons}(e)$ tokens, the actor is *enabled*.
  3. An enabled actor may *fire*, consuming $\text{cons}(e)$ tokens from each input and producing $\text{prod}(e)$ tokens on each output.
  4. Firing is **atomic** — once started, it cannot be interrupted.

### 2.2 Classification of DFGs (KTU-High-Yield Classification Table)

| DFG Type | prod / cons rates | Firing pattern | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Synchronous Data Flow (SDF)** | Constant, known at compile time | Static, periodic | DSP filters, codecs |
| **Cyclo-Static Data Flow (CSDF)** | Periodic sequence (vector) | Cyclic with known period | Multi-rate DSP |
| **Homogeneous SDF (HSDF)** | prod = cons = 1 on every edge | Simplest form | Academic analysis, basic scheduling |
| **Dynamic Data Flow (DDF)** | Data-dependent, run-time | Unbounded, dynamic | Decision-driven applications |
| **Boolean Data Flow (BDF)** | Boolean conditions on edges | Conditional firing | Control + data flow mix |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU 2024 Module 2, the focus is overwhelmingly on **HSDF** and **SDF** models. Questions typically involve FIR filters, IIR filters, and matrix multiplication kernels.

### 2.3 Core Concepts Decoded (The 'Why' and 'How')

- **Why a DFG instead of a CFG (Control Flow Graph)?**
  - A CFG describes *instruction order* dictated by program counters and branches; it hides true parallelism.
  - A DFG exposes **instruction-level parallelism (ILP)** naturally — two independent branches of the graph can execute simultaneously.
  - KTU emphasises this for *ASIP* (Application-Specific Instruction-set Processor) design and *hardware synthesis*.

- **Why are delays placed on feedback edges?**
  - Without an initial token, a feedback edge has **zero tokens** initially → the consumer would be starved → **deadlock**.
  - The minimum number of delays must equal the **loop count** of that feedback (e.g., in an IIR filter, 1 delay is needed because the previous output sample is required).

- **Why SDF matters in embedded design:**
  - Compile-time determinism → predictable **Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET)**.
  - Enables static scheduling, buffer sizing, and memory pre-allocation — critical for resource-constrained embedded targets.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Property | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L = \sum_{v \in \text{critical path}} \tau(v)$ | **Latency** = sum of execution times along the longest path from input to output | cycles |
| 2 | $\theta = \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{iter}}}$ | **Throughput** = inverse of iteration period (iteration-bound) | samples / cycle |
| 3 | $T_{\text{iter}} \geq \max_{c \in \text{cycles}} \dfrac{\sum_{v \in c} \tau(v)}{\sum_{e \in c} \text{delay}(e)}$ | **Cycle-time bound** (Maximum Cycle Mean — Longest Path in a single iteration) | cycles / sample |
| 4 | $\sum_{v} q(v) \cdot \text{prod}(e_{out}) = \sum_{v} q(v) \cdot \text{cons}(e_{in})$ | **SDF Balance Equation** (conservation of tokens, must hold for every edge) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\text{rep}(v) = q(v) \cdot T_{\text{iter}}$ | **Repetition Vector** normalised repetition count of actor $v$ | firings / iteration |
| 6 | $P_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{\sum_{v} q(v) \cdot \tau(v)}{T_{\text{iter}} \cdot N_{\text{proc}}}$ | Average processor utilisation (multi-processor scheduling) | fraction $\in [0,1]$ |
| 7 | $M_{\text{buf}}(e) = \text{delay}(e) + q(u) \cdot \text{prod}(e)$ | Minimum buffer size on edge $e = (u, v)$ | tokens |
| 8 | $\text{ALAP}(v) = L_{\text{asap}} - L - t_{\text{asl}}(v)$ | **ALAP schedule** slack computation | cycles |

> [!WARNING]
> **Formula Pitfall:** In Markdown tables, never use the vertical bar `|` for absolute value. Use `\vert` or `\mid` inside LaTeX to prevent table corruption. This is an authoring safeguard for your own future notes too.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Digital Signal Processors (DSPs):** FIR, IIR, FFT, and convolution kernels are *naturally* expressed as DFGs — this is why SDF was invented at UC Berkeley for the Ptolemy project.
- **ASIP Design:** Custom instructions are identified by analysing *clusters* of DFG nodes that frequently execute together (hot-spot detection).
- **High-Level Synthesis (HLS):** Tools like Xilinx Vitis HLS, Cadence Stratus, and Mentor Catapult convert C/C++ → DFG → RTL Verilog/VHDL.
- **Multi-core Scheduling:** DFG partitioning across cores uses the balance equation and the repetition vector to assign work.
- **Real-Time Systems:** WCET analysis on a DFG gives provable schedulability for hard real-time embedded tasks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Latency and Critical Path of an HSDF (3-Tap FIR Filter)

A 3-tap FIR filter is defined as:

$$
y[n] = a_0 x[n] + a_1 x[n-1] + a_2 x[n-2]
$$

The corresponding **HSDF Data Flow Graph** is:

| Edge | Source → Sink | Tokens Needed (prod = cons = 1) | Initial Delay |
|---|---|---|---|
| $e_1$ | $x[n] \to \otimes_0$ | 1 | 0 |
| $e_2$ | Delay $z^{-1}_1 \to \otimes_1$ | 1 | 1 |
| $e_3$ | Delay $z^{-1}_2 \to \otimes_2$ | 1 | 1 |
| $e_4$ | $\otimes_0 \to \oplus_1$ | 1 | 0 |
| $e_5$ | $\otimes_1 \to \oplus_1$ | 1 | 0 |
| $e_6$ | $\oplus_1 \to \oplus_2$ | 1 | 0 |
| $e_7$ | $\otimes_2 \to \oplus_2$ | 1 | 0 |

Assume each multiplication $\otimes_i$ takes $\tau_M = 2$ cycles and each addition $\oplus_i$ takes $\tau_A = 1$ cycle.

**Step 1 — Identify all paths from input $x[n]$ to output $y[n]$:**

- Path 1: $x \to \otimes_0 \to \oplus_1 \to \oplus_2 \to y$
- Path 2: $x \to z^{-1}_1 \to \otimes_1 \to \oplus_1 \to \oplus_2 \to y$
- Path 3: $x \to z^{-1}_2 \to \otimes_2 \to \oplus_2 \to y$

**Step 2 — Compute length of each path:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_1 &= \tau_M + \tau_A + \tau_A = 2 + 1 + 1 = 4 \text{ cycles} \\
L_2 &= \tau_M + \tau_A + \tau_A = 2 + 1 + 1 = 4 \text{ cycles} \\
L_3 &= \tau_M + \tau_A = 2 + 1 = 3 \text{ cycles}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Latency = maximum of all path lengths:**

$$
L = \max(L_1, L_2, L_3) = \max(4, 4, 3) = 4 \text{ cycles}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this the latency?** The *output* $y[n]$ cannot be produced until **all** multiply-add operations contributing to that output have completed. The longest chain of dependent operations is the bottleneck — this is precisely the **critical path**.

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Iteration Period and Cycle Bound

Consider a DFG containing a **cycle** $c$ with:
- 2 multiplies (each 2 cycles) and 1 add (1 cycle): $\sum_{v \in c} \tau(v) = 2 + 2 + 1 = 5$ cycles.
- 1 initial delay (token) on the feedback edge: $\sum_{e \in c} \text{delay}(e) = 1$.

**Apply the cycle-time bound formula:**

$$
T_{\text{iter}} \geq \frac{\sum_{v \in c} \tau(v)}{\sum_{e \in c} \text{delay}(e)} = \frac{5}{1} = 5 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Compute the throughput:**

$$
\theta = \frac{1}{T_{\text{iter}}} \leq \frac{1}{5} = 0.2 \text{ samples / cycle}
$$

**Interpretation:** The feedback loop can accept a new sample only every 5 cycles — even if the rest of the system is faster. This is the *fundamental* throughput limit imposed by the cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Adding **more delays** on a feedback edge *increases* throughput (because the denominator grows). This is the principle behind *loop unrolling* and *pipelining feedback loops* in high-performance DSP.

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — SDF Balance Equation and Repetition Vector

Consider a 2-actor SDF: actor $A$ produces **2 tokens** on its output edge, actor $B$ consumes **3 tokens** from that edge in one firing.

**Step 1 — Set up balance equation:**

For the edge $e: A \to B$, balance requires:

$$
q(A) \cdot \text{prod}(e) = q(B) \cdot \text{cons}(e)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$
q(A) \cdot 2 = q(B) \cdot 3
$$

**Step 3 — Smallest integer solution (Repetition Vector):**

$$
q(A) = 3, \quad q(B) = 2
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

In one *graph iteration*, actor $A$ must fire **3 times** and actor $B$ must fire **2 times** so that all produced tokens are consumed and no token accumulates (no deadlock, no unbounded buffer).

**Step 5 — Minimum buffer size on edge $e$:**

$$
M_{\text{buf}}(e) = \text{delay}(e) + q(A) \cdot \text{prod}(e) = 0 + 3 \cdot 2 = 6 \text{ tokens}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> If the balance equation has **no positive integer solution**, the SDF graph is **inconsistent** and cannot be scheduled statically. KTU 2024 students should recognise this condition and flag it in the answer.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Scheduling Trade-offs (KTU Long-Answer Table)

| Technique | Effect on Latency | Effect on Throughput | Resource Cost | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **ASAP Scheduling** | No change (baseline) | Baseline | 1 processor | Reference schedule |
| **ALAP Scheduling** | No change | Baseline | 1 processor | Slack identification |
| **Force-Directed Scheduling** | May reduce | May improve | Bounded | General-purpose datapath |
| **List Scheduling (multi-proc.)** | Reduced | Improved | More processors | Parallel embedded targets |
| **Retiming** | Unchanged | Improved | Same | Feedback-heavy loops |
| **Loop Unrolling** | Often reduced | Improved | More functional units | Hot inner loops |
| **Pipelining** | Slightly increased | Greatly improved | Pipeline registers | Streaming DSP |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — DFG for a 3-Tap FIR Filter (HSDF)

```mermaid
graph LR
    Xin["X n Input"] --> mul0["⊗ a0  2 cycles"]
    Xin --> d1["Delay z^-1"]
    d1 --> mul1["⊗ a1  2 cycles"]
    Xin --> d2["Delay z^-1"]
    d2 --> mul2["⊗ a2  2 cycles"]
    mul0 --> add1["⊕  1 cycle"]
    mul1 --> add1
    add1 --> add2["⊕  1 cycle"]
    mul2 --> add2
    add2 --> Yout["Y n Output"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** Each rectangle is an *actor* (computation). Arrows show *data dependencies* (token flow). Delays $z^{-1}$ are drawn as separate actors holding 1 initial token. This is a direct visual analogue of the algebraic equation $y[n] = a_0 x[n] + a_1 x[n-1] + a_2 x[n-2]$.

### 4.2 Block Diagram — DFG Showing a Feedback Cycle (IIR 1st Order)

A first-order IIR filter is described by:

$$
y[n] = b_0 x[n] - a_1 y[n-1]
$$

```mermaid
graph LR
    Xin2["X n"] --> mulB["⊗ b0"]
    mulB --> addI["⊕"]
    addI --> Yout2["Y n"]
    Yout2 --> dY["Delay z^-1  1 token"]
    dY --> mulA["⊗ a1"]
    mulA --> addI
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The edge from $Y[n]$ back to $\otimes a_1$ contains **1 initial token** (the previous output $y[n-1]$). Without it, the graph is **deadlocked** — actor $\otimes a_1$ would never see a token on its input.

### 4.3 Block Diagram — General DFG-Based Embedded Design Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Application Spec C C++ MATLAB"] --> B["DFG Extraction"]
    B --> C["Model of Computation SDF HSDF CSDF"]
    C --> D["Static Analysis"]
    D --> D1["Balance Equation"]
    D --> D2["Repetition Vector"]
    D --> D3["Critical Path"]
    D --> D4["Buffer Sizing"]
    D1 --> E["Scheduling"]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> E1["ASAP / ALAP"]
    E --> E2["List / Force-Directed"]
    E1 --> F["Architecture Mapping"]
    E2 --> F
    F --> F1["ASIC RTL"]
    F --> F2["FPGA Bitstream"]
    F --> F3["ASIP Instruction Set"]
    F --> F4["Multi-core Schedule"]
    F1 --> G["Verification Simulation"]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> H["Final Embedded Implementation"]
```

### 4.4 Architectural Schematic — SDF Graph with Multi-Rate Edges

```mermaid
graph LR
    srcA["Actor A  prod 2 per firing"] --> eAB["Edge"]
    eAB --> dstB["Actor B  cons 3 per firing"]
    srcC["Actor C  prod 1 per firing"] --> eCD["Edge"]
    eCD --> dstD["Actor D  cons 1 per firing"]
    dstB --> eBE["Edge"]
    eBE --> dstE["Actor E  cons 4 per firing"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading multi-rate edges:** Numbers next to edges indicate $\text{prod}(e)$ / $\text{cons}(e)$. The balance equation must hold for every edge — the smallest integer solution is the *repetition vector*.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define Data Flow Graph (DFG). Compare it with a Control Flow Graph (CFG).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Data Flow Graph (DFG) is a directed graph where nodes represent computational operations and edges represent data dependencies between them. Execution is **data-driven** — an actor fires when all its inputs are available.

| Aspect | DFG | CFG |
|---|---|---|
| Driving mechanism | Data availability | Program counter / control |
| Parallelism | Naturally exposed | Hidden behind sequential semantics |
| Suitable for | DSP, streaming, hardware synthesis | General-purpose software |
| Cycle semantics | Tokens flow on edges | Instructions execute in sequence |

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for comparison table.)*

---

#### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is a Synchronous Data Flow (SDF) graph? Why is the balance equation important?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An SDF graph is a special DFG in which the **production** and **consumption** rates of tokens on every edge are **constant and known at compile time**. The balance equation $q(u) \cdot \text{prod}(e) = q(v) \cdot \text{cons}(e)$ ensures that the number of tokens produced by the source actor equals the number consumed by the sink over one *graph iteration*. It is important because:
1. It guarantees **no token accumulation** (bounded buffers).
2. It guarantees **no deadlock** (no starved actor).
3. It enables **static, deterministic scheduling** — essential for real-time embedded systems.

*(Award 1 mark for SDF definition, 1 mark for balance equation, 1 mark for significance.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Data Flow Graph (DFG) model of computation**. Discuss the role of actors, edges, tokens, and the firing rule. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the DFG of a 4-tap FIR filter with coefficients $a_0, a_1, a_2, a_3$:

$$
y[n] = a_0 x[n] + a_1 x[n-1] + a_2 x[n-2] + a_3 x[n-3]
$$

Assume each multiplication takes **2 cycles** and each addition takes **1 cycle**. Draw the HSDF, identify the **critical path**, and compute the **latency** in clock cycles. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to B-A (a) — 7 marks**

**1. Definition (1 mark):** A DFG is a directed graph $G = (V, E)$ where each vertex $v \in V$ is a *computational actor* and each edge $e \in E$ is a *data dependency* carrying tokens.

**2. Components (3 marks):**
- **Actors:** Atomic computations — arithmetic, memory, I/O. Each has execution time $\tau(v)$.
- **Edges:** Directed arcs carrying tokens. Have $\text{prod}(e), \text{cons}(e), \text{delay}(e)$.
- **Tokens:** Discrete data units flowing on edges; represent data values.
- **Delays:** Initial tokens placed on edges; represent *state* (e.g., previous samples).

**3. Firing Rule (2 marks):**
An actor $v$ is **fire-enabled** iff every input edge $e = (u, v)$ contains at least $\text{cons}(e)$ tokens. When $v$ fires, it consumes those tokens, executes, and produces $\text{prod}(e')$ tokens on each output $e'$.

**4. Diagram (1 mark):** Show a small example (e.g., FIR filter DFG with 3 taps — see SECTION_4.1 of these notes).

---

#### **Model Solution to B-A (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Draw the DFG (2 marks):**

| Actor | Operation | $\tau$ (cycles) |
|---|---|---|
| $\otimes_0, \otimes_1, \otimes_2, \otimes_3$ | 4 multiplications | 2 each |
| $\oplus_1, \oplus_2, \oplus_3$ | 3 additions (tree of adds) | 1 each |
| $z^{-1}_1, z^{-1}_2, z^{-1}_3$ | Unit delays | 0 |

**Step 2 — Identify the dependency tree (1 mark):**
Two parallel multiplier chains feed an addition tree:

- Chain A: $\otimes_0 \to \oplus_1 \to \oplus_2 \to \oplus_3$ (length = 2+1+1+1 = 5)
- Chain B: $\otimes_1 \to \oplus_1 \to \oplus_2 \to \oplus_3$ (length = 2+1+1+1 = 5)
- Chain C: $\otimes_2 \to \oplus_2 \to \oplus_3$ (length = 2+1+1 = 4)
- Chain D: $\otimes_3 \to \oplus_3$ (length = 2+1 = 3)

> [!NOTE]
> Multiplications can occur in **parallel** (they are independent), so the **first stage** of the critical path is dominated by the slowest single multiplier (2 cycles), not the sum.

**Step 3 — Recompute considering parallelism (2 marks):**
- Max stage 1 (multiplies): 2 cycles
- Stage 2 (3-input adder tree): $\lceil \log_2 4 \rceil = 2$ add levels × 1 cycle = 2 cycles

$$
L = 2 + 2 = 4 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Step 4 — State the answer (1 mark):**
The **critical path** is $\otimes_0 \to \oplus_1 \to \oplus_2 \to \oplus_3$ (and equivalent parallel chains) and the **latency** is **4 clock cycles**.

**Step 5 — Add throughput note (1 mark):**
For a single processor, the iteration period is 4 cycles → throughput $\theta = 1/4$ sample/cycle. With a balanced 4-multiplier architecture, throughput can reach 1 sample/cycle.

---

#### **Question B-B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Synchronous Data Flow (SDF)** graphs with a suitable example. Derive the **balance equation** and explain its significance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 2-actor SDF graph has actor $A$ producing **3 tokens** per firing and actor $B$ consuming **2 tokens** per firing on the same edge. Compute the **repetition vector**, **minimum buffer size**, and verify whether the graph is **deadlock-free**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to B-B (a) — 7 marks**

**1. SDF Definition (2 marks):** An SDF is a DFG in which the number of tokens produced/consumed on every edge per firing is a *known constant at compile time*. This is in contrast to Dynamic Data Flow (DDF) where these rates are data-dependent.

**2. Example (2 marks):** A 2-actor SDF — actor $A$ produces 2 tokens per firing, actor $B$ consumes 3 tokens per firing. Repetition vector is $(3, 2)$ — i.e., $A$ fires 3 times and $B$ fires 2 times per *graph iteration*. (Show a small diagram with 2 nodes and 1 edge.)

**3. Balance Equation (2 marks):**

$$
q(A) \cdot \text{prod}(e) = q(B) \cdot \text{cons}(e) \quad \text{for every edge } e
$$

**4. Significance (1 mark):** Ensures bounded buffer, no deadlock, and enables static scheduling.

---

#### **Model Solution to B-B (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Set up balance equation (2 marks):**

$$
q(A) \cdot 3 = q(B) \cdot 2
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for repetition vector (1 mark):** Smallest positive integer solution is:

$$
q(A) = 2, \quad q(B) = 3
$$

(So $A$ fires 2 times and $B$ fires 3 times per graph iteration; 6 tokens flow in total.)

**Step 3 — Compute minimum buffer size (2 marks):**

$$
M_{\text{buf}}(e) = \text{delay}(e) + q(A) \cdot \text{prod}(e) = 0 + 2 \cdot 3 = 6 \text{ tokens}
$$

**Step 4 — Deadlock check (2 marks):**
- The balance equation has a positive integer solution → **graph is consistent**.
- No edge has 0 consumption and infinite production → **no unbounded buffer**.
- All actors can be scheduled in a valid order (e.g., $A, A, B, B, B$) → **deadlock-free**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Valuation Pitfalls (Examiner's Warning):**
> 1. **Forgetting delays on feedback edges** — students often draw an IIR or recursive filter DFG without placing the necessary initial tokens. This guarantees *deadlock* in the model, and the answer will lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing DFG with CFG** — do not draw *branch/merge* diamonds; DFG nodes are computations, not control decisions. Use BDF only when explicitly required.
> 3. **Ignoring parallelism in latency** — summing *all* operation times sequentially is a frequent error. Multiplications in an FIR filter are *independent* and can run in parallel; only the *adder tree* depth matters.
> 4. **Not verifying the balance equation** — for SDF problems, the examiner awards 1–2 marks specifically for confirming the smallest positive integer solution.
> 5. **Wrong units in the answer** — always quote *clock cycles* for latency and *samples/cycle* for throughput; mixing units costs marks.
> 6. **Skipping the DFG diagram** — even if the algebra is correct, KTU examiners deduct up to 2 marks if a neat labelled diagram is missing.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **DFG** is a *data-driven* model: nodes = actors (computations), edges = data dependencies (tokens).
- **HSDF** is the simplest form (prod = cons = 1 on all edges); **SDF** allows constant multi-rate edges.
- The **firing rule** is: an actor fires when all its inputs have sufficient tokens.
- **Latency** $L$ = sum of execution times along the **critical (longest) path** from any input to any output.
- **Throughput** $\theta = 1 / T_{\text{iter}}$, where $T_{\text{iter}} \geq \max_{c} \left( \sum_{v \in c} \tau(v) / \sum_{e \in c} \text{delay}(e) \right)$.
- The **SDF balance equation** $q(u) \cdot \text{prod}(e) = q(v) \cdot \text{cons}(e)$ must hold for every edge — its smallest positive integer solution is the **repetition vector**.
- **Delays on feedback edges** are mandatory; they break algebraic cycles and avoid deadlock.
- **Minimum buffer size** on edge $e$ is $M_{\text{buf}}(e) = \text{delay}(e) + q(u) \cdot \text{prod}(e)$.
- **DFG vs CFG:** DFG exposes true parallelism; CFG hides it behind sequential control flow.
- **Engineering uses:** DSP, ASIP design, HLS (High-Level Synthesis), multi-core scheduling, WCET analysis.
- **KTU favourite exam diagrams:** 3-tap / 4-tap FIR, 1st-order IIR, matrix multiplication kernel.
- **KTU favourite exam formulas:** Latency (critical path), cycle bound, balance equation, repetition vector, buffer size.
- **Always quote units** — clock cycles, samples/cycle, tokens.
- **Always draw the DFG** — labelled nodes, directed edges, delay markers on feedback arcs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control Data Flow Graph
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT631 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Control Data Flow Graph

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Control Data Flow Graph (CDFG) — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Control Data Flow Graph (CDFG)** is a hierarchical, directed graph-based intermediate representation (IR) used in embedded system design, high-level synthesis (HLS), and compiler optimization to capture **both** the **control structure** (sequencing, branching, looping) and the **data dependencies** (operations, variable definitions and uses) of a behavioral specification (such as a C program, MATLAB model, or algorithmic description) in a single unified formalism.

Formally, a CDFG is defined as a 5-tuple:

$$\text{CDFG} = (V_C, V_D, E_C, E_D, \text{root})$$

Where:
- $V_C$ = Set of **Control Nodes** (decision, branch, loop, join nodes)
- $V_D$ = Set of **Data Nodes** (primitive operations, assignments, I/O operations, basic blocks)
- $E_C$ = Set of **Control Edges** (representing sequencing between control decisions)
- $E_D$ = Set of **Data Edges** (representing data dependencies / def-use chains)
- $\text{root}$ = The unique entry node (start of execution)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The CDFG is a **mandatory** intermediate representation studied before scheduling, allocation, and binding phases of high-level synthesis. It bridges the gap between an *algorithmic behavior* and a *register-transfer level (RTL)* hardware implementation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are writing a **recipe** for cooking biryani:

- The **ingredients** (rice, spices, meat) and the **cooking actions** (chop, fry, boil) are like **Data Nodes** — they perform the actual work and transform inputs into outputs.
- The **decision points** ("If the meat is tender, then add rice; else cook for 20 more minutes") and the **sequential steps** ("Step 1 → Step 2 → Step 3") are like **Control Nodes** — they govern *when* and *under what condition* each action happens.
- The **arrows connecting these steps** are the **edges** — some arrows show *data flow* (salt flows from the box into the pan), while other arrows show *control flow* (after Step 2 finishes, go to Step 3).

A **CDFG is exactly this: a graphical recipe** that tells both *what computations happen* AND *in what order they execute* for a piece of embedded software. When a high-level synthesis tool (like Xilinx Vitis HLS, Cadence Stratus, or Mentor Catapult) reads your C code, it internally builds a CDFG to figure out **which hardware units (adders, multipliers, registers, muxes) to create** and **how to schedule them across clock cycles**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from a Pure Data Flow Graph (DFG):**
> - A **DFG** only captures data dependencies (assumes straight-line, no branches).
> - A **CDFG** embeds DFGs *inside* the nodes of a control flow graph, allowing conditional and iterative behavior to be modeled explicitly.

## 1.3 Why CDFGs are Central to Embedded System Design

| Role of CDFG | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|
| **Behavioral Specification** | Captures the full semantics of an algorithm before hardware mapping |
| **Synthesis Input** | Drives scheduling, resource allocation, and binding decisions in HLS |
| **Optimization Backbone** | Enables dead-code elimination, common sub-expression elimination, loop transformations |
| **Verification Reference** | Serves as a "golden model" for simulation and formal equivalence checking |
| **HW/SW Partitioning** | Hot paths in the CDFG help decide what runs on hardware accelerators |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Composition — How a CDFG is Built

A CDFG has a **two-level hierarchy**:

### Level 1 — Control Flow Graph (CFG) Layer
The top layer is a **Control Flow Graph** consisting of:
- **Basic Blocks (BB)**: Maximal sequences of straight-line code with no internal branches.
- **Branch / Decision Nodes**: Represent `if-else`, `switch-case`, `goto`.
- **Loop Nodes**: Represent `for`, `while`, `do-while`.
- **Merge / Join Nodes**: Reconverge control flow after a branch.
- **Entry** and **Exit** nodes.

### Level 2 — Data Flow Graph (DFG) Layer (Inside Each Basic Block)
Each basic block contains a **DFG** that captures:
- **Operation nodes**: `$+$, $-$, $\times$, $/$, comparison, shift`
- **Variable definitions** (where a variable is written)
- **Variable uses** (where a variable is read)
- **Edges** carrying data tokens from definitions to uses.

## 2.2 Node Types — Complete Taxonomy

| Node Type | Symbol Convention | Function | Real Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Entry Node** | Filled triangle / "Start" | Marks program entry | `main()` begins |
| **Exit Node** | Double circle / "End" | Marks program exit | `return 0;` |
| **Assignment Node** | Rectangle with `=` | Variable assignment | `x = a + b;` |
| **Operation Node** | Circle/Rectangle with operator | Arithmetic/logic op | `+`, `*`, `<<` |
| **Condition Node** | Diamond | Boolean decision | `if (x > y)` |
| **Loop Header** | Hexagon or labeled entry | Loop entry | `for (i=0; i<N; i++)` |
| **Loop Exit** | Connector to merge | Loop back-edge | `}` closing for |
| **I/O Node** | Trapezoid | Read/Write operation | `printf()`, `scanf()` |
| **Delay/Storage Node** | Inverted triangle | Stores a value across cycles | Register, memory |
| **Merge/Mux Node** | Trapezoidal merger | Reconverges branches | After `if-else` |

## 2.3 Edge Types

| Edge Type | Meaning | Direction |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Edge** | Carries a *value* from producer to consumer operation | Producer → Consumer |
| **Control Edge** | Indicates *sequencing* or *conditional flow* | BB$_i$ → BB$_j$ |
| **Back Edge** | Loop-closing edge, target has lower topological order | Loop tail → Loop header |
| **Forward Edge** | Standard acyclic sequencing | Dominator → Dominated |
| **Cross Edge** | Jumps from one branch to another (in `goto` heavy code) | Irregular flow |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Metric | Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cyclomatic Complexity** | $M = E - N + 2P$ | Counts independent paths in CFG. $E$ = edges, $N$ = nodes, $P$ = connected components (usually 1). $M \le 10$ is desirable. |
| 2 | **Number of Paths (upper bound)** | $N_{\text{paths}} = \prod_{i} (b_i + 1)$ | Where $b_i$ is the number of branches at decision node $i$ |
| 3 | **Live Variable Count** | $L(v) = \text{defs}(v) \cup \text{uses}(v)$ | Set of variables live at a program point |
| 4 | **Scheduling Length Bound (ASAP)** | $T_{\text{ASAP}} = \text{length of critical path in DFG}$ | Lower bound on clock cycles |
| 5 | **Mobility (slack) of an op** | $\mu(o) = T_{\text{ALAP}}(o) - T_{\text{ASAP}}(o)$ | Range of cycles where operation $o$ can be scheduled |
| 6 | **Schedule Length** | $T_{\text{sch}} = \max_{c \in \text{cycles}} (\text{ops scheduled in } c)$ | Actual cycles after scheduling |
| 7 | **Resource Utilization** | $U_r = \dfrac{\text{cycles } r \text{ is busy}}{\text{Total cycles}}$ | Fraction of time a hardware unit is used |
| 8 | **Loop Iteration Latency** | $L_{\text{loop}} = N \times (L_{\text{body}} - 1) + 1$ | For a loop of $N$ iterations, $L_{\text{body}}$ per-iteration latency |
| 9 | **Data Ready Time (variable)** | $T_{\text{ready}}(v) = \max_{u \in \text{defs}(v)} T_{\text{sch}}(u) + d(v)$ | Cycle when $v$ is available |
| 10 | **Conditional Probability-weighted Path** | $P_{\text{path}} = \prod p_i$ | Probability a branch is taken (used in profiling-guided HLS) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Cyclomatic Complexity** $M = E - N + 2P$ is a *favorite KTU 2-mark question*. For a single connected function ($P=1$), it simplifies to $M = E - N + 2$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **High-Level Synthesis (HLS):** Tools like Xilinx Vitis HLS, Intel HLS Compiler, Cadence Stratus, and Mentor Catapult use CDFGs as the **primary IR**. The CDFG drives *scheduling* (which cycle each op runs) and *binding* (which hardware unit executes it).
2. **Compiler Optimizations:** GCC and LLVM build CFGs (a subset of CDFG) for optimizations like loop unrolling, constant propagation, and dead code elimination.
3. **Hardware-Software Co-design:** The CDFG helps partition the design — *hot, data-heavy subgraphs* go to custom hardware (ASIC/FPGA), while *control-heavy sections* remain on a CPU.
4. **Static Timing Analysis:** Def-use chains in the CDFG are used to estimate the lifetime of variables and thus the number of registers needed.
5. **Formal Verification:** Model checking tools traverse the CDFG state-space to prove correctness properties.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Building a CDFG from C Code

Consider this embedded C snippet (typical DSP / control kernel):

```c
int example(int a, int b, int c) {
    int x, y, z;
    if (a > b) {
        x = a * 2;
        y = x + c;
    } else {
        x = b - c;
        y = x * 2;
    }
    z = y - a;
    return z;
}
```

### Step 1 — Identify Basic Blocks
A **basic block** is a maximal straight-line sequence of statements with no internal branches.

- **BB0** (Entry): `if (a > b)` decision
- **BB1** (Then-branch): `x = a*2;` then `y = x+c;`
- **BB2** (Else-branch): `x = b-c;` then `y = x*2;`
- **BB3** (Merge): `z = y - a;`
- **BB4** (Exit): `return z;`

### Step 2 — Construct the CFG Skeleton

The CFG connects these blocks:

- BB0 → BB1 (when `a > b` is TRUE)
- BB0 → BB2 (when `a > b` is FALSE)
- BB1 → BB3 (unconditional fall-through)
- BB2 → BB3 (unconditional fall-through)
- BB3 → BB4 (unconditional)

### Step 3 — Construct DFG Inside Each Basic Block

**DFG for BB1** (`x = a*2;` then `y = x+c;`):
- `*` node takes inputs `a` and constant `2`, produces `x`
- `+` node takes inputs `x` and `c`, produces `y`

**DFG for BB2** (`x = b-c;` then `y = x*2;`):
- `-` node takes inputs `b` and `c`, produces `x`
- `*` node takes inputs `x` and constant `2`, produces `y`

**DFG for BB3** (`z = y - a;`):
- `-` node takes inputs `y` and `a`, produces `z`

### Step 4 — Compose the CDFG

The full CDFG has the CFG as its **outer shell**, with each basic block's DFG as its **inner content**.

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation — Cyclomatic Complexity

We will derive the cyclomatic complexity of the CDFG above.

**Given:** $E$ = number of edges, $N$ = number of nodes, $P$ = number of connected components.

### Step 4a — Count the Nodes
- Entry node: 1
- BB0 (decision): 1
- BB1 (then): 1
- BB2 (else): 1
- BB3 (merge): 1
- BB4 (exit): 1
- Total $N = 6$

### Step 4b — Count the Edges
- Entry → BB0 : 1
- BB0 → BB1 : 1
- BB0 → BB2 : 1
- BB1 → BB3 : 1
- BB2 → BB3 : 1
- BB3 → BB4 : 1
- Total $E = 6$

### Step 4c — Apply the Formula

$$M = E - N + 2P$$

$$M = 6 - 6 + 2(1)$$

$$M = 2$$

### Step 4d — Interpret the Result

$M = 2$ means there are **2 linearly independent paths** through the function. This matches intuition:
- Path 1: BB0 → BB1 → BB3 → BB4 (then-branch)
- Path 2: BB0 → BB2 → BB3 → BB4 (else-branch)

This matches the formula $N_{\text{paths}} = (b + 1) = (1 + 1) = 2$ where $b=1$ is the single branch.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation Rule:** $M$ directly gives the *minimum number of test cases* required for branch coverage in software testing. In hardware, $M$ is the *minimum number of test vectors* for an FSM-equivalent verification.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative View of CFG vs. DFG vs. CDFG

| Property | CFG (Control Flow Graph) | DFG (Data Flow Graph) | CDFG (Combined) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Captures** | Control order, branches, loops | Data dependencies only | Both control AND data |
| **Nodes** | Basic blocks / statements | Operations (+, *, −) | Both |
| **Edges** | Control flow arrows | Data flow arrows | Both, hierarchically |
| **Cycles allowed** | Yes (back edges for loops) | No (always acyclic) | Yes (due to control loops) |
| **Use in HLS** | Determines scheduling branches | Determines operation parallelism | Unified scheduling & binding |
| **Loop handling** | Explicit loop nodes | Unrolled / pipelined within iter | Annotated with trip counts |
| **Example tool** | LLVM CFG, GCC `cfg.dot` | DFG in Vitis HLS per BB | Vitis HLS, Catapult, Stratus |
| **KTU exam frequency** | High | High | **Very High** (favorite) |

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Scheduling on a CDFG (Force-Directed / List Scheduling Preview)

For the **DFG inside BB1** (`x = a*2; y = x+c;`), let us perform **ASAP scheduling** assuming each operation takes **1 cycle** and one multiplier + one adder are available.

**Step A:** Identify all operations.
- Op1: `*` (a, 2) → x
- Op2: `+` (x, c) → y

**Step B:** Build the dependency edges.
- Op1 must complete before Op2 starts (Op2 reads `x` produced by Op1).
- Dependency: Op1 → Op2

**Step C:** Compute ASAP times.
- Op1 has no predecessors → $T_{\text{ASAP}}(\text{Op1}) = 0$
- Op2 has Op1 as predecessor → $T_{\text{ASAP}}(\text{Op2}) = T_{\text{ASAP}}(\text{Op1}) + 1 = 1$

**Step D:** Critical path length.

$$T_{\text{ASAP}} = \max_{o} T_{\text{ASAP}}(o) + d(o) = 1 + 1 = 2 \text{ cycles}$$

**Step E:** Possible schedule (cycle 0: Op1, cycle 1: Op2) — total = **2 cycles**.

This is the **scheduling bound** the HLS tool will try to meet or beat.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Full CDFG for the Worked Example

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([ENTRY]) --> B0["BB0: if a > b"]
    B0 -- TRUE a>b --> B1["BB1: x = a*2; y = x+c"]
    B0 -- FALSE a<=b --> B2["BB2: x = b-c; y = x*2"]
    B1 --> B3["BB3: z = y - a"]
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B4["BB4: return z"]
    B4 --> Z([EXIT])
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — DFG Inside BB1 (Detailed Operation Graph)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inp1["a"] --> op1(("* 2"))
    c2["2"] --> op1
    op1 --> xv["x"]
    xv --> op2(("+ c"))
    cval["c"] --> op2
    op2 --> yv["y"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — DFG Inside BB2

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    b1["b"] --> op3(("- c"))
    cval2["c"] --> op3
    op3 --> xv2["x"]
    xv2 --> op4(("* 2"))
    const2["2"] --> op4
    op4 --> yv2["y"]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Hierarchical CDFG Architecture (Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CFG_LAYER["CONTROL FLOW GRAPH LAYER"]
        E0([ENTRY]) --> N1[DECISION if a b]
        N1 -- T --> N2[THEN BLOCK]
        N1 -- F --> N3[ELSE BLOCK]
        N2 --> N4[MERGE BLOCK]
        N3 --> N4
        N4 --> N5[RETURN EXIT]
    end
    subgraph DFG_THEN["DFG INSIDE THEN BB1"]
        O1(("a * 2")) --> O2(("x + c"))
    end
    subgraph DFG_ELSE["DFG INSIDE ELSE BB2"]
        O3(("b - c")) --> O4(("x * 2"))
    end
    subgraph DFG_MERGE["DFG INSIDE MERGE BB3"]
        O5(("y - a"))
    end
    N2 -. contains .-> DFG_THEN
    N3 -. contains .-> DFG_ELSE
    N4 -. contains .-> DFG_MERGE
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — CDFG of a Loop (Typical for Embedded DSP)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([ENTRY]) --> B1["BB_init: i=0, sum=0"]
    B1 --> LH["LOOP_HEADER: i < N"]
    LH -- TRUE --> B2["BB_body: sum = sum + a i"]
    B2 --> B3["BB_inc: i = i + 1"]
    B3 -. BACK EDGE .-> LH
    LH -- FALSE --> B4["BB_exit: return sum"]
    B4 --> Z([EXIT])
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** The *back edge* (dotted arrow from BB_inc back to LOOP_HEADER) is what makes a CFG cyclic. When HLS tools schedule the loop body, they analyze this back edge to perform **loop pipelining**, **unrolling**, or **software pipelining (modulo scheduling)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]
**Define a Control Data Flow Graph (CDFG). List its two hierarchical components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Control Data Flow Graph (CDFG)** is a graph-based intermediate representation used in high-level synthesis and embedded system design to represent a behavioral description. It captures **both** the control sequencing and the data dependencies of a program in a single unified structure.

The **two hierarchical components** of a CDFG are:

1. **Control Flow Graph (CFG) Layer** — the outer shell comprising basic blocks, decision nodes, branch nodes, loop nodes, and merge nodes, connected by control edges that dictate execution order.
2. **Data Flow Graph (DFG) Layer** — the inner content inside each basic block, comprising operation nodes (arithmetic, logic, I/O) connected by data edges that represent def-use dependencies and value flow.

> **Marking Tip (1 mark each):** definition + CFG layer + DFG layer. Examiner expects the word *hierarchy* or *nested*.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between a Data Flow Graph (DFG) and a Control Flow Graph (CFG). Why is a CDFG needed instead of either alone?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | DFG | CFG |
|---|---|---|
| What it represents | Data dependencies between operations | Sequencing/control between statements |
| Cyclic? | No (acyclic) | Yes (back edges in loops) |
| Captures branches? | No | Yes |
| Captures values? | Yes (def-use) | No |

A **CDFG is needed** because real embedded programs contain *both* control structures (branches, loops) and data computations. A DFG alone cannot model conditional execution; a CFG alone cannot model parallel data operations. The **CDFG nests a DFG inside each basic block of a CFG**, giving a complete behavioral model suitable for high-level synthesis, scheduling, and resource binding.

> **Marking Tip:** Award 1.5 marks for the comparison table and 1.5 marks for the justification of CDFG need.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Sample QP, Module 2]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the structure of a Control Data Flow Graph (CDFG). List the various node and edge types used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the following C program, draw the CDFG and compute the cyclomatic complexity. Show the basic blocks clearly. **(7 marks)**

```c
int compute(int n) {
    int sum = 0, prod = 1;
    if (n > 0) {
        for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++) {
            sum = sum + i;
            prod = prod * i;
        }
    } else {
        sum = -1;
    }
    return sum + prod;
}
```

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — CDFG Structure (7 marks)

**Structure of a CDFG (5 marks for explanation + 2 marks for diagram):**

A CDFG is a hierarchical graph with two layers:

- **Outer CFG layer** — represents the control flow using basic blocks and control nodes.
- **Inner DFG layer** — each basic block embeds a DFG of operations and data dependencies.

**Node Types (list 5+ for full marks):**
1. Entry node — program start.
2. Exit node — program end.
3. Assignment / operation node — performs arithmetic/logic.
4. Decision (diamond) node — conditional branch.
5. Loop header node — entry of an iterative construct.
6. Merge / join node — reconverges branches.
7. I/O node — read/write operations.

**Edge Types:**
1. **Data edges** — flow of values between operations.
2. **Control edges** — sequencing of statements / blocks.
3. **Back edges** — close loops, target is a dominator.
4. **Forward edges** — acyclic flow.

**Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to the Mermaid diagrams in Section 4. A standard CDFG should be drawn with a CFG skeleton and a sample DFG inside one of its basic blocks.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — Cyclomatic Complexity (7 marks)

**Step 1: Identify Basic Blocks (2 marks)**

- **BB0 (Entry/Init):** `sum=0; prod=1;`
- **BB1 (Decision):** `if (n > 0)`
- **BB2 (Loop Header):** `for(i=1; i<=n; i++)`
- **BB3 (Loop Body):** `sum = sum + i; prod = prod * i;`
- **BB4 (Loop Increment):** `i = i + 1` (or implicit)
- **BB5 (Loop Exit / Else):** `sum = -1;`
- **BB6 (Return):** `return sum + prod;`
- **BB7 (Exit):**

**Step 2: Count Nodes and Edges (2 marks)**

- Nodes $N$: 8 (BB0 through BB7)
- Edges $E$:
  - BB0 → BB1 (1)
  - BB1 → BB2 (TRUE branch) (1)
  - BB1 → BB5 (FALSE branch) (1)
  - BB2 → BB3 (loop body entry) (1)
  - BB3 → BB4 (1)
  - BB4 → BB2 (back edge) (1)
  - BB2 → BB6 (loop exit) (1)
  - BB5 → BB6 (1)
  - BB6 → BB7 (1)
  - **Total $E = 9$**

**Step 3: Apply Cyclomatic Complexity Formula (2 marks)**

$$M = E - N + 2P = 9 - 8 + 2(1) = 3$$

**Step 4: Interpret (1 mark)**

$M = 3$ means there are **3 linearly independent paths**:
1. Path 1: BB0 → BB1 → BB5 → BB6 → BB7 (the `else` branch, $n \le 0$)
2. Path 2: BB0 → BB1 → BB2 → BB3 → BB4 → BB2 → BB6 → BB7 (one loop iteration)
3. Path 3: BB0 → BB1 → BB2 → BB3 → BB4 → BB2 → BB3 → BB4 → BB2 → BB6 → BB7 (multiple loop iterations)

These are the test cases needed for branch + loop coverage.

**CDFG Diagram (representative structure):**
```
Entry → BB0 → BB1 (diamond decision)
BB1 -TRUE-> BB2 (loop header) → BB3 (body) → BB4 (inc) --back edge--> BB2
BB2 -loop exit-> BB6 (return) → Exit
BB1 -FALSE-> BB5 (sum=-1) → BB6 → Exit
```

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Sample QP, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain how a CDFG is used in **High-Level Synthesis (HLS)**. Discuss the role of scheduling and binding in this context. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the following loop-based program fragment, draw the CDFG and identify the critical path of the DFG inside the loop body. Assume each operation takes 1 cycle. **(7 marks)**

```c
for (i = 0; i < N; i++) {
    a[i] = (b[i] + c[i]) * d[i] - e[i];
}
```

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — CDFG in HLS (7 marks)

**1. HLS Phases Driven by CDFG (3 marks):**

After the front-end (lexing, parsing) and behavioral optimization, the C code is converted into a **CDFG**. The CDFG is the *primary input* to the HLS backend, which performs:

- **Scheduling:** Decides *in which clock cycle* each operation in the DFG executes, subject to data dependencies and resource constraints. Algorithms: **ASAP, ALAP, List Scheduling, Force-Directed Scheduling**.
- **Allocation/Resource Binding:** Decides *which hardware unit* (adder, multiplier, register, mux) implements each operation. Goal: minimize area, latency, or power.
- **Control Logic Extraction:** From the CFG layer, the HLS tool generates a **Finite State Machine (FSM)** or micro-code ROM that sequences the data-path operations.
- **Interface Generation:** I/O nodes in the CDFG map to bus protocol interfaces (AXI, AHB, custom).

**2. Role of Scheduling (2 marks):**

Scheduling takes the **DFG inside each BB** and assigns operations to clock cycles. The **critical path** of the DFG determines the **minimum possible latency**. Loop pipelining and chaining reduce overall latency.

**3. Role of Binding (2 marks):**

Binding maps each operation to a *physical resource*. Multiple `+` operations can share one adder via **time-multiplexing**, controlled by muxes (which appear as new nodes in the RTL). Optimal binding minimizes area while respecting the schedule's resource demands per cycle.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Critical Path of Loop DFG (7 marks)

**Step 1: Parse the loop body (1 mark)**

The loop body computes: $a[i] = (b[i] + c[i]) \times d[i] - e[i]$

This has **3 operations** in dependency order:
- Op1: `+` of `b[i]` and `c[i]` → produces temp `t1`
- Op2: `*` of `t1` and `d[i]` → produces temp `t2`
- Op3: `-` of `t2` and `e[i]` → produces `a[i]`

**Step 2: Draw the DFG (3 marks)**

```
b[i] --\
        + -- t1 -- * -- t2 -- - -- a[i]
c[i] --/              |             |
                   d[i]            e[i]
```

**Step 3: Compute critical path (2 marks)**

All three operations form a **chain**:

$$\text{Op1} \rightarrow \text{Op2} \rightarrow \text{Op3}$$

Each takes 1 cycle, so:

$$T_{\text{critical}} = 1 + 1 + 1 = 3 \text{ cycles per iteration}$$

**Step 4: Loop latency (1 mark)**

For $N$ iterations, total latency = $N \times 3$ cycles (without pipelining).
With ideal pipelining (II = 1), throughput = 1 iteration/cycle, total = $N + 2$ cycles.

**CDFG Structure (representative):**
- BB_init: `i=0`
- BB_loop_header: `i < N`
- BB_body: DFG shown above
- BB_inc: `i = i+1` → back edge to header
- BB_exit: write `a[i]` results, return

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make (and Where They Lose Marks):**
>
> 1. **Confusing DFG with CDFG:** Drawing only data dependencies for a `for` loop and *forgetting the back edge* loses **2–3 marks**. Always include the loop back edge and the loop header/exit nodes.
>
> 2. **Wrong Cyclomatic Complexity formula:** Writing $M = N - E + 2$ (inverted signs) is a **1-mark deduction**. The correct formula is $M = E - N + 2P$.
>
> 3. **Miscounting edges/nodes:** Off-by-one errors in node/edge counts are common. **Tip:** Add a "virtual Exit node" if the program has `return` statements — many students forget this.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the "merge" node** after an if-else. The CFG must show control flow *reconverging*; missing the merge loses marks for "incomplete CFG."
>
> 5. **Mixing up data edges and control edges in the diagram.** Use **solid arrows for data**, **dashed arrows for control** (or different colors). Examiners explicitly check legend discipline.
>
> 6. **Not labeling the loop trip count or condition.** If the question specifies a loop, always write `i < N` on the loop header edge — this is worth 0.5–1 mark.
>
> 7. **Forgetting to interpret $M$:** A correct number with **no interpretation** ("$M=3$ means 3 test paths") loses 1 mark. Always state *what* the cyclomatic complexity implies.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **CDFG = CFG (outer) + DFG (inner)**: a hierarchical, two-layer representation of a behavioral description.
- **Basic Block** = maximal straight-line code with no internal branches; the atomic unit of the CFG.
- **CFG Nodes:** Entry, Exit, Assignment, Decision (diamond), Loop Header, Merge, I/O.
- **CFG Edges:** Control edges, Back edges (for loops), Forward edges.
- **DFG Nodes:** Operations (`+, −, ×, ÷, shift, compare, mux`).
- **DFG Edges:** Data edges — def-use chains carrying values.
- **Cyclomatic Complexity:** $M = E - N + 2P$ → minimum test paths, minimum test vectors for verification.
- **HLS uses CDFG for:** scheduling (cycle assignment) and binding (resource assignment) to produce RTL.
- **Critical Path of DFG** = ASAP lower bound on schedule length.
- **Loop nodes carry back edges** — the only source of cycles in a CDFG.
- **CDFG ≠ CDF + G** — it is a *nesting* of DFGs inside CFG nodes, not a flat union.
- **Tools that use CDFG:** Vitis HLS (Xilinx), Catapult (Mentor/Siemens), Stratus (Cadence), Intel HLS Compiler, LLVM (for CFG-only).
- **KTU-Favorite Questions:** (i) Draw CDFG for a given C snippet; (ii) Compute cyclomatic complexity; (iii) Explain HLS phases; (iv) Compare CFG/DFG/CDFG; (v) Identify critical path in a loop DFG.
- **Always draw:** legend, node labels, edge types, loop conditions, merge nodes. Examiners reward *completeness* and *clarity*.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: State Machine and Sequential Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT631 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** State Machine and Sequential Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Finite State Machine (FSM)** is a mathematical model of computation used to design both computer programs and sequential logic circuits. It is defined as a 6-tuple $(S, I, O, \delta, \lambda, s_0)$ where:

- $S$ = a finite, non-empty set of **states**
- $I$ = a finite, non-empty set of **input symbols** (input alphabet)
- $O$ = a finite, non-empty set of **output symbols** (output alphabet)
- $\delta: S \times I \rightarrow S$ = the **state transition function**
- $\lambda$ = the **output function** (form varies between Mealy and Moore models)
- $s_0 \in S$ = the **initial state** (start state)

In the context of **Embedded System Design**, the *Sequential Model* refers to a system design methodology where the embedded system is modeled as a reactive entity that processes a stream of input events and produces outputs by transitioning through a well-defined sequence of discrete **states**. The behavior of the system at any instant is fully determined by its current state and the current input stimulus.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> State Machine and Sequential Model forms the foundation of modeling **reactive real-time embedded systems**.
