The embedded target devices: - Frequently have **no display**, **no keyboard**, and **no file system**. - Run **bare-metal firmware** or a lightweight **RTOS** (e.g., FreeRTOS, Zephyr). - Operate with severely limited **power budgets** (battery-operated IoT nodes). - Use **non-standard or proprietary ISAs** (AVR, MSP430, PIC, custom ASICs). Therefore, all compilation, linking, and binary preparation MUST occur on a powerful general-purpose host machine — a process fundamentally dependent on the **Cross Compilation Toolchain**. ## 1.4 Key Terminology Mapping (KTU Board-Ready Vocabulary) | Term | Engineering Meaning | Typical Example | |---|---|---| | **Host** | Machine where the compiler runs | Intel i7 laptop running Ubuntu 22.04 | | **Target** | Machine that will execute the binary | STM32F407 (ARM Cortex-M4) | | **Cross Toolchain** | Suite of cross-development tools | `arm-none-eabi-gcc`, `binutils`, `gdb` | | **ABI** | Application Binary Interface | AAPCS for ARM, ILP32 for 32-bit RISC-V | | **ELF** | Executable and Linkable Format | Standard output for `gcc` toolchains | | **OBJ/HEX** | Intel HEX / Motorola S-record | Flashed onto target's non-volatile memory | | **Sysroot** | Directory of target system libraries | `/usr/local/arm-none-eabi/lib` | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Knowledge Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of a Cross-Compilation Toolchain A complete cross toolchain is **not just a single compiler**. It is a coordinated collection of binaries, each performing a specific transformation. For the GNU GCC ecosystem, the canonical naming convention follows the pattern: $$\text{tuple} = \text{[arch]}-\text{[vendor]}-\text{[os]}-\text{[abi]}$$ For example: `arm-none-eabi-gcc` translates to: - **arch** = `arm` (target architecture) - **vendor** = `none` (no specific vendor) - **os** = `eabi` (Embedded Application Binary Interface, no operating system) - **gcc** = GNU C Compiler (the actual executable) > [!IMPORTANT] > The prefix tells the toolchain driver *which backend* to invoke at each stage. The **cross compilation driver** is just a wrapper that invokes the correct preprocessor, assembler, and linker with the appropriate `--target` flags. ## 2.2 The Four-Stage Cross-Compilation Pipeline The cross compilation process is logically identical to native compilation, but every stage is *target-aware* and *host-executed*: ### **Stage 1: Preprocessing (`cpp`)** - **Input:** `main.c` (source file) + `header.h` files - **Host Action:** The preprocessor resolves all `#include`, `#define`, and `#ifdef` directives using the *target's* system header path (specified via `-I` and `--sysroot`). - **Output:** `main.i` — pure C code with all macros expanded and headers inlined. - **Tool:** `arm-none-eabi-cpp main.c -o main.i` ### **Stage 2: Compilation to Assembly (`cc1`)** - **Input:** `main.i` - **Host Action:** The compiler's front-end parses C syntax, performs semantic analysis, and the back-end generates **target-specific ARM assembly instructions** (e.g., `MOV`, `LDR`, `STR`, `B`). - **Output:** `main.s` — ARM assembly language. - **Tool:** `arm-none-eabi-gcc -S main.i -o main.s` ### **Stage 3: Assembly to Object Code (`as`)** - **Input:** `main.s` - **Host Action:** The cross-assembler converts ARM mnemonics into **binary machine code** for the target CPU, producing relocatable object code. - **Output:** `main.o` — relocatable ELF object file with target-format symbols. - **Tool:** `arm-none-eabi-as main.s -o main.o` ### **Stage 4: Linking (`ld`)** - **Input:** `main.o` + library objects (e.g., `libc.a`, `libm.a`) + linker script (`STM32F407.ld`) - **Host Action:** The cross-linker resolves symbols, applies **memory-mapped addresses** (defined in the linker script), and produces the final executable. - **Output:** `firmware.elf` → optionally converted to `firmware.hex` (Intel HEX) for flashing. - **Tool:** `arm-none-eabi-ld -T STM32F407.ld main.o -o firmware.elf` ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Knowledge Sheet (Cheat Table) | Parameter | Native Compilation | Cross Compilation | |---|---|---| | **Host CPU** | Same as Target (e.g., x86 → x86) | Different (e.g., x86 → ARM) | | **Host OS** | Same as Target (e.g., Linux → Linux) | Often different (Ubuntu → Bare-metal) | | **Compiler Prefix** | `gcc`, `cc` | `arm-none-eabi-gcc`, `riscv64-unknown-elf-gcc` | | **Binary Format** | Native ELF (glibc-linked) | Bare-metal ELF (newlib-linked) | | **Typical Output** | Runs directly on host | Flashed via JTAG/SWD/USB to target | | **Performance Tuning** | Optimize for host's cache, pipeline | Optimize for target's pipeline (e.g., `-mcpu=cortex-m4`) | | **Debug Info** | GDB on host (native) | GDB + `gdbserver` or OpenOCD (remote) | | **Linking** | Dynamic linking common (`glibc.so`) | **Static linking mandatory** for bare-metal | | **File System** | Required | Often **none** on target | | **Standard Library** | Host's libc (e.g., glibc) | Newlib / picolibc / musl (target-specific) | ## 2.4 The Three Critical Cross-Compiler Toolchain Variants (KTU Favourite) 1. **GNU Toolchain (`gcc` + `binutils` + `newlib` + `gdb`)** — *Most widely taught in KTU labs* - Open source, supports ARM, RISC-V, AVR, MSP430, x86, MIPS, PPC - Tools: `arm-none-eabi-gcc`, `binutils-arm-none-eabi` - License: GPL 2. **LLVM/Clang Toolchain** — *Modern industry standard* - Modular, better diagnostics, single static compilation passes - Tools: `clang --target=arm-none-eabi`, `lld` (linker), `lldb` (debugger) - License: Apache 2.0 / LLVM Exception 3. **Vendor-Proprietary Toolchains** — *High optimization, closed source* - ARM Compiler 6 (`armclang`), IAR Embedded Workbench, Keil µVision - Used in safety-critical automotive (ISO 26262) and aerospace (DO-178C) - License: Commercial (per-seat) ## 2.5 Why Standard Library Choice Matters (Often Tested) A cross compiler for bare-metal targets **cannot link against the host's glibc** because the target has no dynamic loader, no system calls, and no file descriptor model. Hence: - **Newlib** — Lightweight libc for embedded ARM, RISC-V, MIPS - **Picolibc** — Stripped-down derivative, smaller footprint - **uClibc-ng** — Used in older Linux-based embedded systems The newlib `syscalls.c` provides stubs for `_sbrk`, `_write`, `_read`, etc., that you as the firmware engineer must redirect to your target's peripherals (UART, GPIO, etc.). > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Cross-compilers are the silent foundation of every smartphone (ARM-compiled iOS/Android), every car ECU (AUTOSAR + ARM/RH850), every IoT sensor (RISC-V + Zephyr RTOS), and even NASA's Mars helicopter Ingenuity (custom radiation-hardened cross-compiled binary). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough & Transformation Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Analytical Example — The Cross-Compilation Command Chain **Scenario:** You are developing firmware for an **STM32 Nucleo-F411RE** board (ARM Cortex-M4) on an Ubuntu 22.04 x86-64 host. The source file is `blink.c`. The linker script is `stm32f411.ld`. The startup file is `startup_stm32f411xe.s`. ### **Analytical Derivation: The Transformation Sequence** **Step 1 — Invoke the Preprocessor (target-aware)** $$ \texttt{arm-none-eabi-gcc -E -mcpu=cortex-m4 -mthumb \\ -I./include -I./cmsis blink.c -o blink.i} $$ - `-E` halts after preprocessing - `-mcpu=cortex-m4` selects the **target CPU backend** - `-mthumb` generates **16-bit Thumb-2 instructions** (denser code, smaller flash footprint) - `-I` flags add include paths using the *target* CMSIS headers, not host headers **Step 2 — Generate Target Assembly** $$ \texttt{arm-none-eabi-gcc -S -mcpu=cortex-m4 -mthumb \\ -O2 blink.i -o blink.s} $$ - `-O2` applies target-specific optimizations: loop unrolling, function inlining, dead-code elimination - The output `blink.s` contains real ARM instructions like: $$\begin{aligned} \texttt{bl} &\quad \texttt{GPIO\_ToggleBits} \\ \texttt{ldr} &\quad \texttt{r0, [r1]} \\ \texttt{bx} &\quad \texttt{lr} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Assemble to Object Code** $$ \texttt{arm-none-eabi-as -mcpu=cortex-m4 -mthumb \\ blink.s -o blink.o} $$ - Produces relocatable ELF object file with unresolved symbols - Symbol table entries use 32-bit ARM AAPCS calling convention **Step 4 — Link with Startup and Linker Script** $$ \texttt{arm-none-eabi-gcc -mcpu=cortex-m4 -mthumb \\ -T stm32f411.ld startup\_stm32f411xe.s blink.o \\ -nostdlib -lgcc -o firmware.elf} $$ - `-T` selects the **linker script** that defines memory regions: $$\begin{aligned} \texttt{MEMORY} \\ \{ \\ &\quad \texttt{FLASH (rx) : ORIGIN = 0x08000000, LENGTH = 512K} \\ &\quad \texttt{RAM (rwx) : ORIGIN = 0x20000000, LENGTH = 128K} \\ \} \end{aligned}$$ - `-nostdlib` prevents linking host's glibc; only newlib's `libc.a` and compiler's `libgcc.a` are used - The vector table is placed at the start of FLASH; `.data` and `.bss` are placed in RAM **Step 5 — Convert ELF to Intel HEX for Flashing** $$ \texttt{arm-none-eabi-objcopy -O ihex \\ firmware.elf firmware.hex} $$ - Intel HEX is the de-facto format accepted by ST-Link, J-Link, and OpenOCD programmers - Each line encodes: `:LLAAAATT[DD...]CC\n` (Start code, Length, Address, Type, Data, Checksum) **Step 6 — Size Verification (KTU-style sanity check)** $$ \texttt{arm-none-eabi-size firmware.elf} $$ Sample output: | Section | Size (bytes) | |---|---| | **text** (code in FLASH) | 1,248 | | **data** (initialized vars in RAM, copied from FLASH at boot) | 16 | | **bss** (zero-initialized vars in RAM) | 512 | | **Total FLASH occupancy** | **1,264** | | **Total RAM occupancy** | **528** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cross-Verification Logic:** The compiled binary consumes only **1,264 bytes of FLASH** out of the available **512 KB**, confirming the cross-compiler generated ultra-efficient target code. A native x86 binary for the same logic would be ~16 KB. The size reduction is a direct consequence of the **Thumb-2 instruction set** and the **bare-metal newlib** (no POSIX overhead). ## 3.2 Cross-Compiler Issue Analysis — Common Pitfalls | Issue Symptom | Root Cause | Resolution | |---|---|---| | `error: unrecognized opcode` | Compiler defaults to x86 backend | Verify `--target` or prefix is correct | | `undefined reference to _sbrk` | Missing newlib syscalls stub | Implement `syscalls.c` for your BSP | | Binary runs on host, not target | Accidentally used native `gcc` | Check `which gcc` vs `which arm-none-eabi-gcc` | | Hard fault on target startup | Vector table misaligned | Ensure `.isr_vector` at `0x08000000` in linker script | | Flash overflow on target | `-O0` debug build, no optimization | Use `-Os` (optimize for size) for release | ## 3.3 Mathematical Model — Cross-Compiler Selection Cost Function For a KTU design question, you may be asked to choose a toolchain. The decision metric can be modeled as: $$ C_{\text{total}} = w_1 \cdot S_{\text{code}} + w_2 \cdot t_{\text{build}} + w_3 \cdot C_{\text{license}} $$ Where: - $S_{\text{code}}$ = Final binary size in KB - $t_{\text{build}}$ = Wall-clock build time in seconds - $C_{\text{license}}$ = Commercial cost in USD (0 for open source) - $w_1, w_2, w_3$ = Application-specific weights > [!NOTE] > For a **medical implant**, $w_1$ is dominant (code size must be minimal for tiny flash). For an **automotive ECU with ASIL-D**, $w_3$ is high (paid ARM Compiler is ISO 26262 certified). For a **student hobbyist**, $w_3 = 0$ (use free GCC). # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Cross-Compilation Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph HOST["HOST MACHINE: x86-64 Ubuntu Workstation"] source["Source Code
main.c"] crossCompiler["CROSS COMPILER
arm-none-eabi-gcc"] crossAssembler["CROSS ASSEMBLER
arm-none-eabi-as"] crossLinker["CROSS LINKER
arm-none-eabi-ld"] objcopy["OBJ COPY
arm-none-eabi-objcopy"] hostGDB["HOST GDB
arm-none-eabi-gdb"] end subgraph TARGET["TARGET: STM32 ARM Cortex-M4"] flashMem["Internal FLASH
512 KB at 0x08000000"] ramMem["Internal SRAM
128 KB at 0x20000000"] cpuCore["ARM Cortex-M4 Core
Thumb-2 ISA"] peripherals["On-Chip Peripherals
GPIO UART ADC TIM"] end debugger["JTAG / SWD Probe
ST-Link V2"] source --> crossCompiler crossCompiler -->|"Assembly
main.s"| crossAssembler crossAssembler -->|"Relocatable
main.o"| crossLinker crossLinker -->|"Linked ELF
firmware.elf"| objcopy objcopy -->|"Intel HEX
firmware.hex"| debugger debugger -->|"Flash Write"| flashMem flashMem --> cpuCore cpuCore --> ramMem cpuCore --> peripherals hostGDB -.->|"Remote Protocol
GDB Remote Serial"| debugger ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Native vs Cross Compilation Comparison Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD startA["Developer writes
C source code"] startA --> decision{"Is the target
machine
the same as
host?"} decision -->|"YES
Same CPU and OS"| nativeCompiler["Native Compiler
gcc or clang
Runs on x86-64
Produces x86-64 binary"] decision -->|"NO
Different CPU or OS"| crossCompiler["Cross Compiler
arm-none-eabi-gcc
Runs on x86-64
Produces ARM binary"] nativeCompiler --> nativeBinary["Native Binary
Runs immediately
on host"] nativeBinary --> hostRun["Execute on
developer laptop"] crossCompiler --> crossBinary["Cross-Compiled Binary
firmware.elf / .hex"] crossBinary --> flashAction["Flash via
JTAG / SWD / UART"] flashAction --> targetRun["Execute on
embedded target board"] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Four-Stage Cross-Compilation Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Stage 1
Preprocessor
cpp"] -->|"main.c to main.i"| B["Stage 2
Compiler Front + Back end
cc1"] B -->|"main.i to main.s
ARM Assembly"| C["Stage 3
Cross Assembler
as"] C -->|"main.s to main.o
Relocatable ELF"| D["Stage 4
Cross Linker
ld"] D -->|"main.o plus
libc.a plus libgcc.a
plus startup.o plus linker script"| E["Final Image
firmware.elf"] E --> F["OBJ Copy
objcopy"] F --> G["Intel HEX
firmware.hex
Ready for flashing"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Architectural Insight:** Notice how the **entire pipeline stays on the host machine** until the final binary. The cross compiler is the *transformer* — it converts human-readable source into target-native machine code without ever executing on the target. This is the defining characteristic that distinguishes a cross compiler from a native compiler. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** *Define a cross compiler. Why is it indispensable in embedded system development?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **cross compiler** is a compiler that runs on a *host* machine (e.g., x86-64 PC running Linux) and produces executable code for a *target* machine (e.g., ARM Cortex microcontroller) with a different Instruction Set Architecture. It is **indispensable in embedded systems** for the following reasons: 1. **Resource Constraints:** Most embedded targets lack the CPU power, RAM, and storage to host a full compiler. For example, an 8-bit ATmega328P has only 2 KB RAM and 32 KB flash — insufficient to run GCC. 2. **Lack of I/O Peripherals:** Targets usually have no display, keyboard, or file system needed to interact with a compiler. 3. **Performance & Convenience:** Developers need a rich editing, debugging, and version-control environment available only on full PCs. 4. **Architecture Diversity:** Embedded systems use varied ISAs (ARM, AVR, RISC-V, PIC). A cross compiler abstracts this complexity. > [!NOTE] > **Valuation Key:** Full 3 marks requires the definition **plus** at least two specific reasons. A bare definition alone scores 1 mark. ### **Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Differentiate between a native compiler and a cross compiler with suitable examples.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Parameter | Native Compiler | Cross Compiler | |---|---|---| | **Definition** | Compiles code for the same machine on which it runs | Compiles code for a machine different from the one it runs on | | **Example** | `gcc main.c` on x86-64 Linux produces x86-64 Linux binary | `arm-none-eabi-gcc main.c` on x86-64 Linux produces ARM binary | | **Standard Library** | Links against host's glibc | Links against newlib / picolibc | | **Output Execution** | Runs immediately on host | Must be transferred to target via JTAG, SWD, or bootloader | | **Toolchain Prefix** | None (just `gcc`) | Triple-prefixed (e.g., `arm-none-eabi-`) | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]` **(a)** Explain the architecture and working of a typical cross-compilation toolchain. List the major components and their functions. **\[7 marks\]** **(b)** With a neat diagram, describe the step-by-step process of cross compiling a C program for an ARM Cortex-M4 target on an x86 host. Mention the role of the linker script. **\[7 marks\]** --- #### **Model Solution for Question A(a):** A cross-compilation toolchain is a coordinated set of software utilities that together transform high-level source code into an executable binary suitable for an embedded target. The architecture has **five major components**: **1. Preprocessor (`cpp`)** - Resolves `#include`, `#define`, `#ifdef` directives - Uses *target* system header directories via `-I` and `--sysroot` - Output: translation unit (`.i` file) with all macros expanded **2. Compiler (`cc1`)** - Two sub-modules: **front-end** (parses C/C++ syntax, builds Abstract Syntax Tree) and **back-end** (generates target assembly) - Performs target-specific optimizations: register allocation, instruction scheduling, dead-code elimination - Output: assembly language file (`.s`) with target ISA mnemonics **3. Assembler (`as`)** - Converts assembly mnemonics into binary machine code - Generates **relocatable ELF object files** (`.o`) with unresolved external symbols - Builds the symbol table and relocation entries **4. Linker (`ld`)** - Combines multiple `.o` files with **static libraries** (e.g., `libc.a`, `libgcc.a`) - Resolves symbol references and applies **relocations** to absolute addresses - Uses a **linker script** to define memory regions and section placement - Output: final ELF executable (`.elf`) **5. Binary Utilities (`objcopy`, `objdump`, `size`, `readelf`)** - `objcopy`: Converts ELF to Intel HEX / Motorola S-record - `objdump`: Disassembles for inspection - `size`: Reports section-wise memory usage - `readelf`: Examines ELF headers and symbols **Example toolchain triple:** `arm-none-eabi-gcc` - `arm` = Target architecture - `none` = No specific vendor - `eabi` = Embedded Application Binary Interface - `gcc` = GNU Compiler Collection binary #### **Model Solution for Question A(b):** The step-by-step process with the host-target data flow: **Step 1:** The developer writes `main.c` on the x86-64 host and runs: $$\texttt{arm-none-eabi-gcc -c -mcpu=cortex-m4 -mthumb -I./include main.c -o main.o}$$ This single command invokes the preprocessor, compiler, and assembler in sequence. **Step 2:** The linker combines `main.o` with the startup file and libraries: $$\texttt{arm-none-eabi-ld -T stm32f4.ld startup.o main.o -lc -lgcc -o firmware.elf}$$ **Role of the Linker Script (`stm32f4.ld`):** The linker script is a declarative text file that instructs the linker how to map logical **sections** of the program (`.text`, `.data`, `.bss`, `.isr_vector`) to physical **memory regions** of the target MCU. For the STM32F411: - **`.isr_vector`** → placed at start of FLASH (`0x08000000`), contains the vector table of interrupt handler addresses - **`.text`** → placed in FLASH, contains all program code - **`.data`** → placed in RAM but initialized from FLASH (copied at boot) - **`.bss`** → placed in RAM, zero-initialized by startup code **Step 3:** Convert to flashing format: $$\texttt{arm-none-eabi-objcopy -O ihex firmware.elf firmware.hex}$$ **Step 4:** Flash to target via ST-Link: $$\texttt{st-flash write firmware.hex 0x08000000}$$ **Step 5:** The ARM Cortex-M4 boots, reads the vector table from `0x08000000`, initializes `.data`/`.bss`, and jumps to `main()`. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Deductions:** > 1. Confusing the **assembler** (`.s → .o`) with the **linker** (`.o → .elf`) costs 1–2 marks. > 2. Forgetting to mention the **linker script** in part (b) costs 2 marks — it is a compulsory item. > 3. Writing `gcc` instead of `arm-none-eabi-gcc` in commands costs 1 mark per occurrence. > 4. Not specifying the **target ABI** (e.g., AAPCS) loses 1 mark. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2** `[KTU University Exam — Supplementary June 2024]` **(a)** Compare and contrast GCC, LLVM/Clang, and commercial cross compilers for embedded development. **\[7 marks\]** **(b)** Explain the concept of ABI and sysroot in cross compilation. How does the choice of C standard library (glibc vs newlib) affect the cross-compilation output? **\[7 marks\]** --- #### **Model Solution for Question B(a):** | Parameter | GNU GCC (arm-none-eabi-gcc) | LLVM/Clang (clang) | Commercial (ARM Compiler 6 / IAR) | |---|---|---|---| | **License** | GPL (open source) | Apache 2.0 + LLVM Exception (open source) | Commercial, per-seat license | | **Backend Optimization** | Excellent, mature RTL-based | Excellent, modern ML-based scheduling (in development) | Best-in-class for ARM (e.g., `-Omax`) | | **Code Size** | Good with `-Os` | Comparable to GCC | Often 10–20% smaller due to proprietary passes | | **Compile Speed** | Slower for large codebases | Significantly faster (1.5x–2x typical) | Moderate | | **Debug Info (DWARF)** | DWARF 4/5 | DWARF 4/5 | DWARF 3/4/5 | | **Standards Compliance** | Full C11/C17/C23 | Full C11/C17/C23, better C++20/23 support | Full C11/C17 | | **Ecosystem** | Largest, used by all RTOS vendors | Growing rapidly (Zephyr, Android, embedded ML) | Vendor-locked to specific MCU families | | **Certification** | None | None | ISO 26262, IEC 61508, DO-178C certified variants | | **Cost** | Free | Free | $1,000–$10,000+ per seat annually | **Conclusion:** GCC and Clang dominate the open-source embedded world. Commercial toolchains are preferred in **safety-critical** domains where certification is mandatory. #### **Model Solution for Question B(b):** **ABI (Application Binary Interface):** The ABI is the contract between compiled code and the target system, defining: - **Register usage conventions** (e.g., AAPCS: `r0-r3` for arguments, `r4-r11` for callee-saved, `r12` IP, `r13` SP, `r14` LR, `r15` PC) - **Stack frame layout** and alignment rules - **Function calling conventions** - **Object file format** (ELF, COFF) - **Data type sizes and endianness** (little-endian for ARM Cortex-M) **Sysroot:** The sysroot is a directory on the host that **mirrors the target's file system hierarchy**. When the cross compiler sees `#include `, it searches the sysroot (e.g., `/usr/local/arm-none-eabi/include`) rather than the host's `/usr/include`. This prevents accidental linking of host headers. **Effect of C Library Choice:** | Library | Target Use Case | Output Characteristics | |---|---|---| | **glibc** | Linux-based embedded (Raspberry Pi, industrial gateways) | Large (~1.5 MB), full POSIX support, dynamic linking | | **musl** | Alpine Linux, size-constrained Linux embedded | Small (~400 KB), static-friendly, full POSIX | | **newlib** | Bare-metal ARM/RISC-V microcontrollers | Tiny (~200 KB), minimal POSIX subset, requires custom `syscalls.c` | | **picolibc** | Ultra-low-power 8/16-bit MCUs | Smallest (~50 KB), minimal stdio support | > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall Callout — Examiner's Notes:** A very common error is students writing that "cross compilation uses glibc on the target." This is **wrong** for bare-metal targets. For bare-metal ARM, **newlib is the standard**. Glibc is only used when the embedded target runs **Linux** (e.g., Raspberry Pi, BeagleBone, NVIDIA Jetson). Deduct 2 marks for this confusion. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute KTU Board Revision — Memorize These 10 Points:** 1. **Definition:** Cross compiler = runs on *host*, produces code for *different target* (different ISA and/or OS). 2. **Toolchain Triple Format:** `[arch]-[vendor]-[os/abi]-` (e.g., `arm-none-eabi-`, `riscv64-unknown-elf-`, `avr-`). 3. **Four Compilation Stages:** Preprocess → Compile → Assemble → Link. All four run on the **host**. 4. **Linker Script:** Defines physical memory layout (FLASH base, RAM base, sizes) and section placement. Mandatory for embedded. 5. **Bare-metal Standard Library:** **Newlib** (not glibc) is used for ARM/RISC-V MCUs. Host's glibc cannot run on a target without an OS. 6. **Output Formats:** `.elf` (debugging), `.hex` (Intel HEX for flashing), `.bin` (raw binary), `.map` (linker map file). 7. **Static Linking:** Bare-metal cross-compiled firmware is **statically linked** because the target has no dynamic loader (`ld.so`). 8. **Thumb-2 Instructions:** Use `-mthumb` flag — generates denser 16-bit instructions, halving flash usage on Cortex-M. 9. **Debugging:** Uses **GDB + OpenOCD/J-Link** via the GDB Remote Serial Protocol over JTAG/SWD — *not* native GDB. 10. **Build Tools:** `make`, `CMake`, and `Ninja` are commonly used to automate cross-compilation in KTU lab assignments.

topics.title: Disassemblers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT631 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Disassemblers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Disassembler (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> A **disassembler** is a software utility (or hardware-assisted tool) that translates **machine code (object code / binary instructions)** back into its equivalent **assembly language mnemonics**. It performs the *inverse* of the assembly process — given a sequence of raw bytes representing CPU opcodes, it produces a human-readable listing of mnemonics, operands, and addresses.

In the context of **Embedded System Design**, a disassembler is classified as a *reverse-engineering / analysis tool* that operates on the final binary image of firmware (e.g., `.elf`, `.hex`, `.bin`, `.axf`) to recover symbolic information, instruction boundaries, and control flow.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "De-Baking" of a Cake

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — Reading a Recipe Backwards**

Imagine an **assembler** as a baker who meticulously converts a written **recipe** (assembly language) into a fully baked **cake** (machine code). The cake looks perfect, but if you never saw the recipe, can you reconstruct it just by looking at the finished cake?

A **disassembler** is the "culinary forensic scientist" who analyzes the cake layer by layer:
- Identifies which "ingredient" (opcode) is which.
- Determines the "amounts" (operands) of each ingredient.
- Reconstructs the **original recipe steps** (mnemonics).

However, unlike baking, a single "ingredient" in CPU code might *look* like code but actually be *data* (e.g., a lookup table), making the reconstruction **non-trivial and heuristic** in nature.

## 1.3 Why Disassemblers Matter in Embedded Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Disassemblers are the **first line of attack** in:
> 1. **Firmware reverse engineering** (when source code is unavailable).
> 2. **Security auditing** (finding hidden backdoors, malware analysis).
> 3. **Debugging optimized code** (understanding compiler-generated instructions).
> 4. **Legacy system maintenance** (analyzing firmware from obsolete controllers).
> 5. **Code size and performance analysis** (verifying compiler output).

## 1.4 The Fundamental Workflow Pipeline

$$\text{Source Code (C/C++)} \xrightarrow{\text{Compiler}} \text{Assembly (.s)} \xrightarrow{\text{Assembler}} \text{Object Code (.o)} \xrightarrow{\text{Linker}} \text{Binary (.elf)}$$

A disassembler operates on the final binary at the **right end** of this pipeline and walks *backwards* to recover the assembly.

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Distinction:** Disassembly ≠ Decompilation.
> - **Disassembler:** Binary → Assembly (low-level, accurate, 1-to-1 mapping).
> - **Decompiler:** Binary → High-level C-like pseudocode (lossy, heuristic, approximate).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — The 5-Stage Disassembly Process

A disassembler, regardless of its underlying algorithm, performs the following logical sequence:

1. **Binary Ingestion:** Loads the executable file (`.elf`, `.hex`, `.bin`) and parses section headers, entry points, and symbol tables (if present with debug info).
2. **Architecture Detection:** Identifies the target ISA (ARM, AVR, PIC, x86, RISC-V) from header flags.
3. **Entry Point Resolution:** Locates the reset vector / start address (e.g., `0x08000000` for STM32, `0x00000000` for AVR).
4. **Instruction Decoding:** Sequentially or recursively decodes each byte sequence into mnemonic + operand form.
5. **Symbolic Annotation:** Cross-references addresses, identifies functions (heuristic), labels branch targets, and formats the output listing.

## 2.2 The Two Fundamental Disassembler Algorithms

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Favorite — This is a guaranteed question type!**

### Algorithm A: Linear Sweep Disassembler

**Definition:** Decodes instructions sequentially from a known start address, advancing by the size of the previously decoded instruction.

**Operational Steps:**
- Begin at the entry point address $A_0$.
- Decode the first instruction of size $S_0$ bytes.
- Move to address $A_1 = A_0 + S_0$.
- Repeat until end of section or invalid opcode is encountered.

**Mathematical Model:**

$$A_{n+1} = A_n + S(I_n)$$

where $S(I_n)$ is the byte-size of the $n$-th decoded instruction.

**Examples:** `objdump -d`, `ndisasm`, basic `avr-objdump`.

**Limitation:** Cannot distinguish **embedded data** (jump tables, constant pools) from code — will mis-decode data as garbage instructions.

### Algorithm B: Recursive Descent Disassembler

**Definition:** Follows the **control flow graph (CFG)** of the program, decoding only addresses that are reachable via branches, jumps, or function calls.

**Operational Steps:**
- Start at the entry point.
- Decode the instruction at the current address.
- If instruction is a **branch / call / jump**, decode the target address next.
- Maintain a **work-list** of pending addresses.
- Continue until work-list is empty.

**Mathematical Model:**

$$W = \{A_0\} \cup \bigcup_{I \in \text{Decoded}} \text{Targets}(I)$$

where $W$ is the work-list and $\text{Targets}(I)$ returns branch target addresses of instruction $I$.

**Examples:** IDA Pro, Ghidra, Binary Ninja, Radare2 (Cutter).

**Advantage:** Correctly skips over data regions when control flow doesn't reach them.

## 2.3 The Decoding Challenge — Variable Instruction Length

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Concept for KTU:** Modern ISAs use **fixed-length** instructions (ARM = 4 bytes, RISC-V = 4 bytes), but legacy and CISC ISAs (x86, 8051) use **variable-length** instructions (1–15 bytes for x86). This directly affects disassembly complexity.

**For fixed-length ISA (ARM):**

$$S(I) = \text{constant} = 4 \text{ bytes (ARM mode)}, \quad 2 \text{ bytes (Thumb mode)}$$

**For variable-length ISA (x86):**

$$S(I) = f(\text{opcode}, \text{ModR/M}, \text{SIB}, \text{displacement}, \text{immediate})$$

$$S(I) \in [1, 15] \text{ bytes}$$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Comparison Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Linear Sweep | Recursive Descent |
|---|---|---|
| **Traversal Strategy** | Sequential, address-ordered | Control-flow following |
| **Data Mis-identification** | High (treats data as code) | Low (skips unreachable data) |
| **Indirect Branch Handling** | Ignores them | Adds to work-list, may stall |
| **Output Completeness** | Complete byte coverage | May miss dead code |
| **Speed** | **Fast** (O(n)) | Slower (graph traversal) |
| **Typical Users** | `objdump`, `ndisasm` | IDA Pro, Ghidra, Binary Ninja |
| **KTU Exam Weight** | 7 marks (Algorithm only) | 7 marks (Algorithm only) |

| Address Mode | Branch Target Formula | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **PC-relative (ARM)** | $\text{Target} = \text{PC} + 8 + \text{Offset}$ | Conditional `B` / Unconditional `B` |
| **Absolute (Thumb-2)** | $\text{Target} = \text{Offset}_{32}$ | Long branch `B.W` |
| **Register-indirect** | $\text{Target} = \text{Reg}$ | Computed jump (jumptable) |
| **Thumb BL offset** | $\text{Target} = \text{PC} + 4 + (\text{Offset} \ll 12)$ | Function call `BL label` |

## 2.5 Real-World Embedded Engineering Utility

- **Automotive ECUs:** Reverse-engineering vendor firmware for ISO 26262 compliance audits.
- **IoT Devices:** Auditing third-party firmware blobs for security vulnerabilities (CVE analysis).
- **Industrial PLCs:** Maintaining legacy ladder-logic-to-assembly mappings.
- **Bootloader Development:** Verifying that bootloader code resides in protected regions (e.g., STM32 Option Bytes).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Disassembling an ARM Cortex-M Instruction Stream

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** A bare-metal embedded firmware's `.elf` file shows a function at address `0x08000100`. The raw bytes starting at that address are:
>
> `10 2A 00 F0 0F 00 00 E0 00 21 23 60 ...`
>
> Goal: Disassemble this byte stream using a **Linear Sweep** approach (ARM Cortex-M0, Thumb-2 mode, little-endian).

### Step 1 — Identify Endianness and Architecture

> **Logic:** ARM Cortex-M is a **Thumb-only** core, meaning every instruction is **2 bytes (Thumb)** or **4 bytes (Thumb-2)**. Bytes are stored in **little-endian** order, so the byte sequence `10 2A` represents the 16-bit value `0x2A10`.

**Conversion:**

$$I_0 = (\text{Byte}_0) + (\text{Byte}_1 \times 256) = 0x10 + (0x2A \times 256) = 0x2A10$$

### Step 2 — Decode the First Instruction

> **Logic:** Look up opcode `0x2A10` in the ARMv6-M Architecture Reference Manual.

The bit pattern `0010 1010 0001 0000` decodes to:

$$\text{MOVS} \quad R0, \ \#0$$ 

This is a 2-byte Thumb instruction: *Move the immediate value 0 into register R0*.

### Step 3 — Advance the Disassembler Pointer (Linear Sweep)

$$A_1 = A_0 + S(I_0) = \text{0x08000100} + 2 = \text{0x08000102}$$

The disassembler's *cursor* moves to the next 2 bytes: `00 F0`.

### Step 4 — Decode the Second Instruction

$$I_1 = 0x00 + (0xF0 \times 256) = 0xF000$$

> **Logic:** `0xF000` is the 16-bit half of a **Thumb-2 32-bit instruction**. We must read the *next* 2 bytes to form the full 32-bit opcode.

$$I_1 = 0xF0000F00$$ 

*(combined from bytes 2-5: `00 F0 0F 00`)*

This decodes to:

$$\text{BEQ} \quad \text{+24 (relative forward branch)}$$

> **Logic:** The full 32-bit `B.cond` (Branch Conditional) instruction in Thumb-2.

### Step 5 — Compute the Branch Target (ARM Branch Target Arithmetic)

> **Logic:** For Thumb-2 conditional branches, the PC value used is `PC + 4` (where PC is the address of the current branch), and the immediate is sign-extended then shifted left by 1.

The 32-bit opcode `0xF0000F00` has the immediate field `imm32` extracted as follows (per ARM ARM §A8.7.49):

$$\text{imm32} = \text{SignExtend}(0x000F00_{19..0}) \times 2 = 0x1E \times 2 = 0x3C$$

Wait — let me re-derive carefully for the actual standard `B.cond` encoding:

$$\text{Target} = \text{PC} + 4 + \text{imm32}$$

Given:
- Current instruction address: $A_1 = 0x08000102$ 
- The branch occupies 4 bytes, so $A_1$ refers to the *start* of the 4-byte instruction.
- PC read value (for ARM) = $A_1 + 4 = 0x08000106$
- For Thumb, the offset shift is already baked in: $\text{imm} = +0x1E$ (relative units, scaled by 2 in hardware).

$$\text{Target} = 0x08000106 + 0x1E \times 2 = 0x08000106 + 0x3C = 0x08000142$$

### Step 6 — Advance and Decode the Third Instruction

$$A_2 = A_1 + S(I_1) = 0x08000102 + 4 = 0x08000106$$

Next bytes: `00 21 23 60`

$$I_2 = (0x00 + 0x21 \times 256) = 0x2100 \quad \text{(low half)}$$ 
$$I_2 = (0x23 + 0x60 \times 256) = 0x6023 \quad \text{(high half)}$$

Combined 32-bit opcode: `0x60232100` which decodes to:

$$\text{STR} \quad R0, \ [R1, \ \#0x00]$$

*Store register R0 to the memory address pointed to by R1 (offset 0).*

### Step 7 — Final Disassembled Listing

> **Logic:** Continue this process recursively for the `BEQ` target, and sequentially for fall-through code.

| Address | Bytes | Mnemonic | Operand | Comment |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `0x08000100` | `10 2A` | `MOVS` | `R0, #0` | Initialize R0 = 0 |
| `0x08000102` | `00 F0 0F 00` | `BEQ` | `+0x3C` | Branch if equal to `0x08000142` |
| `0x08000106` | `00 21 23 60` | `STR` | `R0, [R1]` | Store R0 to *R1 |
| `...` | `...` | `...` | `...` | (continued by disassembler) |

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Disassembler Selection Decision Tree

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** This matrix appears frequently in viva voce and 7-mark questions.

| Scenario | Recommended Disassembler | Algorithm | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Quick CLI disassembly of `.elf` | `arm-none-eabi-objdump -d` | Linear Sweep | Fast, no GUI needed, embedded in GCC toolchain |
| Reverse engineering unknown firmware | **Ghidra** (NSA open-source) | Recursive Descent | Free, decompiler included, function detection |
| Commercial / professional security audit | **IDA Pro** | Recursive Descent | Industry gold standard, rich plugin ecosystem |
| Scriptable batch analysis | **Radare2 / Cutter** | Recursive Descent | Open-source, fully CLI-scriptable |
| Decoding raw `.bin` (no headers) | `ndisasm` (NASM) | Linear Sweep | Auto-detects mode, no symbols |
| Analyzing AVR (Atmel) firmware | `avr-objdump` | Linear Sweep | Architecture-specific, knows AVR ISA |

## 3.3 Exhaustive Comparison: Disassembler vs Decompiler vs Debugger

| Feature | Disassembler | Decompiler | Debugger |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Input** | Binary | Binary | Binary (live process) |
| **Output** | Assembly mnemonics | Pseudo-C code | Live register/memory state |
| **Runs Code?** | ❌ No (static) | ❌ No (static) | ✅ Yes (dynamic) |
| **Accuracy** | 100% (1:1 mapping) | ~70% (heuristic) | 100% (runtime) |
| **Round-trip Safe?** | ✅ Yes (asm → bin → asm) | ❌ No (lossy) | N/A |
| **KTU Board Frequency** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐ |
| **Use Case** | Static firmware analysis | High-level reverse eng. | Step-through execution |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Disassembler Operational Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Binary Firmware Image] --> B[File Format Parser ELF HEX BIN]
    B --> C[Architecture Detection ARM AVR x86 RISC-V]
    C --> D[Section Loader Code Data BSS]
    D --> E[Algorithm Selector]
    E --> F[Linear Sweep Decoder]
    E --> G[Recursive Descent Decoder]
    F --> H[Instruction Decoder Opcode Operand Resolution]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Control Flow Graph Builder]
    I --> J[Cross Reference Engine]
    J --> K[Symbol Annotation Function Names Labels]
    K --> L[Assembly Listing Output]
    L --> M[User Reviewer / Reverse Engineer]
```

## 4.2 Linear Sweep vs Recursive Descent — Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Start([Start: Load Binary]) --> Init[Initialize Work-List W with Entry Point A0]
    Init --> Check{W empty?}
    Check -- No --> Pop[Pop address An from W]
    Pop --> Decode[Decode instruction at An]
    Decode --> Size[Determine size S In]
    Size --> LS{Are we in Linear Sweep mode?}
    LS -- Yes --> Advance[An+1 = An + S In]
    LS -- No --> RD[Recursive Descent Mode]
    RD --> Branch{Is instruction a branch or call?}
    Branch -- Yes --> AddTarget[Add target address to W]
    Branch -- No --> FallThrough[Add An+1 to W]
    AddTarget --> Check
    FallThrough --> Check
    Advance --> Check
    Check -- Yes --> Output[Output formatted assembly listing]
    Output --> End([End])
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Disassembler Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Input[Input Stage]
        I1[Raw Binary Bytes] --> I2[Endianness Detection]
        I2 --> I3[ISA Identification]
    end
    
    subgraph Decode[Decoding Stage]
        I3 --> D1[Opcode Table Lookup]
        D1 --> D2[Operand Field Extractor]
        D2 --> D3[Immediate Value Resolver]
    end
    
    subgraph Analysis[Analysis Stage]
        D3 --> AN1[Branch Target Calculator]
        AN1 --> AN2[Function Boundary Detector]
        AN2 --> AN3[Data vs Code Classifier]
    end
    
    subgraph Output[Output Stage]
        AN3 --> O1[Address Label Generator]
        O1 --> O2[Xref Cross-Reference Mapper]
        O2 --> O3[Assembly Text Formatter]
        O3 --> O4[Human Readable Listing]
    end
```

## 4.4 Memory Layout — How a Disassembler Sees the Firmware

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Flash[Embedded Flash Memory 0x08000000]
        F1[0x08000000: Reset Vector 4 bytes]
        F2[0x08000004: Interrupt Vector Table]
        F3[0x08000100: main function code]
        F4[0x08000200: Lookup Table DATA]
        F5[0x08000300: ISR Handlers]
    end
    
    subgraph Decoded[Disassembler Output View]
        V1[Recognized as code, decoded as instructions]
        V2[Recognized as data, listed as DC.B directives]
        V3[Recognized as code, recursive descent follows]
    end
    
    F1 -.-> V1
    F3 -.-> V1
    F4 -.-> V2
    F5 -.-> V3
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question Pattern]**

### **Q1. Define a disassembler. How is it different from a decompiler?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **disassembler** is a software tool that translates machine code (binary instructions) into equivalent assembly language mnemonics on a one-to-one basis. It performs the inverse operation of an assembler and is used primarily for static analysis of firmware when source code is unavailable.

**Key Differences (1 mark each):**

| Aspect | Disassembler | Decompiler |
|---|---|---|
| Output | Assembly mnemonics | High-level pseudo-C code |
| Mapping | **1:1 (lossless)** | Many-to-one (lossy, heuristic) |
| Accuracy | 100% accurate | Approximate, requires manual review |
| Use | Debugging, security audit | High-level reverse engineering |

---

### **Q2. List any three popular disassemblers used in embedded system development. State one distinguishing feature of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **`arm-none-eabi-objdump`** — Open-source, part of GNU ARM Toolchain; uses **linear sweep** algorithm. Best for quick command-line disassembly of `.elf` files.
2. **Ghidra (NSA)** — Free and open-source; combines **recursive descent disassembler + decompiler**. Best for full-scale reverse engineering without licensing costs.
3. **IDA Pro** — Industry standard commercial tool; uses **recursive descent** with rich plugin architecture (Hex-Rays). Best for professional security audits.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question with Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Linear Sweep Disassembler Deep Dive*

**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model 2024 Scheme Pattern]`**

**(a)** Explain the **Linear Sweep** disassembler algorithm with a suitable diagram. Discuss its **operational steps**, the **mathematical model** for pointer advancement, and **two major limitations** when applied to ARM firmware. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given the following ARM Cortex-M0 (Thumb mode) instruction stream starting at address `0x08000010`:

| Address | Byte Sequence (hex) |
|---|---|
| `0x08000010` | `00 20` |
| `0x08000012` | `01 1C` |
| `0x08000014` | `FF F7 F4 FF` |
| `0x08000018` | `FE E7` |

**Disassemble the above byte stream** and explain each decoded instruction. Compute the **branch target** of the `BL` instruction. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part A(a) [7 marks]**

**Linear Sweep Algorithm Explanation (4 marks):**

The Linear Sweep algorithm is a **sequential decoding** method used by disassemblers such as `objdump` and `ndisasm`. The operational steps are:

1. **Initialize** the current address $A_0$ to the entry point of the program (e.g., reset vector for embedded systems).
2. **Fetch** the byte(s) at the current address.
3. **Decode** the instruction by matching the opcode against the ISA's opcode table.
4. **Determine** the instruction size $S(I_n)$ in bytes.
5. **Advance** the pointer to $A_{n+1} = A_n + S(I_n)$.
6. **Repeat** steps 2–5 until the end of the code section is reached.

**Mathematical Model (1 mark):**

$$A_{n+1} = A_n + S(I_n)$$

This forms a simple linear recurrence — the disassembler's "cursor" moves forward by a fixed or variable amount depending on the decoded instruction.

**Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to the Linear Sweep branch of the flowchart in Section 4.2.

**Two Major Limitations on ARM Firmware (1 mark):**

1. **Data-in-Code Mis-identification:** ARM compilers embed **constant pools** (e.g., 32-bit floating-point constants, jump table addresses) inline within the code section. A linear sweep disassembler will mis-decode these as bogus instructions (e.g., `DCI 0x12345678`), producing misleading listings.
2. **No Branch Target Following:** Linear sweep cannot identify function boundaries automatically; it treats all bytes as code sequentially, missing the natural CFG structure of the program.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part A(b) [7 marks]**

**Disassembly Table (5 marks):**

| Address | Bytes | Mnemonic | Operands | Explanation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `0x08000010` | `00 20` | `MOVS` | `R0, #0` | Load immediate 0 into R0 (`0x2000` = `MOVS R0, #0`) |
| `0x08000012` | `01 1C` | `ADDS` | `R1, R0, #0` | Add R0 to R1; effectively `MOV R1, R0` |
| `0x08000014` | `FF F7 F4 FF` | `BL` | `<label>` | Branch with Link — call a subroutine |
| `0x08000018` | `FE E7` | `B` | `.` (infinite loop) | Branch to self — `0xE7FE` = `B .` |

**Branch Target Calculation for `BL` (2 marks):**

> **Logic:** The 32-bit `BL` (Branch with Link) Thumb-2 instruction encodes a signed 23-bit immediate `imm32`, which is sign-extended and shifted left by 1. The target is computed as:

$$\text{Target} = \text{PC} + 4 + \text{imm32}$$

For ARM Thumb-2 BL, the encoded immediate in `0xFFF7F4FF` is `imm32 = 0xFFFFEC` (sign-extended, scaled). PC value used = $A + 4$ where $A$ is the address of the `BL` instruction.

$$\text{PC} = 0x08000014 + 4 = 0x08000018$$
$$\text{Target} = 0x08000018 + (\text{sign-extended imm} \times 2) = 0x08000018 + (-0x18) = 0x08000000$$

> **Result:** The `BL` calls the function at `0x08000000` (likely the `SystemInit()` or `Reset_Handler`).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Recursive Descent Disassembler + Practical Application*

**`[KTU University Exam — May 2024 / Model 2024 Scheme Pattern]`**

**(a)** Explain the **Recursive Descent** disassembler algorithm. How does it differ from the Linear Sweep algorithm? Construct a **work-list based pseudocode** (or flow) for the same. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A reverse engineer discovers a suspicious firmware blob at `0x08000000` for an STM32F4 (Cortex-M4, Thumb-2). The first 16 bytes are:

`00 48 00 47 00 C0 9F E5 1C 30 9F E5 00 00 00 00`

Using a **Recursive Descent** approach, identify all **reachable code** and **branch targets**. Comment on how this differs from a Linear Sweep output. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part B(a) [7 marks]**

**Recursive Descent Algorithm Explanation (3 marks):**

The **Recursive Descent** algorithm traverses the program by following its **control flow graph (CFG)**. Unlike linear sweep, it does not blindly decode sequential bytes; instead, it maintains a **work-list of addresses** to decode, prioritizing branch targets and call destinations.

**Key Difference from Linear Sweep (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Linear Sweep | Recursive Descent |
|---|---|---|
| Traversal | Sequential (address-ordered) | Graph-based (control-flow ordered) |
| Data Handling | Decodes everything as code | Skips unreachable data |
| Branch Handling | Treats branches as data | Actively follows branch targets |
| Output | May contain "garbage" instructions | Cleaner, semantically accurate |

**Pseudocode / Flow (2 marks):**

```
function RecursiveDisassemble(entryPoint):
    W = empty work-list
    push W with entryPoint
    
    while W is not empty:
        A = pop from W
        if A already decoded:
            continue
        I = decode instruction at A
        output I with address A
        S = size of I
        
        if I is BRANCH (B, BEQ, BNE, etc.):
            Target = compute target from I
            push W with Target
            
        if I is CALL (BL, BLX):
            Target = compute target from I
            push W with Target
            push W with (A + S)   // save return address
            
        if I is RETURN (BX LR, POP {PC}):
            do not advance (control flow continues from caller)
        else:
            push W with (A + S)   // fall-through
```

---

#### **Model Solution — Part B(b) [7 marks]**

**Disassembly Table (5 marks):**

| Address | Bytes | Mnemonic | Operand | Comment |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `0x08000000` | `00 48` | `LDR` | `R0, [PC, #0]` | Load from PC-relative address |
| `0x08000002` | `00 47` | `BX` | `R0` | Branch to address in R0 (indirect!) |
| `0x08000004` | `00 C0 9F E5` | `LDR` | `R12, [PC, #imm]` | Load R12 from constant pool |
| `0x08000008` | `1C 30 9F E5` | `LDR` | `R3, [PC, #imm]` | Load R3 from constant pool |
| `0x0800000C` | `00 00 00 00` | `DCD` | `0x00000000` | **Data word** (zero) — skipped by RD |

**Recursive Descent Analysis (1 mark):**

- Starts at `0x08000000`, decodes `LDR R0, [PC, #0]`.
- Decodes `BX R0` — **indirect branch**, target is *unknown statically*. The disassembler adds `R0`'s value to the work-list once resolved from the literal pool at `0x08000004`.
- The bytes at `0x08000004`–`0x0800000B` decode as two more `LDR` instructions loading R12 and R3 from the same constant pool region.
- The constant `0x00000000` at `0x0800000C` is correctly identified as a **data word** because no branch leads to it directly.

**Comparison with Linear Sweep (1 mark):**

A **Linear Sweep** disassembler would:
- Decode the `LDR` at `0x08000000` ✓ (correct)
- Decode the `BX R0` at `0x08000002` ✓ (correct)
- Decode the `0xC09FE500` value at `0x08000004` as a **bogus instruction** like `LDRB R4, [R5, R3, ROR #1]` or similar garbage.
- The `0x00000000` at `0x0800000C` would be mis-decode as `MOVS R0, R0` (a no-op), which is misleading.
- The disassembler would **never know** that `0x08000000` is actually a function entry point without manual annotation.

> **Conclusion:** Recursive descent produces **cleaner, more accurate** disassembly for ARM firmware containing literal pools and indirect branches.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Assembler vs Disassembler:** Students often write "assembler converts assembly to binary" but forget that **disassembler is the EXACT reverse**. Always state both directions explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting Endianness:** In ARM disassembly examples, students decode bytes in the wrong order. Remember: **little-endian** means lowest byte first in memory; combine as `Byte0 + (Byte1 × 256)`.
> 3. **Linear Sweep = "Stupid" Decoding:** Examiners award 0 marks for "Linear Sweep is faster so it's better." Acknowledge the **trade-off** — it sacrifices accuracy for speed.
> 4. **Not Drawing CFG:** In 7-mark algorithm questions, always include a **diagram or pseudocode**. Pure text-only answers get capped at 4–5 marks.
> 5. **Skipping Branch Arithmetic:** For the `BL` / `B` target calculation, **show the formula explicitly**: `Target = PC + 4 + imm32` (for Thumb-2). Examiners check for the `+4` PC offset.
> 6. **Mixing up Decompiler and Disassembler:** A 14-mark question asking "explain disassembly" should NOT contain phrases like "the output is high-level C code."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Print This Section Before Exam!**

### 🔑 Core Concepts
- **Disassembler** = Binary → Assembly (lossless, 1-to-1, accurate).
- **Decompiler** = Binary → High-level pseudocode (lossy, heuristic).
- **Debugger** = Binary → Live runtime state (dynamic, requires target hardware).

### 🔑 The Two Algorithms
- **Linear Sweep:** $A_{n+1} = A_n + S(I_n)$ — fast, blind, mis-decodes data.
- **Recursive Descent:** Uses work-list $W$ of branch targets — accurate, slower, follows CFG.

### 🔑 ARM-Specific Disassembly Rules
- **Cortex-M = Thumb-only** (2-byte or 4-byte instructions).
- **Little-endian** byte order: combine as `Byte0 + (Byte1 × 256)`.
- **Branch target formula:** $\text{Target} = \text{PC} + 4 + \text{imm32}$ (Thumb-2).
- **Constant pools** = data embedded in code section — a major disassembly challenge.

### 🔑 Popular Tools (Recall for 3-mark tool questions)
- `arm-none-eabi-objdump -d file.elf` (GNU ARM, CLI)
- **Ghidra** (NSA, free, decompiler included)
- **IDA Pro** (commercial, industry standard)
- **Radare2 / Cutter** (open-source, scriptable)
- `ndisasm` (NASM, raw binary, no headers)

### 🔑 KTU Board Frequently Asked Patterns
- **"Explain Linear Sweep with example"** → 7 marks → must include formula + limitations.
- **"Differentiate Linear Sweep and Recursive Descent"** → 7 marks → use a comparison table.
- **"Decode the given byte stream"** → 7 marks → show byte combining, mnemonic lookup, branch arithmetic.
- **"List disassemblers used in embedded systems"** → 3 marks → name 3 tools with 1-line use case each.

### 🔑 Mnemonic Aids
- **"L**inear = **L**ogical sequence, **L**acks intelligence" (vs Recursive = **R**eal CFG aware)
- **"BX R0"** = "Branch to whatever R0 holds" = indirect branch = **work-list stall**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Decompilers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT631 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Decompilers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Decompiler** (also known as a *reverse compiler*, *disassembler-decompiler hybrid*, or *source code recovery tool*) is a specialized software utility that translates an **executable binary program** (machine code / object code / firmware image) back into a **higher-level programming language representation** — typically C, C++, or a structured pseudo-code. In the context of **Embedded System Design (PEBMT631)**, decompilers are categorized under *Reverse Engineering (RE) tools* and are critical for firmware analysis, legacy code recovery, security auditing, and Intellectual Property (IP) protection verification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEBMT631 - Module 3):**
> A *decompiler* is a programming tool that performs the **inverse operation of a compiler**. Where a compiler translates high-level source code into low-level machine code, a decompiler attempts to reconstruct readable, higher-level source code from the compiled binary executable. It is widely used in embedded firmware reverse engineering, vulnerability discovery (CVE analysis), and post-mortem analysis of legacy microcontroller firmware.

### Formal Symbolic Representation

Let the compiler mapping function be:

$$C : S \longrightarrow B$$

where $S$ represents the *Source Code* (high-level language) and $B$ represents the *Binary Executable* (machine code). Then the decompiler mapping function is its logical inverse:

$$D : B \longrightarrow S' \approx S$$

where $S'$ is an *approximate reconstruction* of the original source $S$. The approximation operator ($\approx$) is critical because **decompilation is a lossy, heuristic-driven process** — not all original information (variable names, comments, original types) is preserved during compilation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the Approximation ($\approx$) Operator Matters:**
> Compilation discards *semantic metadata* such as:
> - Original variable and function names (replaced by symbols or offsets)
> - Comments and documentation strings
> - High-level data type distinctions (e.g., `int` vs `long` — both become generic 32-bit registers)
> - Original source-level control structures (e.g., `for`, `while`, `switch` — all lowered to `goto`/branch instructions)
>
> Therefore, $D$ is **non-deterministic** and **lossy**, whereas $C$ is generally deterministic.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you bake a **complex chocolate cake** (the compiled binary). A compiler is like a chef who follows a written recipe (source code) to bake the cake — transforming readable instructions into a finished product.

A **decompiler** is like a food scientist who, when given only the finished cake, tries to **reverse-engineer the original recipe**. The scientist can determine:
- The cake contains flour, sugar, eggs, and cocoa (equivalent to identifying *opcodes* and *operands*)
- The cake was probably baked at 180°C for 30 minutes (equivalent to identifying *calling conventions* and *register usage*)

However, the scientist **cannot** recover:
- The exact brand of flour used
- The original recipe author's secret ingredient ratios
- The handwritten notes the chef scribbled in the recipe margin

> [!TIP]
> **Embedded Systems Analogy — The Black Box Recorder:**
> Think of a deployed embedded device (e.g., a car's Engine Control Unit, a smart thermostat, a medical infusion pump) as an aircraft's *black box*. The firmware binary inside is the recorded data. A decompiler is the forensic tool investigators use to "listen" to what the device was doing. This is exactly the workflow used in **IoT security audits** and **automotive cybersecurity** (per ISO 21434 standard).

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

| Metric | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Decompilation Accuracy** | **60% – 85%** | Typical recovery rate for well-structured C code |
| **Information Loss Bound** | **~30%** | Minimum metadata lost during `-O2` optimization |
| **RISC-V Decompilation Latency** | **2 – 8 seconds** | For 100 KB firmware images (Ghidra benchmark) |
| **ARM Cortex-M Decompilation Time** | **< 1 second/MB** | Industry standard for embedded MCU RE |

> [!NOTE]
> These metrics are **representative industry benchmarks**, not exam-critical. They are included for contextual industrial awareness, which is a KTU 2024 Scheme graduate attribute (*Engineering Knowledge* and *Life-long Learning*).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Decomposition of a Decompiler

A production-grade decompiler (e.g., **Ghidra**, **IDA Pro + Hex-Rays**, **Binary Ninja**, **RetDec**) operates as a multi-stage pipeline. Each stage transforms the input binary into progressively higher-level representations.

### Stage-by-Stage Logic

1. **Binary Loading (File I/O Stage)**
   - Parses ELF (Executable and Linkable Format), PE (Portable Executable), COFF, or raw flat binary (`.bin` for bare-metal MCUs).
   - Extracts *section headers*, *symbol tables*, *relocation entries*.
   - For embedded targets: identifies **memory-mapped regions** (e.g., `.text` at `0x08000000` for STM32).

2. **Disassembly (Machine Code → Assembly)**
   - Decodes raw bytes into human-readable **assembly mnemonics** (e.g., `MOV R0, #1`, `BNE 0x200`).
   - Performs *linear sweep* or *recursive traversal* disassembly.
   - Resolves *cross-references (XREFs)* between functions and data.

3. **Intermediate Representation (IR) Generation**
   - Translates assembly into a **platform-independent IR** (e.g., Ghidra's *P-code*, LLVM IR).
   - IR normalizes register names, calling conventions, and instruction semantics.
   - This is the **lossy boundary** — semantic information begins collapsing here.

4. **Data Flow Analysis (DFA)**
   - Constructs *Use-Def chains* and *Def-Use chains*.
   - Performs **liveness analysis**, **reaching definitions**, and **constant propagation**.
   - Identifies stack frame layouts, local variable boundaries, and parameter passing.

5. **Control Flow Analysis (CFA)**
   - Builds the **Control Flow Graph (CFG)** — a directed graph $G = (V, E)$ where:
     - $V$ = basic blocks (maximal straight-line instruction sequences)
     - $E$ = control transfer edges (branches, calls, returns)
   - Identifies loop headers, dominators, and natural loop structures.

6. **Type Recovery & Variable Reconstruction**
   - Infers high-level data types from instruction patterns and memory access sizes.
   - Reconstructs local variables from stack slot analyses.
   - Resolves function signatures and parameter types.

7. **High-Level Code Generation (AST → C Source)**
   - Maps IR primitives back to C-like constructs: `for`, `while`, `if-else`, `switch-case`.
   - Emits the final pseudo-C source file with recovered identifiers (e.g., `FUN_0800042a`).

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Stage

| Stage | Why it Exists | How it Works (Mechanism) |
|---|---|---|
| **Disassembly** | Compiled binaries are opaque byte streams | Pattern-matching against ISA encoding tables |
| **IR Generation** | Decouples architecture specifics from analysis logic | SSA-form translation, register virtualisation |
| **DFA** | Identifies *what values flow where* | Fixed-point iteration over data dependencies |
| **CFA** | Recovers *structured control flow* from raw jumps | Dominator tree computation, loop detection |
| **Type Recovery** | Original types were erased during compilation | Heuristic inference from access widths and API patterns |
| **Code Gen** | Final human-readable output | Pattern matching IR to C templates |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Notation Safeguard:** All absolute values, set notations, and conditions use `\vert` (LaTeX vertical bar) instead of the raw `|` pipe to preserve markdown table integrity.

| Symbol / Concept | Mathematical Form | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Compiler Function** | $C : S \rightarrow B$ | Maps source $S$ to binary $B$ |
| **Decompiler Function** | $D : B \rightarrow S' \approx S$ | Maps binary $B$ to approximate source $S'$ |
| **Composition Identity** | $D \circ C \neq I$ | Decompiling a compiled program does **not** yield the original source |
| **CFG Vertices** | $V = \{v_1, v_2, \dots, v_n\}$ | Basic blocks of the function |
| **CFG Edges** | $E \subseteq V \times V$ | Control transfer relationships |
| **Cyclomatic Complexity** | $M = E - V + 2P$ | Measures CFG complexity; $P$ = connected components |
| **Information Loss** | $L = 1 - \frac{\vert S' \cap S \vert}{\vert S \vert}$ | Fractional information lost; $0 \le L \le 1$ |
| **Decompilation Quality** | $Q = 1 - L$ | Higher $Q$ means better recovery; $Q \in [0, 1]$ |
| **Optimization Level Effect** | $\frac{\partial Q}{\partial O} < 0$ | Higher compiler optimization $O$ reduces quality $Q$ |
| **Stack Frame Size** | $F_{size} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{sizeof}(v_i)$ | Total bytes for recovered local variables $v_i$ |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Decompilers are indispensable in the following embedded systems engineering domains:

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Application Domains (KTU 2024 Graduate Attribute Mapping):**
>
> 1. **IoT Firmware Security Auditing** — Analyzing third-party device firmware for known CVEs (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures). Tools: *Ghidra*, *Binwalk* (for unpacking), *Firmware Analysis Toolkit (FAT)*.
> 2. **Automotive Cybersecurity (ISO/SAE 21434)** — Reverse engineering ECUs (Engine Control Units) to verify UN R155 compliance.
> 3. **Medical Device Forensics** — FDA post-market surveillance requires firmware analysis for malfunctioning devices.
> 4. **Legacy System Migration** — Recovering source code from 20+ year-old industrial PLC firmware when original developers are unavailable.
> 5. **Competitive IP Analysis** — Verifying that a competitor's device firmware does not infringe on proprietary algorithms (within legal bounds).
> 6. **Malware Analysis (Embedded Ransomware)** — Analyzing malicious firmware implants in industrial control systems (ICS/SCADA).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Decompilation Pipeline Formalism

The following is a **rigorous, step-by-step mathematical derivation** of the decompilation process — exactly the kind of analytical treatment expected in KTU 2024 Scheme university examinations for 14-mark questions.

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

> **Given:** A stripped ARM Cortex-M firmware binary $B$ located at base address $\beta = \text{0x08000000}$, containing a function $f$ whose entry point is at offset $\alpha = \text{0x0800042A}$.
>
> **Objective:** Reconstruct a C-equivalent source representation $S'_f$ of the function $f$.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Binary Loading and Memory Mapping**

The decompiler first parses the ELF section headers to identify the executable code region. For an STM32 firmware:

$$
\text{Load} : B \longrightarrow \mathcal{M}
$$

where $\mathcal{M}$ is the *virtual memory map* defined as:

$$
\mathcal{M} = \{ m \vert m_i = \langle a_i, b_i, t_i \rangle,\ a_i \in \mathbb{N},\ t_i \in \{\text{code, data, rodata, bss}\} \}
$$

Here, $a_i$ is the starting virtual address, $b_i$ is the byte content, and $t_i$ is the section type.

> **Explanation of conversion logic:** The binary $B$ is sliced into its constituent memory regions. The `.text` section (containing code) is loaded into the *code analysis buffer*, while `.data` and `.bss` are loaded into the *data analysis buffer*. This separation is critical for embedded MCUs because `.bss` (Block Started by Symbol) is zero-initialized RAM and contains no recoverable code.

**Step 2 — Disassembly**

For the code region bytes $b_{\text{code}}$, the disassembler applies the ARM Thumb-2 instruction decoding function:

$$
D_{\text{asm}} : b_{\text{code}} \longrightarrow \mathcal{A}
$$

where $\mathcal{A}$ is the set of assembly tuples:

$$
\mathcal{A} = \{ a_i \vert a_i = \langle \text{op}, \text{rd}, \text{rs}_1, \text{rs}_2, \text{imm} \rangle \}
$$

For example, bytes at offset $\alpha = \text{0x0800042A}$:

$$
b_{\text{code}} = \text{0x4A F2 6A 50}
$$

are decoded to:

$$
\mathcal{A}_f = \{ \langle \text{MOV}, R0, \text{undef}, \text{undef}, \text{0x123} \rangle, \dots \}
$$

> **Explanation of conversion logic:** Each 2-byte or 4-byte Thumb-2 word is looked up in the ARM instruction encoding table. The opcode field is extracted, register fields are mapped to architectural names (R0–R12, SP, LR, PC), and the immediate field is sign-extended. This is a *purely deterministic* transformation — no information loss yet.

**Step 3 — Intermediate Representation (IR) Construction**

The assembly tuples are translated into platform-independent SSA-form IR. The MOV instruction becomes:

$$
\text{MOV } R0, \text{0x123} \longrightarrow t_1 = 0x123
$$

where $t_1$ is a fresh temporary variable in SSA (Static Single Assignment) form. The full IR for the function is the set:

$$
\mathcal{I}_f = \{ t_1, t_2, \dots, t_n \}
$$

such that each $t_i$ is defined exactly once. The IR program counter $p$ satisfies:

$$
p : \mathcal{I}_f \rightarrow \mathbb{N}
$$

where $p(t_i)$ is the linear program order.

> **Explanation of conversion logic:** SSA form ensures that every variable has a single definition point, which dramatically simplifies data flow analysis. This is the standard representation used in modern compiler infrastructure (LLVM, Ghidra's P-code).

**Step 4 — Control Flow Graph (CFG) Construction**

Basic blocks are identified by scanning $\mathcal{I}_f$ for *leader instructions*:

- The first instruction of the function (entry point)
- Any target of a branch instruction
- Any instruction immediately following a branch

Mathematically, the set of leaders $L \subseteq \mathcal{I}_f$ is:

$$
L = \{ t_1 \} \cup \{ t_i \vert \exists\, t_j : \text{target}(t_j) = p(t_i) \} \cup \{ t_i \vert \text{prev}(t_i) \in B_{\text{ranch}} \}
$$

A basic block $B_k$ is then:

$$
B_k = \{ t_i \in \mathcal{I}_f \vert t_i \in [l_m, l_{m+1} - 1] \}
$$

where $l_m, l_{m+1} \in L$ are consecutive leaders. The CFG is the directed graph:

$$
G_f = (V, E), \quad V = \{B_1, B_2, \dots, B_m\}
$$

and the edge set is:

$$
E = \{ (B_i, B_j) \vert \exists\, t_k \in B_i : \text{succ}(t_k) \in B_j \}
$$

> **Explanation of conversion logic:** The CFG captures the *possible execution paths* through the function. Each node is a straight-line sequence of IR instructions; each edge is a possible control transfer (conditional branch, fall-through, call, return).

**Step 5 — Cyclomatic Complexity Calculation**

The **cyclomatic complexity** $M$ of the CFG is computed as:

$$
M = E - V + 2P
$$

For a typical decompiled function with $V = 8$ basic blocks, $E = 11$ edges, and $P = 1$ connected component:

$$
M = 11 - 8 + 2(1) = 5
$$

This metric indicates the **minimum number of independent paths** through the function — a key indicator of testability and structural complexity.

> **Explanation of conversion logic:** McCabe's cyclomatic complexity is widely used in software engineering to quantify code complexity. For embedded firmware, $M > 10$ is considered high-risk and may indicate obfuscation or malware.

**Step 6 — Type Recovery via Data Flow Analysis**

Stack frame analysis is performed by tracking SP-relative accesses. Consider the IR instruction:

$$
t_5 = \text{load}(\text{SP} - 8)
$$

The *reaching definitions* analysis determines that the slot at $\text{SP} - 8$ is written only by:

$$
t_3 = \text{store}(\text{SP} - 8, t_2)
$$

and read by $t_5$. The size of the access (4 bytes) is inferred from the load width, yielding:

$$
\text{Type}(t_3) = \text{uint32\_t}
$$

The recovered local variable is named `local_8` (a placeholder) and the function signature becomes:

$$
\text{Sig}(f) = \text{uint32\_t}\ f(\text{void})
$$

> **Explanation of conversion logic:** Compilers do not store type information in the binary — they only emit load/store instructions of specific widths. The decompiler must heuristically infer types from access patterns, calling conventions, and API call sites (e.g., a value passed to `printf("%d", x)` is likely an `int`).

**Step 7 — High-Level Code Generation**

The CFG is traversed in reverse post-order to reconstruct structured C code. The IR sequence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= 0 \\
t_2 &= t_1 < 10 \\
\text{if } t_2 \text{ goto } L_1 \\
t_3 &= t_1 + 1 \\
L_1 : \text{ return } t_1
\end{aligned}
$$

is matched against the *while-loop template* and emitted as:

```c
uint32_t FUN_0800042a(void) {
    uint32_t local_8 = 0;
    while (local_8 < 10) {
        local_8 = local_8 + 1;
    }
    return local_8;
}
```

> **Explanation of conversion logic:** The pattern matching engine uses a library of C AST templates. The IR pattern `if (cond) goto L; body; L: ...` is the canonical *while-loop* signature. This is how decompilers "see through" the optimization-induced flattening of structured code.

## 3.2 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Stage | Input | Transformation | Output | Lossy? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Loading | Binary file $B$ | Parsing | Memory map $\mathcal{M}$ | No |
| 2. Disassembly | Code bytes $b_{\text{code}}$ | ISA decoding | Assembly set $\mathcal{A}$ | No |
| 3. IR Generation | Assembly $\mathcal{A}$ | SSA translation | IR set $\mathcal{I}_f$ | Minimal |
| 4. CFA | IR $\mathcal{I}_f$ | Basic block partitioning | CFG $G_f = (V, E)$ | No |
| 5. DFA | IR + CFG | Use-def chains | Type & variable info | **Yes** |
| 6. Type Recovery | Stack trace | Heuristic inference | Recovered types | **Yes** |
| 7. Code Generation | IR + CFG + Types | Pattern matching | C source $S'_f$ | **Yes** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Decompilation Pipeline — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **end-to-end decompilation pipeline** as a functional architecture, mapping the interaction between each transformation stage. This diagram is rendered as a flow chart rather than a physical schematic, which is more appropriate for software-tooling workflows.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["BINARY FIRMWARE INPUT"] --> B["STAGE 1: FILE LOADER"]
    B --> C["STAGE 2: DISASSEMBLER"]
    C --> D["STAGE 3: IR GENERATOR"]
    D --> E["STAGE 4: DATA FLOW ANALYZER"]
    D --> F["STAGE 5: CONTROL FLOW ANALYZER"]
    E --> G["STAGE 6: TYPE RECOVERY ENGINE"]
    F --> G
    G --> H["STAGE 7: STRUCTURAL PATTERN MATCHER"]
    H --> I["STAGE 8: C CODE EMITTER"]
    I --> J["RECOVERED C SOURCE OUTPUT"]

    C -. "Assembly Mnemonics" .-> K["INTERMEDIATE DATA STORE"]
    D -. "SSA IR Tuples" .-> K
    F -. "CFG Graph GV, E" .-> K
    K -. "Shared Context" .-> E
    K -. "Shared Context" .-> G

    style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0a2942,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#2e75b6,color:#000000
    style E fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#000000
    style F fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#000000
    style G fill:#ffc000,stroke:#bf8f00,color:#000000
    style H fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#bf5f17,color:#ffffff
    style I fill:#c00000,stroke:#7f0000,color:#ffffff
    style J fill:#00b050,stroke:#007030,color:#ffffff
    style K fill:#e2e2e2,stroke:#808080,color:#000000,stroke-dasharray: 5 5
```

## 4.2 Diagram Interpretation Guide

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Pipeline (Top-to-Bottom Flow):**
>
> - **Solid arrows** represent the **primary data flow** — the binary $B$ is progressively transformed at each stage.
> - **Dashed arrows** represent the **shared intermediate data store** — analyses exchange results bidirectionally to enable cross-validation (e.g., data flow informs type recovery, and type recovery improves control flow structuring).
> - **Color coding** indicates the *loss level* at each stage:
>   - **Blue shades** = lossless transformations (loading, disassembly, IR gen)
>   - **Yellow/Orange** = heuristic transformations (type recovery, pattern matching)
>   - **Red/Green** = lossy code generation stages

## 4.3 Decompiler vs Disassembler — Comparative Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph "DISASSEMBLER ONLY"
        direction TB
        A1["Binary B"] --> A2["Asm Mnemonics"]
        A2 --> A3["Manual Analysis"]
    end

    subgraph "FULL DECOMPILER"
        direction TB
        B1["Binary B"] --> B2["Asm Mnemonics"]
        B2 --> B3["IR Generation"]
        B3 --> B4["DFA and CFA"]
        B4 --> B5["Type Recovery"]
        B5 --> B6["C Source Output"]
    end

    A3 -. "Time Consuming" .-> C["Human Expert"]
    B6 -. "Automated" .-> D["Quick Triage"]

    style A3 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000000
    style B6 fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#00b050,color:#000000
    style C fill:#ffe699,stroke:#bf8f00,color:#000000
    style D fill:#c6e0b4,stroke:#548235,color:#000000
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When asked the difference between a *disassembler* and a *decompiler*, emphasize that a **disassembler produces assembly code** (a 1:1 mapping that a human must analyze), while a **decompiler produces high-level C-like code** (a many-to-one abstraction requiring semantic analysis). Disassembly is *syntactic*; decompilation is *semantic*.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Definition of a Decompiler
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP, Q1a]**

> **Q1a.** Define a *decompiler* in the context of embedded systems. Mention any two practical applications.

**Model Answer:**

A **decompiler** is a software tool that translates a compiled executable program (machine code or firmware binary) back into a higher-level language representation (typically C or C++). It performs the **inverse operation of a compiler**, though the result is only an approximation of the original source due to information loss during compilation.

**Two practical applications in embedded systems:**
1. **Firmware security auditing** — analyzing third-party IoT device firmware for known vulnerabilities (CVE verification).
2. **Legacy code recovery** — reconstructing source code from old microcontroller firmware when the original source is lost or unavailable.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (1 mark lost commonly):** Students often confuse *decompilers* with *disassemblers*. A disassembler produces **assembly code**; a decompiler produces **high-level source code**. Always clarify the distinction.

---

### Question 2: Compiler-Decompiler Inverse Relationship
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP, Q1b]**

> **Q1b.** Explain why the composition $D \circ C$ does **not** equal the identity function $I$, where $C$ is a compiler and $D$ is a decompiler.

**Model Answer:**

The composition $D \circ C \neq I$ because the compilation process is **lossy and many-to-one**. During compilation:

- Variable and function names are replaced with symbols or numeric offsets.
- High-level type distinctions (e.g., `int`, `char`, `float`) are erased and replaced with raw memory access widths.
- High-level control structures (`for`, `while`, `switch`) are lowered to `goto`-style branch instructions.
- Comments, whitespace, and preprocessor directives are discarded.
- Compiler optimizations may further *reorder, eliminate, or duplicate* code.

Since this metadata is **not stored in the binary**, the decompiler cannot recover it. Therefore, the decompiled output $S' = D(C(S))$ is only an approximation of the original $S$, not an exact copy. Thus, $D \circ C \approx I$, not exactly equal to $I$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Each Part B question features two sub-parts (typically 7 marks each). Students may choose **either Question A or Question B**. Both options are provided below for comprehensive preparation.

---

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) — Pipeline Analysis

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Q2A]**

> **Q2A.** With a neat block diagram, explain the **complete decompilation pipeline** in detail. Discuss the role of **Data Flow Analysis (DFA)** and **Control Flow Analysis (CFA)** in the recovery of high-level source code. **(14 marks: 7 + 7)**
>
> **Part (a)** — Draw and explain the 7-stage decompilation pipeline. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Part (b)** — Explain DFA and CFA with reference to type recovery and CFG construction. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — The 7-Stage Decompilation Pipeline (7 marks):**

The decompilation pipeline consists of the following sequential stages:

| Stage | Function | Output |
|---|---|---|
| 1. **File Loader** | Parses ELF/PE/COFF/flat binary headers, maps sections to virtual addresses | Memory map $\mathcal{M}$ |
| 2. **Disassembler** | Decodes raw machine code bytes into assembly mnemonics using ISA encoding tables | Assembly set $\mathcal{A}$ |
| 3. **IR Generator** | Translates architecture-specific assembly into platform-independent SSA IR | IR set $\mathcal{I}_f$ |
| 4. **Control Flow Analyzer** | Partitions IR into basic blocks, builds the CFG $G_f = (V, E)$ | CFG |
| 5. **Data Flow Analyzer** | Computes use-def chains, liveness, reaching definitions | DFA facts |
| 6. **Type Recovery Engine** | Infers high-level types from access widths and calling conventions | Recovered types |
| 7. **Code Emitter** | Pattern-matches IR/CFG to C templates, emits C source | C source $S'_f$ |
