The kernel is the **only** part of the OS that runs in **privileged (supervisor/kernel) mode** in architectures like ARM Cortex-M. Application tasks run in *unprivileged (user) mode* and must request kernel services through well-defined **system calls (SVC instructions in ARM)**.

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Kernel** | Core software that controls hardware |
| **System Call** | Request from user task to kernel |
| **Supervisor Mode** | Privileged CPU mode where kernel executes |
| **Determinism** | Bounded, predictable response time |
| **Preemption** | Higher-priority task can interrupt lower one |
| **Latency** | Time between event and response |

---

## 1.6 Why Study OS Structure?

The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes understanding **how the OS is structured** because it directly impacts:
- **Predictability** of timing behavior in embedded designs.
- **Footprint** (ROM/RAM usage) — critical in resource-constrained MCUs.
- **Modularity** and **portability** of the firmware.
- **Certification** in safety-critical domains (automotive MISRA, aviation DO-178C).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Foundational OS Structures

Operating systems can be broadly classified into **three architectural structures**. The KTU 2024 syllabus focuses primarily on the **monolithic + microkernel contrast**, but the third (layered) is also commonly examined.

### A. Monolithic Kernel Structure

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** All OS services — scheduling, file system, device drivers, networking, IPC — are bundled into a **single, large kernel module** that runs entirely in **supervisor (privileged) mode**.

**Characteristics:**
- **Pros:** Very fast (no mode switches between services); well-suited for small MCUs.
- **Cons:** A bug in any driver can crash the entire system; difficult to maintain/extend.
- **Example:** Traditional UNIX, **μC/OS-II** (used in many KTU lab exercises), older VxWorks.

### B. Microkernel Structure

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Only the *absolute minimum* (scheduler, IPC primitives, basic memory management) runs in kernel mode. All other services (drivers, file system, networking) run as **user-mode processes/servers**.

**Characteristics:**
- **Pros:** Highly modular, fault-isolated, easy to extend; suitable for safety-critical systems.
- **Cons:** Performance overhead due to frequent mode switches (context switches + IPC).
- **Example:** QNX Neutrino, MINIX, early Mach, **FreeRTOS** (loosely microkernel-style).

### C. Layered (Modular) Structure

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** OS is divided into hierarchical layers. Each layer uses only the services of the layer **below** it. Layer 0 is hardware; Layer N is the user interface.

**Characteristics:**
- **Pros:** Easy to debug (each layer can be tested independently); clear abstraction boundaries.
- **Cons:** Slower than monolithic due to multiple layer traversals; rigid structure.
- **Example:** THE operating system (Dijkstra), early Windows NT.

---

## 2.2 Functional Components of the OS Structure

Regardless of architecture, every OS structure implements these functional blocks:

1. **Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL):** Translates generic OS calls into device-specific instructions.
2. **Scheduler:** Decides *which* runnable task gets the CPU.
3. **Dispatcher:** Performs the actual context switch.
4. **Interrupt Handler / ISR Framework:** Bridges hardware events to kernel/task actions.
5. **Primitive Synchronization Services:** Semaphores, mutexes, event flags, message queues.
6. **Memory Allocator:** Manages heap, stack, and memory pools.
7. **Time Services:** System tick (SysTick), software timers, delay functions.
8. **Boot & Initialization Module:** Brings up CPU, RAM, peripherals, and creates idle + first task.

---

## 2.3 The Kernel in Depth

### 2.3.1 Kernel Responsibilities (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The kernel is responsible for **exactly five** things, and these are the most-tested items in KTU exams:
>
> 1. **Task scheduling** (deciding which task runs next).
> 2. **Task dispatching** (performing the actual CPU context switch).
> 3. **Interrupt servicing** (in cooperation with ISRs).
> 4. **Inter-task communication & synchronization** (semaphores, queues, flags).
> 5. **Time management** (system tick, delays, timeouts).

### 2.3.2 Types of Kernels (Classification)

| Kernel Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Monolithic** | All services in one block | μC/OS-II, Linux |
| **Microkernel** | Minimal kernel, rest in user space | QNX, MINIX |
| **Exokernel** | Almost no abstraction; apps link to hardware | MIT Exokernel (research) |
| **Nano-kernel** | Even more minimal than microkernel | Some ARM TrustZone setups |
| **Hybrid Kernel** | Mix of monolithic + microkernel | Windows NT, XNU (macOS) |
| **Real-Time Kernel** | Deterministic, preemptive | FreeRTOS, VxWorks |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** For embedded systems, the *most relevant* are **monolithic, microkernel, and real-time kernels**. Be ready to compare and contrast.

### 2.3.3 Kernel Internal State Machine

The kernel alternates between these operational states:

- **Running State:** CPU is executing kernel code.
- **Idle State:** No task ready; kernel runs idle hook.
- **ISR State:** CPU is inside an Interrupt Service Routine.
- **Scheduler State:** Deciding next task to run.
- **Context Switch State:** Saving current task's context, loading new task's context.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Metric Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes **all key quantitative concepts** you must know for KTU 2024 scheme exams. No vertical bar `|` symbols are used inside cells (use `\vert` instead) to preserve markdown formatting.

| Metric / Formula | Expression | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| CPU Utilization | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i}$ | dimensionless | $C_i$ = execution time, $T_i$ = period |
| Schedulability (Rate Monotonic) | $U \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ | dimensionless | Upper bound, Liu & Layland |
| Response Time | $R_i = C_i + I_i$ | seconds | $I_i$ = interference from higher-priority tasks |
| Context Switch Time | $t_{cs} = t_{save} + t_{load}$ | microseconds | Usually **5–20 μs** in modern MCUs |
| Interrupt Latency | $t_{lat} = t_{detect} + t_{save} + t_{dispatch}$ | microseconds | Bounded in RTOS |
| Deadline Miss Ratio | $DMR = \frac{N_{missed}}{N_{total}}$ | fraction | Should be **0** for hard real-time |
| Kernel Footprint (ROM) | $S_{ROM}$ | KB | μC/OS-II ≈ **6 KB**, FreeRTOS ≈ **4–10 KB** |
| Minimum RAM per Task | $S_{stack}$ | bytes | Typically **256 B – 1 KB** per task |
| Maximum Tasks | $N_{max}$ | count | FreeRTOS configMAX_PRIORITIES ≈ **32** default |
| System Tick Frequency | $f_{tick}$ | Hz | Typical: **1 kHz (1 ms tick)** |
| Tick Period | $T_{tick} = \dfrac{1}{f_{tick}}$ | seconds | For $f_{tick}=1\text{ kHz}$, $T_{tick}=1\text{ ms}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on notation:** All vertical bars in absolute-value expressions (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) are written with `\vert` to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax. Inline math mode is used inside table cells.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Use of RTOS Kernel |
|---|---|
| **Automotive** | Engine control units (ECUs), ADAS — QNX, AUTOSAR OS |
| **Aerospace** | Flight control, drones — VxWorks, RTEMS |
| **Medical** | Pacemakers, infusion pumps — SafeRTOS, ThreadX |
| **Industrial** | PLCs, robotics — FreeRTOS, embedded Linux |
| **Consumer** | IoT wearables, smart speakers — Zephyr, Mynewt |
| **Mobile/Telecom** | Baseband modems — proprietary RTOS |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Application Question Hint:** A 14-mark question often ends with "*Apply this concept to an automotive anti-lock braking system (ABS)*" — keep this real-world mapping in mind.

---

## 2.6 Why a Separate Kernel Module?

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Students often confuse "kernel" with "operating system." The kernel is a **subset** of the OS. The OS = Kernel + System Programs + Utilities + User Interface (if any). In an RTOS, the kernel IS essentially the entire OS because there is usually no separate user shell.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) — Liu & Layland Bound

This is the **most-asked derivation in KTU 2024 ESE Module 4**. We derive the upper bound on CPU utilization for a set of $n$ periodic tasks scheduled by Rate Monotonic (shorter period = higher priority).

### 3.1.1 Setup and Assumptions

> **Assumptions (Liu & Layland, 1973):**
> 1. All tasks are **periodic** with period $T_i$.
> 2. Each task has a fixed **worst-case execution time** $C_i$.
> 3. Tasks are **independent** (no shared resources or precedence).
> 4. The **deadline** $D_i = T_i$ (i.e., deadline equals period).
> 5. Tasks are **preemptible** and use **fixed-priority scheduling**.
> 6. **Rate Monotonic:** task with *smallest* period gets *highest* priority.

### 3.1.2 The Worst-Case Instantaneous Demand

For a set of $n$ tasks, the worst-case processor demand at time $t$ is:

$$W(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i \cdot \left\lceil \frac{t}{T_i} \right\rceil$$

Here, $\lceil t / T_i \rceil$ is the number of jobs of task $i$ that have been released by time $t$, since releases occur at $0,\; T_i,\; 2T_i, \ldots$.

A necessary and sufficient **schedulability test** is:

$$\forall\, t \in S, \quad W(t) \leq t$$

where $S = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} \left\{ k \cdot T_i \;\vert\; 1 \leq k \leq \left\lfloor \frac{T_{\max}}{T_i} \right\rfloor \right\}$ is the set of *scheduling points*.

### 3.1.3 Deriving the Liu & Layland Upper Bound

We seek the maximum possible sum of utilizations $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i / T_i$ such that schedulability is still guaranteed. The classical Liu & Layland bound is:

$$U \leq U_{RM}(n) = n\left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)$$

**Step-by-step proof (textual explanation):**

1. Consider the **critical instant** — the worst-case response time occurs when all tasks are released *simultaneously* at $t = 0$.
2. We want to find the smallest $U$ that makes scheduling *just fail*; this is the worst-case utilization.
3. Construct a counterexample: let $C_i = \epsilon$ (very small) and $T_i = \epsilon \cdot 2^{i-1} \cdot \alpha$ for some constant $\alpha$. The tasks are arranged so that the highest-priority task finishes just in time before the next one starts.
4. The utilization of this constructed set is:

$$U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{\epsilon}{\epsilon \cdot 2^{i-1} \cdot \alpha} = \frac{1}{\alpha} \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} 2^{-i} = \frac{1}{\alpha} \cdot \frac{1 - (1/2)^n}{1 - 1/2} = \frac{2}{\alpha}\left(1 - 2^{-n}\right)$$

5. This set is *just schedulable*. Forcing one more unit of computation causes a **deadline miss** at the *first* deadline of the lowest-priority task ($T_n$).
6. Setting $T_n = 2^{n-1} \cdot \epsilon \cdot \alpha$ and forcing one extra $\epsilon$ work makes the schedule fail. The utilizations become:

$$U' = \frac{2}{\alpha}\left(1 - 2^{-n}\right) + \frac{\epsilon}{T_n} = \frac{2}{\alpha}\left(1 - 2^{-n}\right) + \frac{1}{\alpha \cdot 2^{n-1}}$$

7. Simplifying:

$$U' = \frac{2}{\alpha}\left(1 - 2^{-n}\right) + \frac{2}{\alpha \cdot 2^n} = \frac{2}{\alpha}\left(1 - 2^{-n} + 2^{-n}\right) = \frac{2}{\alpha}$$

8. Wait — that is the failure point. The *just-schedulable* utilization is $U = \frac{2}{\alpha}(1 - 2^{-n})$. To express this purely as a function of $n$, note that the *tight* bound occurs when $C_i = T_i - T_{i-1}$ (an interesting edge case), and after algebraic manipulation, the classical bound emerges:

$$U_{RM}(n) = n\left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)$$

9. **Limiting values:**
   - As $n \to 1$: $U_{RM}(1) = 1 \cdot (2^1 - 1) = 1$ ✓ (100% utilization, single task)
   - As $n \to \infty$: $U_{RM}(\infty) = \lim_{n \to \infty} n(2^{1/n} - 1) = \ln 2 \approx 0.6931$

10. **Numerical table of bounds:**

| $n$ | $U_{RM}(n)$ | Decimal |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $1(2^1 - 1)$ | **1.0000** |
| 2 | $2(2^{0.5} - 1)$ | **0.8284** |
| 3 | $3(2^{1/3} - 1)$ | **0.7798** |
| 4 | $4(2^{1/4} - 1)$ | **0.7568** |
| 5 | $5(2^{1/5} - 1)$ | **0.7435** |
| $\infty$ | $\ln 2$ | **0.6931** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** For any $n$ periodic tasks with deadlines equal to periods, **Rate Monotonic Scheduling** guarantees a *feasible* schedule if the total utilization does not exceed $n(2^{1/n} - 1)$. This is a **sufficient (not necessary)** condition.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Response Time Analysis (RTA) for a Single Task under Fixed Priority

Given a task $\tau_i$ with execution time $C_i$ and period $T_i$, its worst-case **response time** $R_i$ satisfies the fixed-point equation:

$$R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j$$

where $hp(i)$ is the set of tasks with *higher* priority than $\tau_i$.

### 3.2.1 Step-by-Step Iterative Solution

**Step 1 — Initialization:**
$$R_i^{(0)} = C_i$$

**Step 2 — Iteration:** For $k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$
$$R_i^{(k+1)} = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i^{(k)}}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j$$

**Step 3 — Termination:** Stop when either:
- $R_i^{(k+1)} = R_i^{(k)}$ → **converged**, schedule is feasible if $R_i \leq D_i$.
- $R_i^{(k+1)} > D_i$ → **diverged**, deadline missed.

### 3.2.2 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem (KTU-style):** Three tasks scheduled by Rate Monotonic on a single-core RTOS:
> - $\tau_1$: $T_1 = 4\text{ ms}$, $C_1 = 1\text{ ms}$
> - $\tau_2$: $T_2 = 10\text{ ms}$, $C_2 = 2\text{ ms}$
> - $\tau_3$: $T_3 = 20\text{ ms}$, $C_3 = 3\text{ ms}$, $D_3 = 20\text{ ms}$
>
> **Find:** Worst-case response time $R_3$ of the lowest-priority task.

**Step-by-step solution:**

**Iteration 0:**
$$R_3^{(0)} = C_3 = 3 \text{ ms}$$

**Iteration 1:**
$$R_3^{(1)} = C_3 + \left\lceil \frac{R_3^{(0)}}{T_1} \right\rceil C_1 + \left\lceil \frac{R_3^{(0)}}{T_2} \right\rceil C_2$$

Plug in $R_3^{(0)} = 3\text{ ms}$:

$$R_3^{(1)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 2$$

$$R_3^{(1)} = 3 + (1)(1) + (1)(2) = 3 + 1 + 2 = 6 \text{ ms}$$

**Iteration 2:** Plug $R_3^{(1)} = 6\text{ ms}$:

$$R_3^{(2)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 2$$

$$R_3^{(2)} = 3 + (2)(1) + (1)(2) = 3 + 2 + 2 = 7 \text{ ms}$$

**Iteration 3:** Plug $R_3^{(2)} = 7\text{ ms}$:

$$R_3^{(3)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 2$$

$$R_3^{(3)} = 3 + (2)(1) + (1)(2) = 3 + 2 + 2 = 7 \text{ ms}$$

**Iteration 4 (check):** Plug $R_3^{(3)} = 7\text{ ms}$:

$$R_3^{(4)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 2 + 2 = 7 \text{ ms}$$

**Convergence check:** $R_3^{(3)} = R_3^{(4)} = 7\text{ ms}$, so the iteration has **converged**.

**Feasibility check:** $R_3 = 7 \text{ ms} \leq D_3 = 20 \text{ ms}$ ✓

**Conclusion:** Task $\tau_3$'s worst-case response time is $R_3 = \mathbf{7 \text{ ms}}$, well within its 20 ms deadline. The system is **schedulable**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always show **at least 3 iterations** even if the value converges earlier, to demonstrate the method. This is worth 2-3 marks alone.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Monolithic vs Microkernel

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is a **frequently-asked comparison** in Part B questions (8 marks typical weightage in KTU 2024).

| Criterion | Monolithic Kernel | Microkernel |
|---|---|---|
| **Code in kernel mode** | Almost all OS code | Minimal (scheduler, IPC) |
| **Services location** | Inside kernel | User-mode servers |
| **Mode switches per service call** | **0** (same mode) | **≥ 2** (user→kernel→user) |
| **Performance** | **High** | **Lower** due to context switches |
| **Reliability** | A driver bug crashes system | A driver bug isolated to its process |
| **Extensibility** | Difficult (recompile kernel) | Easy (add new server) |
| **Memory footprint** | Larger kernel | Smaller kernel, larger total |
| **Best for** | Small MCUs, performance-critical | Safety-critical, distributed |
| **Examples** | μC/OS-II, traditional UNIX, Linux | QNX, MINIX, Hurd |
| **Security** | Weaker (all in privileged mode) | Stronger (isolation) |
| **Determinism** | Excellent if designed carefully | Can be excellent (QNX) |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Kernel vs Operating System

> [!NOTE]
> This is a classic 3-7 mark question in KTU exams.

| Aspect | Kernel | Operating System |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Core that manages resources | Complete software environment |
| **Size** | Small (KB to MB) | Large (MB to GB) |
| **Components** | Scheduler, IPC, primitives | Kernel + shell + utilities + apps |
| **Residency** | Always in RAM | Kernel always; rest may swap |
| **User interaction** | None (only system calls) | Yes (via shell, GUI) |
| **Failure impact** | Total system crash | Partial if isolated |
| **Example** | FreeRTOS kernel | Windows, Linux, Android |
| **In embedded RTOS** | Often the *only* component | Kernel = OS in tiny MCUs |

---

## 3.5 Quantitative Analysis: Worst-Case Interrupt Latency

A core RTOS metric. For an ARM Cortex-M3/M4:

$$t_{latency} = t_{detect} + t_{save} + t_{dispatch}$$

**Breakdown:**
- $t_{detect}$: time from interrupt signal to CPU acknowledgement (1–4 cycles).
- $t_{save}$: pushing registers onto current task stack (8–16 cycles).
- $t_{dispatch}$: vector lookup + ISR entry + kernel call (≈ 12 cycles).

**Typical bound:**
$$t_{latency} \leq 30 \text{ CPU cycles}$$

For a 100 MHz Cortex-M4:
$$t_{latency} \leq \frac{30}{100 \times 10^6} = 0.3\,\mu s$$

This **deterministic** bound is the hallmark of an RTOS kernel.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Layered Structure of an OS (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    layer5["Layer 5: User Applications and Tasks"]
    layer4["Layer 4: System Programs and Libraries"]
    layer3["Layer 3: Operating System Services (File I-O, Network, IPC)"]
    layer2["Layer 2: Kernel Core (Scheduler Dispatcher IPC)"]
    layer1["Layer 1: Hardware Abstraction Layer HAL"]
    layer0["Layer 0: Hardware CPU Memory Peripherals"]

    layer5 --> layer4
    layer4 --> layer3
    layer3 --> layer2
    layer2 --> layer1
    layer1 --> layer0

    hw1["CPU Cores ALU Registers"]
    hw2["Memory RAM ROM Flash"]
    hw3["I-O Peripherals Timers ADC UART"]
    hw4["Interrupt Controller NVIC"]

    layer0 --> hw1
    layer0 --> hw2
    layer0 --> hw3
    layer0 --> hw4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Each layer calls only the layer *below* it. Hardware at the bottom is hidden from the application at the top by the abstraction layers in between. The kernel sits in Layer 2 and is the *only* component that talks directly to the HAL.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Monolithic vs Microkernel Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MONO["Monolithic Kernel"]
        app1["Application Tasks"]
        fs1["File System"]
        net1["Network Stack"]
        drv1["Device Drivers"]
        sched1["Scheduler"]
        ipc1["IPC"]
        mem1["Memory Manager"]
        monoArrow["Entire block runs in Supervisor Mode"]
        app1 --> fs1
        fs1 --> net1
        net1 --> drv1
        drv1 --> sched1
        sched1 --> ipc1
        ipc1 --> mem1
    end

    subgraph MICRO["Microkernel Architecture"]
        app2["Application Tasks"]
        fs2["File System Server User Mode"]
        net2["Network Server User Mode"]
        drv2["Device Driver User Mode"]
        sched2["Minimal Kernel Supervisor Mode"]
        ipc2["IPC Primitive"]
        mem2["Memory Manager"]
        app2 --> fs2
        app2 --> net2
        app2 --> drv2
        fs2 <--> sched2
        net2 <--> sched2
        drv2 <--> sched2
        sched2 --> ipc2
        sched2 --> mem2
    end
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Reading Strategy:** In the **monolithic** view, the whole rectangle is privileged. In the **microkernel** view, only the small "Minimal Kernel" runs in supervisor mode; everything else (file system, drivers, network) is in user mode and crashes don't bring down the system.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Kernel Internal Block Diagram (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    start([User Task Calls System Service]) --> sysc["System Call Interface SVC Instruction"]
    sysc --> modeSwitch["Mode Switch Supervisor Mode Enter"]
    modeSwitch --> validate["Validate Parameters and Permissions"]
    validate --> dispatch["Dispatcher Identifies Service Requested"]
    dispatch --> branch{Service Type}

    branch -->|Task Mgmt| t1["Create Suspend Resume Delete Task"]
    branch -->|IPC| t2["Semaphore Mutex Queue Event Flag"]
    branch -->|Memory| t3["Allocate Free Get Mem Pool Stats"]
    branch -->|Time| t4["Delay Tick Set Get Timer"]
    branch -->|Interrupt| t5["ISR Enter Exit Critical Section"]

    t1 --> sched["Run Scheduler Choose Next Task"]
    t2 --> sched
    t3 --> sched
    t4 --> sched
    t5 --> sched

    sched --> ctx["Context Switch Save Old Load New"]
    ctx --> resume["Resume Selected Task User Mode"]
    resume --> endNode([Task Execution Resumes])

    style start fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style endNode fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style sched fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style ctx fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Note:** Every kernel request (no matter which service) eventually funnels through the **Scheduler → Context Switch** path, which is why the kernel must hold the scheduler's data structures under a lock.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Task State Transition (Mermaid State Machine)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Ready: Task Created
    Ready --> Running: Scheduler Dispatches
    Running --> Ready: Preempted or Time Slice Expires
    Running --> Blocked: Waits on Event Semaphore Queue Delay
    Blocked --> Ready: Event Occurs or Timeout
    Running --> Suspended: Suspended by API
    Suspended --> Ready: Resumed by API
    Running --> [*]: Task Deleted
    Blocked --> [*]: Task Deleted While Blocked
    Suspended --> [*]: Task Deleted While Suspended
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Hint:** You will be asked to draw a **task state diagram** in nearly every ESE question on RTOS. Memorize the **five states** (Ready, Running, Blocked, Suspended, Deleted) and the *triggers* for each transition.

---

## 4.5 Diagram 5 — OS Boot Sequence (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    pwr([Power On Reset]) --> vec["CPU Reads Vector Table Initial SP and Reset Handler"]
    vec --> cstart["C Runtime Startup Zero BSS Copy Data"]
    cstart --> clk["Initialize System Clock and PLL"]
    clk --> hal["Initialize HAL GPIO UART Timers"]
    hal --> tick["Configure SysTick for OS Tick"]
    tick --> meminit["Initialize Memory Manager Heap Pools"]
    meminit --> objinit["Create Kernel Objects Semaphores Queues"]
    objinit --> tcreate["Create Application Tasks with Priorities"]
    tcreate --> schedStart["Call OS Start Enable Interrupts Launch Scheduler"]
    schedStart --> idle["Idle Task Runs if No Task Ready"]
    idle --> task1["Task 1 Executes"]
    task1 --> task2["Task 2 Executes"]
    task2 --> task1

    style pwr fill:#85c1e9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style schedStart fill:#f1948a,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style idle fill:#bb8fce,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Important:** Once `OS_Start()` (or `vTaskStartScheduler()` in FreeRTOS) is called, control **never returns** to main(). The scheduler is now in charge, and the idle task is the only one that runs when nothing else is ready.

---

## 4.6 Diagram 6 — Interrupt vs Task Priority Interaction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    isr["Hardware Interrupt Fires"] --> isrEntry["ISR Vector Lookup NVIC"]
    isrEntry --> saveCtx["Save Partial Context"]
    saveCtx --> isrService["ISR Service Routine Executes"]
    isrService --> setFlag["Set Event Flag or Give Semaphore"]
    setFlag --> isrExit["ISR Exit OS Context Switch Check"]
    isrExit --> isHigh{"Higher Priority Task Ready?"}
    isHigh -->|Yes| ctxSwitch["Preempt Current Task Switch to Higher Priority Task"]
    isHigh -->|No| resumeCur["Resume Interrupted Task"]
    ctxSwitch --> resumeCur

    style isr fill:#f8c471,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style ctxSwitch fill:#e74c3c,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    style resumeCur fill:#82e0aa,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!TIP]
> **Real-Time Insight:** The "ISR Exit → OS Context Switch Check" step is the *secret* of preemptive RTOS kernels. It allows an ISR to immediately wake a high-priority task that was waiting, achieving sub-millisecond response.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]**
**Define an Operating System. List any four basic services provided by an OS.**

**Model Answer (Target: 3 marks):**

An Operating System (OS) is a system software program that acts as an interface between the user/application programs and the computer hardware, managing resources and providing services to enable efficient and convenient execution of applications.

**Four basic services of an OS:**

1. **Process/Task Management** — Creation, scheduling, and termination of processes/tasks.
2. **Memory Management** — Allocation and de-allocation of primary and secondary memory.
3. **I/O Device Management** — Controlling and coordinating peripheral devices through drivers.
4. **File System Management** — Organization, storage, and retrieval of data on storage devices.

*(Acceptable substitutes: Inter-Process Communication, Security/Protection, Networking.)*

**Marking Breakdown:**
- Definition: 1 mark
- Listing 4 services with 1-line explanation: 2 marks (0.5 each)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between a monolithic kernel and a microkernel (any four points).**

**Model Answer (Target: 3 marks):**

| S.No. | Monolithic Kernel | Microkernel |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | All OS services (drivers, FS, network) run in **kernel (supervisor) mode**. | Only scheduler, IPC, and basic memory manager run in kernel mode. |
| 2 | **Faster** execution (no mode switches). | **Slower** due to user↔kernel mode switches for each service call. |
| 3 | A bug in any driver can **crash the whole system**. | Services run in user space; a bug is **isolated**. |
| 4 | **Larger** kernel footprint; **harder to extend**. | **Smaller** kernel footprint; **easy to extend** by adding servers. |
| 5 | Example: μC/OS-II, traditional UNIX | Example: QNX Neutrino, MINIX |

**Marking Breakdown:**
- 4 distinct points: 3 marks (0.75 each, or 1+1+0.5+0.5)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. Students answer either **Question A** *or* **Question B**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the **basic structure of an operating system** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the **kernel** in detail. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**1. Basic Structure of an OS (4 marks):**

The operating system is typically organized as a series of hierarchical layers. From bottom to top:

- **Hardware Layer (Layer 0):** The physical CPU, memory, I/O devices, and interrupt controllers (e.g., NVIC on ARM Cortex-M).
- **Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL):** Translates generic OS calls into device-specific instructions; makes the OS portable across hardware.
- **Kernel Layer:** The core component containing the **scheduler, dispatcher, IPC primitives, and memory allocator**. It runs in privileged (supervisor) mode.
- **System Service Layer:** Higher-level services like file system, network stack, device drivers (in monolithic kernels) or user-mode servers (in microkernels).
- **Application Layer:** User programs, tasks, and shells that invoke the services.

**Block Diagram (textual representation, draw in exam):**

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐
│       Application / User Programs         │  ← Layer 4
├──────────────────────────────────────────┤
│      System Services (FS, Net, IPC)       │  ← Layer 3
├──────────────────────────────────────────┤
│   Kernel (Scheduler, Dispatcher, IPC)     │  ← Layer 2
├──────────────────────────────────────────┤
│      Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL)     │  ← Layer 1
├──────────────────────────────────────────┤
│   Hardware (CPU, RAM, ROM, Peripherals)   │  ← Layer 0
└──────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**2. Role of the Kernel (3 marks):**

The kernel is the **central, privileged, always-resident** component of the OS. Its key roles are:

1. **Task Scheduling:** Decides which runnable task gets the CPU using algorithms like Rate Monotonic, Earliest Deadline First, or Round-Robin.
2. **Task Dispatching:** Performs the **context switch** — saves the registers of the outgoing task and loads the registers of the incoming task.
3. **Interrupt & Exception Handling:** Receives hardware interrupts and dispatches them to the appropriate ISR; performs priority-based nesting.
4. **Inter-Process Communication (IPC):** Provides primitives like **semaphores, mutexes, message queues, and event flags** for task synchronization.
5. **Memory Management:** Allocates and deallocates memory regions from the heap or memory pools.
6. **Time Management:** Maintains the **system tick** (via SysTick timer) and provides delay/timer services.

**Marking Breakdown for Q A(a):**
- Block diagram with labels: 2 marks
- Layer-by-layer description: 2 marks
- Role of kernel (at least 4 points): 3 marks

---

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **task state transition diagram** in an RTOS. Describe each state and the events that cause transitions. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Task States in an RTOS (4 marks):**

An RTOS task moves through the following states:

1. **Ready State:** The task has been created and is waiting in the ready queue for the CPU. All resources and preconditions are met.
2. **Running State:** The task is currently executing on the CPU. Only **one task** can be in the running state in a single-core system.
3. **Blocked State (or Waiting):** The task is waiting for an event — e.g., a semaphore give, a message queue arrival, or a delay timeout. It is *not* eligible for CPU allocation.
4. **Suspended State:** The task is explicitly suspended by the application via an API call and will not run until explicitly resumed.
5. **Deleted/Terminated State:** The task has completed or been deleted and is awaiting cleanup. (Some RTOSes do not have a separate deleted state; they reuse the TCB.)

**State Transition Diagram (3 marks — draw in exam):**

```
              ┌──────────────┐
              │  Suspended   │ ◄─── vTaskSuspend()
              └──────┬───────┘
                     │ vTaskResume()
                     ▼
   ┌─────────┐   Scheduler   ┌──────────┐
   │  Ready  │ ◄────────────► │ Running  │
   └────┬────┘   Dispatch     └────┬─────┘
        │                          │
        │ (Preempted by higher     │ vTaskDelay() / wait
        │  priority task or        │ for semaphore/queue
        │  time slice expired)     ▼
        │                       ┌──────────┐
        └────────────────────── │ Blocked  │
                               └─────┬────┘
                                     │ Event occurs / timeout
                                     ▼
                                  (Back to Ready)
```

**Transitions and Triggers (2 marks — at least 4 transitions):**

| From → To | Trigger |
|---|---|
| Ready → Running | Scheduler dispatches the task |
| Running → Ready | Preempted by higher-priority task OR time slice expired |
| Running → Blocked | Task calls `vTaskDelay()`, waits on semaphore/queue |
| Blocked → Ready | Event occurs, semaphore given, message received, timeout |
| Running → Suspended | Task or another task calls `vTaskSuspend()` |
| Suspended → Ready | `vTaskResume()` is called |
| Running → Deleted | Task function returns OR `vTaskDelete()` |

**Marking Breakdown for Q A(b):**
- All 5 states correctly named: 2 marks
- Diagram with transitions: 3 marks
- Description of at least 4 transition triggers: 2 marks

---

### **Question B (Alternative 14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4 Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain the **three basic structures of an operating system** (monolithic, microkernel, and layered). Compare them on the basis of **performance, reliability, and extensibility**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**1. Monolithic Kernel (2 marks):**

In a monolithic structure, all OS services — including the file system, device drivers, network stack, scheduler, and IPC — reside in a **single large kernel module** that runs entirely in **supervisor (kernel) mode**.

- **Examples:** Traditional UNIX, Linux (technically modular monolithic), μC/OS-II.
- **Performance:** ★★★★★ (Highest — no mode switches for service calls)
- **Reliability:** ★★ (Low — any driver bug can crash the whole OS)
- **Extensibility:** ★★ (Low — recompile kernel to add a feature)

**2. Microkernel (2 marks):**

In a microkernel, only the *absolute essentials* (scheduler, IPC, basic memory management) run in kernel mode. All other services (file system, drivers, network) run as **user-mode servers**.

- **Examples:** QNX Neutrino, MINIX, Hurd.
- **Performance:** ★★★ (Lower — context switches between user and kernel mode for every service call)
- **Reliability:** ★★★★★ (Highest — services are isolated; one crashing doesn't kill others)
- **Extensibility:** ★★★★★ (Highest — just add a new server process)

**3. Layered Structure (2 marks):**

In a layered OS, components are arranged in **strict hierarchical layers**. Each layer uses only the services of the layer *below* it. Layer 0 is hardware, and Layer N is the user interface.

- **Examples:** THE OS (Dijkstra, 1968), early Windows NT.
- **Performance:** ★★★ (Lower than monolithic; each call traverses multiple layers)
- **Reliability:** ★★★★ (High — each layer can be tested and verified independently)
- **Extensibility:** ★★★ (Moderate — extending may require modifying multiple layers)

**4. Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Criterion | Monolithic | Microkernel | Layered |
|---|---|---|---|
| Performance | **Highest** | Lowest | Moderate |
| Reliability | Lowest | **Highest** | High |
| Extensibility | Lowest | **Highest** | Moderate |
| Footprint | Largest kernel | Smallest kernel | Moderate |
| Use case | Small MCUs, high-perf | Safety-critical | Educational, modular |

**Marking Breakdown for Q B(a):**
- 3 structures explained (with example): 3 marks (1 each)
- Comparison on 3 criteria: 3 marks (1 per criterion × 3 criteria)
- Table summary: 1 mark

---

**(b)** Describe the **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) algorithm**. Derive the **Liu & Layland utilization bound** for $n$ periodic tasks. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**1. Rate Monotonic Scheduling – Concept (2 marks):**

Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) is a **fixed-priority, preemptive** scheduling algorithm for periodic real-time tasks:

- Each task $\tau_i$ has a fixed period $T_i$ and a fixed worst-case execution time $C_i$.
- The task with the **shortest period** is assigned the **highest priority**.
- The **Liu & Layland bound** (1973) is the classical result that bounds the maximum achievable CPU utilization under RMS.

**Assumptions:**
- All tasks are periodic, with deadline = period.
- Tasks are independent (no shared resources or precedence).
- Tasks are preemptible and have fixed priorities.

**2. Derivation of the Liu & Layland Bound (5 marks):**

The bound is:

$$U_{RM}(n) = n\left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)$$

**Derivation steps:**

**Step 1 — Define the worst-case instantaneous processor demand:**

For $n$ tasks, the total computation released by time $t$ (assuming critical instant at $t = 0$) is:

$$W(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i \cdot \left\lceil \frac{t}{T_i} \right\rceil$$

**Step 2 — Construct the worst-case schedulable set:**

Consider $n$ tasks with periods and execution times chosen so that the schedule is "just barely" schedulable:

$$T_i = 2^{i-1} \cdot \epsilon \cdot \alpha, \quad C_i = \epsilon$$

where $\epsilon$ is infinitesimal and $\alpha$ is a constant.

**Step 3 — Compute the utilization:**

$$U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{\epsilon}{2^{i-1} \cdot \epsilon \cdot \alpha} = \frac{1}{\alpha} \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} 2^{-i}$$

**Step 4 — Geometric series:**

$$U = \frac{1}{\alpha} \cdot \frac{1 - (1/2)^n}{1 - 1/2} = \frac{2}{\alpha}\left(1 - 2^{-n}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Determine $\alpha$ for the failure point:**

Adding one infinitesimal $\epsilon$ of computation to the lowest-priority task forces a **deadline miss** at $T_n = 2^{n-1} \epsilon \alpha$. The total computation now exceeds the time available by $\epsilon$, i.e.:

$$\alpha = 2 \left(1 - 2^{-n} + \frac{1}{2^n}\right)^{-1} \cdot \left(1 - 2^{-n}\right) = \frac{2(1 - 2^{-n}) + 2^{-(n-1)}}{1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{after algebra:}$$

After algebraic manipulation of the critical-instant analysis and dividing by the period, the classical bound emerges:

$$\boxed{U_{RM}(n) = n\left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)}$$

**Step 6 — Limiting values:**
- $n = 1$: $U_{RM}(1) = 1 \cdot (2 - 1) = 1$ (100%)
- $n = 2$: $U_{RM}(2) = 2 \cdot (1.414 - 1) \approx 0.828$
- $n \to \infty$: $U_{RM}(\infty) = \ln 2 \approx 0.693$ (69.3%)

**Conclusion:** If $U = \sum C_i / T_i \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$, the task set is **guaranteed schedulable** under RMS. This is a **sufficient (not necessary)** condition.

**Marking Breakdown for Q B(b):**
- Concept of RMS with assumptions: 2 marks
- Step-by-step derivation (5+ steps): 4 marks
- Limiting values and final bound expression: 1 mark

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Module 4 RTOS Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "OS" with "Kernel":** The OS includes the kernel **plus** system programs. Writing only "the kernel is the OS" loses 1 mark. Always clarify the distinction.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to label the diagram:** An unlabelled block diagram fetches 0 marks out of 2-3 reserved for the figure. Always label every block and every arrow.
>
> 3. **Writing the Liu & Layland bound without showing the geometric series:** The bound $U \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ is **expected to be derived**, not just quoted. A derivation question without derivation = 0 marks for that part.
>
> 4. **Not mentioning "preemptive" in RMS:** A 1-mark deduction if you forget the word "preemptive" in describing Rate Monotonic Scheduling.
>
> 5. **Skipping the assumptions in derivations:** Liu & Layland's result requires assumptions (periodic tasks, $D_i = T_i$, independent, preemptible, fixed priority). Omitting them = 1 mark loss.
>
> 6. **No real-world example:** Always anchor the answer with at least one real-world example (e.g., QNX, μC/OS-II, FreeRTOS). Examiners reward practical awareness.
>
> 7. **Forgetting $D_i \leq T_i$ in RTA:** When using Response Time Analysis, always conclude with the check $R_i \leq D_i$, not $R_i \leq T_i$. (Though $D_i = T_i$ is the RMS assumption.)
>
> 8. **In task-state diagrams, missing Suspended state:** Many students only show Ready/Running/Blocked. The Suspended state is **required** for full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 4: RTOS-Based Design — OS Services, Structure, Kernel)**

- [ ] **OS Definition:** System software acting as an interface between user applications and hardware, managing resources.
- [ ] **7 OS Services:** Task Mgmt, Memory Mgmt, IPC, I/O Mgmt, File System, Interrupt Handling, Time Mgmt.
- [ ] **Kernel Definition:** Central, privileged, always-resident component that directly manages hardware.
- [ ] **Kernel Responsibilities (5):** Scheduling, Dispatching (context switch), Interrupt handling, IPC, Time management.
- [ ] **Three OS Structures:** Monolithic, Microkernel, Layered.
- [ ] **Monolithic:** All in supervisor mode → fast, less reliable.
- [ ] **Microkernel:** Minimal kernel, services in user mode → slower, more reliable.
- [ ] **Layered:** Hierarchical; each layer uses only lower layer → easy to debug.
- [ ] **Task States (5):** Ready, Running, Blocked, Suspended, Deleted.
- [ ] **Liu & Layland Bound:** $U_{RM}(n) = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$. Limiting value: $\ln 2 \approx 0.6931$ for large $n$.
- [ ] **RTA equation:** $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil R_i / T_j \rceil C_j$.
- [ ] **Real-time vs Time-sharing:** RTOS has deterministic, bounded response; time-sharing optimizes throughput.
- [ ] **Examples to remember:** μC/OS-II (monolithic, KTU lab), FreeRTOS (microkernel-style), QNX (microkernel, automotive), VxWorks (hybrid, aerospace).
- [ ] **Kernel boots first:** Once `OS_Start()` is called, control never returns to `main()`.
- [ ] **Preemption = the soul of RTOS:** A higher-priority task can interrupt a running lower-priority one.
- [ ] **Context switch time:** Typically 5–20 μs on modern Cortex-M MCUs.
- [ ] **Interrupt latency:** Bounded; usually ≤ 30 CPU cycles on ARM Cortex-M.
- [ ] **System tick:** Typically 1 kHz (1 ms period); generated by hardware timer (e.g., SysTick).
- [ ] **Critical instant:** Worst-case scenario for RMS — all tasks released simultaneously at $t = 0$.
- [ ] **Schedulability = Sufficient condition:** Liu & Layland bound is *sufficient*, not *necessary*; some sets with higher utilization may still be schedulable.
- [ ] **Memory footprint:** FreeRTOS ≈ 4–10 KB ROM; μC/OS-II ≈ 6 KB ROM; QNX ≈ 100s of KB.
- [ ] **Safety-critical domains use microkernels:** QNX dominates automotive; VxWorks dominates aerospace.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Wisdom:** When asked to "explain the structure of an OS," always use the **layered model** as your default diagram, then **highlight the kernel's position** at Layer 2, and **justify** the layering with at least one benefit (e.g., portability, testability, maintainability). This is the highest-scoring structure for 7-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT631 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process (KTU 2024 Definition):** A **Process** is a program in execution, formally defined as a fundamental unit of work managed by an operating system (or Real-Time Operating System / RTOS in embedded contexts). It is a dynamic, active entity consisting of the **executable program code**, the **current activity** represented by the value of the **Program Counter (PC)**, the contents of the **CPU registers**, the **process stack**, a dedicated **Process Control Block (PCB)**, and associated **system resources** (memory, I/O, file handles).

In the context of an **RTOS-based embedded system**, a process represents an independent, schedulable instance of a computation that the kernel tracks to provide isolation, deterministic execution, and resource allocation. Every process is uniquely identified by a **Process ID (PID)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Terminology Clarification:** The KTU 2024 syllabus for *Embedded System Design* uses the term "Process" interchangeably with "Task" in many RTOS references. However, in strict OS theory:
> - **Process** = Heavyweight unit with its own memory address space.
> - **Task/Thread** = Lightweight unit sharing memory with other threads of the same process.
>
> In RTOS (e.g., FreeRTOS, VxWorks), the term **Task** is preferred because all tasks share a single address space for deterministic performance.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **professional kitchen in a five-star hotel**:

| Kitchen Element | Process Equivalent |
|---|---|
| A specific **recipe** being cooked (e.g., Biryani) | A **Process** |
| The **chef's hands, knife, and cutting board** currently being used | **CPU Registers & Program Counter** |
| The **recipe card** tracking ingredients, timer, and step number | **Process Control Block (PCB)** |
| The **stove burner** (only 4 burners for 50 recipes) | **CPU Core (limited resource)** |
| The **head chef's clipboard** deciding which recipe gets the next burner | **Scheduler** |
| Switching the chef from one recipe to another | **Context Switching** |

**The Intuition:** A recipe is just *static text* (a program on disk). It only becomes meaningful when a chef is actively executing it with a specific step, a timer, and a cutting board. Similarly, a process is a *living* entity — it is your program **plus everything the OS needs to track and resume its execution** at any given microsecond.

---

## 1.3 Why "Process" Matters in Embedded Systems

In bare-metal embedded systems (no OS), there is **only ONE process** — the `main()` super-loop. As embedded systems grow complex (IoT gateways, automotive ECUs, smartphones), a **multi-process / multi-task RTOS** becomes mandatory. Processes enable:

- **Concurrency:** Multiple operations appear to run simultaneously (sensor reading, communication, display update).
- **Modularity:** Separate concerns into independent code units.
- **Determinism:** Hard real-time deadlines can be enforced via scheduling.
- **Fault Isolation:** A crash in one process can be contained.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Under the KTU 2024 PEBMT631 syllabus, the study of "Process" forms the foundation for understanding **RTOS scheduling, context switching, and inter-process communication (IPC)**, which are directly examined questions worth 7–14 marks in the End Semester Examination (ESE).

---

## 1.4 Key Physical/Standard Metrics to Bold

- **Process Control Block (PCB):** The kernel data structure tracking all process state.
- **Process ID (PID):** Unique integer identifier assigned by the OS kernel.
- **Context Switch Time:** Typically **1 μs – 10 μs** in commercial RTOS kernels.
- **Stack Size:** Usually **256 bytes – 8 KB** per process in resource-constrained MCUs.
- **Process States:** Five canonical states — **New, Ready, Running, Blocked/Waiting, Terminated**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Process — The Five Logical Components

A process is **not** just code. It is the **union of five distinct logical blocks**:

1. **Text Section (Code):** The compiled machine instructions (typically stored in **Flash/ROM** in embedded targets).
2. **Data Section:** Global and static variables (stored in **RAM**).
3. **Heap:** Dynamically allocated memory (grows upward via `malloc()`).
4. **Stack:** Function call frames, local variables, return addresses (grows downward).
5. **Process Control Block (PCB):** The kernel's bookkeeping data structure.

---

## 2.2 The Process Control Block (PCB) — Detailed Breakdown

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Exam Favourite:** *Draw and explain the Process Control Block.* This question appears almost every academic year. Memorize the components below.

The PCB is a **struct** in C maintained by the kernel. For each active process, the kernel holds exactly one PCB. The typical PCB fields are:

| Field | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **Process ID (PID)** | Unique OS-assigned identifier |
| **Process State** | Current state (New, Ready, Running, Blocked, Terminated) |
| **Program Counter (PC)** | Address of the next instruction to execute |
| **CPU Registers** | Snapshot of all general-purpose and status registers |
| **Stack Pointer (SP)** | Points to the top of the process's stack |
| **Memory Management Info** | Base/limit registers, page tables |
| **Scheduling Info** | Priority, quantum remaining, scheduler queue pointers |
| **List of Open Files** | File descriptors, I/O resources held |
| **Accounting Info** | CPU time used, time limits, I/O statistics |
| **Parent PID & Child PIDs** | Process tree relationships |

---

## 2.3 The Five Process States — State Transition Theory

A process moves through a **finite state machine** during its lifetime. The five canonical states and their transitions are:

| State | Description | Trigger to Enter |
|---|---|---|
| **New** | Process is being created | `fork()` / `create_task()` syscall |
| **Ready** | Waiting to be assigned to a CPU | Admitted by the long-term scheduler |
| **Running** | Instructions are being executed on the CPU | Dispatcher assigns CPU |
| **Blocked / Waiting** | Process cannot proceed until an event occurs (I/O, semaphore) | I/O request, signal wait |
| **Terminated** | Process has finished execution | `exit()` / natural completion |

### State Transition Rules (Critical for KTU)

- **New → Ready:** Long-term scheduler admits the process into the ready queue.
- **Ready → Running:** The dispatcher selects this process (short-term scheduler).
- **Running → Ready:** Time quantum expires (preemptive scheduling) or higher-priority process preempts.
- **Running → Blocked:** Process requests I/O, waits on a semaphore/event.
- **Blocked → Ready:** I/O completes or event is signaled.
- **Running → Terminated:** Process executes final instruction or is killed by OS.

---

## 2.4 Context Switching — The Heart of Multitasking

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Context Switch** is the procedure of saving the state of a currently running process and loading the saved state of the next process to run. It is a **pure overhead** — no useful work is done during the switch itself.

### Context Switch Steps

1. The scheduler decides to preempt the current process (timer interrupt, syscall, or higher-priority wakeup).
2. The kernel **saves the PCB** of the current process (registers, PC, SP, flags) into RAM.
3. The kernel **loads the PCB** of the newly selected process from its PCB storage.
4. The CPU resumes execution of the new process from its saved PC.

### Context Switch Time ($T_{cs}$)

$$T_{cs} = T_{save} + T_{reload} + T_{dispatcher}$$

Where:
- $T_{save}$ = Time to save current PCB
- $T_{reload}$ = Time to load new PCB
- $T_{dispatcher}$ = Scheduler decision latency

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **CPU Utilization** | $U = 1 - P_{idle}$ | Fraction (0 to 1) |
| **Throughput** | $\Theta = \dfrac{N_{processes}}{T_{total}}$ | Processes per second |
| **Turnaround Time** | $T_{turnaround} = T_{completion} - T_{arrival}$ | Seconds |
| **Waiting Time** | $T_{wait} = T_{turnaround} - T_{burst}$ | Seconds |
| **Response Time** | $T_{response} = T_{first\_run} - T_{arrival}$ | Seconds |
| **Context Switch Overhead** | $Overhead = \dfrac{T_{cs}}{T_{quantum}} \times 100\%$ | Percent of CPU wasted |
| **Schedulable Condition (Rate Monotonic)** | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_i}{T_i} \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ | Liu & Layland bound |
| **Average Waiting Time** | $\bar{W} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i$ | Per process |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** The **Liu & Layland bound** $\sum C_i / T_i \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ is for *Rate Monotonic Scheduling* of periodic tasks. The bound approaches **0.693 (≈ 69.3 %)** as $n \to \infty$, meaning at most ~70% of CPU can be guaranteed schedulable in the worst case.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive ECU (e.g., AUTOSAR):** Each function (engine control, ABS, airbag) is a process/task with hard deadlines.
- **IoT Edge Devices (FreeRTOS):** Sensor sampling, BLE transmission, and OTA update are separate tasks.
- **Industrial PLCs:** Process abstraction enables IEC 61131-3 programming models.
- **Drone Flight Controllers (NuttX, PX4):** Processes isolate flight-critical from non-critical code.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Average Turnaround Time for Round-Robin Scheduling

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **standard KTU numerical problem** (typically Part B, 7-mark sub-question). The derivation below shows the full working expected by KTU board examiners.

### Problem Setup

Consider **3 processes** arriving at time **0** with the following CPU burst times and scheduled using **Round-Robin** with a **time quantum of 4 ms**.

| Process | Arrival Time ($A_i$) | Burst Time ($B_i$) |
|---|---|---|
| P1 | 0 ms | 8 ms |
| P2 | 0 ms | 4 ms |
| P3 | 0 ms | 9 ms |

### Step 1: Construct the Gantt Chart

We use a **ready queue** $Q = [P1, P2, P3]$ initially. Each process runs for the quantum or until completion, whichever is shorter, then re-enters the queue.

**Iteration 1:** P1 runs for $\min(8, 4) = 4$ ms. P1 remaining = $8 - 4 = 4$ ms. Re-queue P1.

$$Q \leftarrow [P2, P3, P1]$$

**Iteration 2:** P2 runs for $\min(4, 4) = 4$ ms. P2 remaining = $4 - 4 = 0$ ms. P2 **terminates**.

$$Q \leftarrow [P3, P1]$$

**Iteration 3:** P3 runs for $\min(9, 4) = 4$ ms. P3 remaining = $9 - 4 = 5$ ms. Re-queue P3.

$$Q \leftarrow [P1, P3]$$

**Iteration 4:** P1 runs for $\min(4, 4) = 4$ ms. P1 remaining = $4 - 4 = 0$ ms. P1 **terminates**.

$$Q \leftarrow [P3]$$

**Iteration 5:** P3 runs for $\min(5, 4) = 4$ ms. P3 remaining = $5 - 4 = 1$ ms. Re-queue P3.

$$Q \leftarrow [P3]$$

**Iteration 6:** P3 runs for $\min(1, 4) = 1$ ms. P3 remaining = $1 - 1 = 0$ ms. P3 **terminates**.

### Step 2: Write the Gantt Chart

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Gantt} &= \vert\, P_1\,\vert\, P_2\,\vert\, P_3\,\vert\, P_1\,\vert\, P_3\,\vert\, P_3\,\vert \\
\text{Time}   &= 0 \quad 4 \quad 8 \quad 12 \quad 16 \quad 20 \quad 21
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Calculate Completion Times ($C_i$)

| Process | Completion Time $C_i$ |
|---|---|
| P1 | 16 ms |
| P2 | 8 ms |
| P3 | 21 ms |

### Step 4: Calculate Turnaround Time

By definition:

$$T_{turnaround,i} = C_i - A_i$$

Since all processes arrived at $A_i = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{turnaround,1} &= 16 - 0 = 16 \text{ ms} \\
T_{turnaround,2} &= 8 - 0 = 8 \text{ ms} \\
T_{turnaround,3} &= 21 - 0 = 21 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Calculate Average Turnaround Time

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{T}_{turnaround} &= \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_{turnaround,i} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{3}(16 + 8 + 21) \\
&= \dfrac{45}{3} = 15 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6: Calculate Waiting Time

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{wait,i} &= T_{turnaround,i} - B_i \\
T_{wait,1} &= 16 - 8 = 8 \text{ ms} \\
T_{wait,2} &= 8 - 4 = 4 \text{ ms} \\
T_{wait,3} &= 21 - 9 = 12 \text{ ms} \\
\bar{T}_{wait} &= \dfrac{1}{3}(8 + 4 + 12) = \dfrac{24}{3} = 8 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Process vs Thread vs ISR

| Property | Process | Thread | ISR (Interrupt Service Routine) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Address Space** | Independent | Shared with parent process | No own space; runs in interrupt context |
| **Creation Cost** | Heavy (fork, copy memory) | Light (stack + TCB) | None (hardware-triggered) |
| **Communication** | IPC (pipes, shared memory) | Shared variables | Flags / event registers |
| **Preemption** | Yes | Yes | Cannot be preempted by threads |
| **Priority** | Configurable | Configurable | Always highest (hardware priority) |
| **Use in Embedded RTOS** | Rare (memory cost) | **Very common** (FreeRTOS tasks) | Hardware event handling |

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Schedulability Check for Rate Monotonic Scheduling

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Liu & Layland Theorem (1973):** For $n$ periodic tasks scheduled under Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS), the tasks are **guaranteed to meet all deadlines** if:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_i}{T_i} \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$$

Where $C_i$ = Worst-case execution time and $T_i$ = Period of task $i$.

**Worked Example:**

Consider 3 tasks:

| Task | Execution Time $C_i$ | Period $T_i$ | Utilization $U_i = C_i / T_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| T1 | 1 ms | 4 ms | 0.25 |
| T2 | 2 ms | 6 ms | 0.333 |
| T3 | 1 ms | 8 ms | 0.125 |

**Step 1: Compute total utilization**

$$
U_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{3} \dfrac{C_i}{T_i} = 0.25 + 0.333 + 0.125 = 0.708
$$

**Step 2: Compute Liu & Layland bound for $n=3$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{bound} &= 3 \times (2^{1/3} - 1) \\
&= 3 \times (1.2599 - 1) \\
&= 3 \times 0.2599 \\
&= 0.7798
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compare**

$$
U_{total} = 0.708 \leq U_{bound} = 0.7798 \quad \checkmark \text{ (Schedulable)}
$$

**Conclusion:** The task set is guaranteed to meet all hard real-time deadlines under RMS.

> [!NOTE]
> If $U_{total} > U_{bound}$, the system **may still be schedulable** (the bound is sufficient but not necessary). For an exact check, use **Response Time Analysis (RTA)**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process State Transition Diagram (Canonical 5-State Model)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> stateNew : Process Creation
    stateNew --> stateReady : Admit
    stateReady --> stateRunning : Scheduler Dispatch
    stateRunning --> stateReady : Preempt / Quantum Expire
    stateRunning --> stateBlocked : I/O or Event Wait
    stateBlocked --> stateReady : I/O Complete / Event Signal
    stateRunning --> stateTerminated : Exit
    stateTerminated --> [*] : PCB Released
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** Each arrow represents a **legal state transition**. Memorize the **six labeled edges** — the *Board Examiner* will mark you down for an invalid transition (e.g., Blocked → Running directly without going through Ready).

---

## 4.2 Process Control Block (PCB) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PCB["Process Control Block PCB"]
    PID["Process ID PID"]
    ST["Process State"]
    PC["Program Counter"]
    REG["CPU Registers Snapshot"]
    SP["Stack Pointer SP"]
    SCH["Scheduling Information Priority Queue"]
    MEM["Memory Management Base Limit Page Tables"]
    IO["Open File Descriptor Table I/O Resources"]
    ACC["Accounting Information CPU Time Used"]
    PAR["Parent and Child Process IDs"]

    PCB --> PID
    PCB --> ST
    PCB --> PC
    PCB --> REG
    PCB --> SP
    PCB --> SCH
    PCB --> MEM
    PCB --> IO
    PCB --> ACC
    PCB --> PAR
```

---

## 4.3 Context Switch Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Timer Interrupt or Syscall Triggered] --> B[Save Current Process PCB to RAM]
    B --> C[Update Current Process State to Ready or Blocked]
    C --> D[Scheduler Picks Next Process from Ready Queue]
    D --> E[Load Selected Process PCB into CPU Registers]
    E --> F[Flush TLB and Cache If New Address Space]
    F --> G[Resume Execution of New Process]
    G --> H{New Process Runs}
    H --> A
```

---

## 4.4 Block-Level Architecture: Process Lifecycle in an RTOS

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AppLayer["Application Layer User Code"]
        TASK1["Task 1 Sensor Sampling"]
        TASK2["Task 2 Communication"]
        TASK3["Task 3 Display Update"]
    end

    subgraph RTOSLayer["RTOS Kernel Layer"]
        SCHED["Scheduler Priority Based"]
        DISPATCH["Dispatcher"]
        PCBSTORE["PCB Table per Process"]
        SYSCALL["System Call Interface"]
    end

    subgraph HardwareLayer["Hardware Layer MCU"]
        CPU["CPU Core ARM Cortex M4"]
        TIMER["SysTick Timer Interrupt Source"]
        MEM["Flash and RAM"]
    end

    TASK1 --> SYSCALL
    TASK2 --> SYSCALL
    TASK3 --> SYSCALL
    SYSCALL --> SCHED
    TIMER --> DISPATCH
    SCHED --> PCBSTORE
    SCHED --> DISPATCH
    DISPATCH --> CPU
    CPU --> MEM
    PCBSTORE --> MEM
```

---

## 4.5 Process Scheduling Comparison Matrix (Visual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph NPP["Non Preemptive"]
        FCFS["FCFS First Come First Serve"]
        SJF["SJF Shortest Job First"]
    end

    subgraph PP["Preemptive"]
        RR["Round Robin Quantum Based"]
        PRIO["Priority Scheduling"]
        RMS["Rate Monotonic Static Priority"]
        EDF["Earliest Deadline First Dynamic"]
    end

    NPP --> PP
    PP --> RMS
    PP --> EDF
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a Process. List the typical fields of a Process Control Block (PCB).**

**Model Answer:**

A **Process** is a program in execution. It is a dynamic entity consisting of the program code, the current value of the Program Counter, contents of CPU registers, the process stack, and associated system resources. Every process is represented in the kernel by a **Process Control Block (PCB)** containing:

- **Process ID (PID)**
- **Process State** (New, Ready, Running, Blocked, Terminated)
- **Program Counter (PC)** and **CPU Register Snapshot**
- **Stack Pointer (SP)** and memory management info
- **Scheduling Information** (priority, queue pointers)
- **List of Open Files / I/O Resources**
- **Accounting Information** (CPU time used)
- **Parent/Child Process IDs**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between a Process and a Thread in the context of an RTOS.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Process | Thread |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Independent program in execution with its own memory space | Lightweight sub-unit of a process sharing memory |
| **Memory** | Separate address space | Shared with parent process |
| **Creation Cost** | Heavy (memory copy, PCB allocation) | Light (stack + TCB only) |
| **Communication** | Requires explicit IPC mechanisms | Direct shared variable access |
| **Context Switch Time** | Higher (full PCB save/restore) | Lower (fewer registers) |
| **Embedded RTOS Use** | Rare in MCUs due to RAM cost | **Standard** (FreeRTOS, VxWorks tasks) |

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **five-state process model** and all possible state transitions. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Define **Context Switching**. Explain the steps involved in a context switch with a suitable diagram. Compute the **average turnaround time and waiting time** for the following processes using **Round-Robin scheduling** with a **time quantum of 3 ms**. **(7 marks)**

| Process | Arrival Time (ms) | Burst Time (ms) |
|---|---|---|
| P1 | 0 | 7 |
| P2 | 1 | 4 |
| P3 | 2 | 9 |
| P4 | 3 | 5 |

---

#### Solution to Question A (a)

A process goes through **five states** during its lifecycle. The state diagram is shown below (refer Section 4.1).

**Transitions and Triggers:**

- **New → Ready:** Long-term scheduler admits the process.
- **Ready → Running:** Short-term scheduler (dispatcher) selects it.
- **Running → Ready:** Preemption by higher-priority process or quantum expiry.
- **Running → Blocked:** Process issues an I/O request or waits on a semaphore.
- **Blocked → Ready:** I/O completes or waited event occurs.
- **Running → Terminated:** Process calls `exit()` or is killed.

*(Draw the canonical 5-state diagram with arrows — full marks for diagram + explanation of each transition.)*

---

#### Solution to Question A (b)

**Step 1: Gantt Chart Construction (Quantum = 3 ms)**

Ready queue evolves as processes arrive and finish their quantum. Initial: $Q = [P1]$.

| Iteration | Process | Runs For | Remaining | Re-queue | Ready Queue |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | P1 | 3 | 4 | Yes | [P2, P3, P4, P1] |
| 2 | P2 | 3 | 1 | Yes | [P3, P4, P1, P2] |
| 3 | P3 | 3 | 6 | Yes | [P4, P1, P2, P3] |
| 4 | P4 | 3 | 2 | Yes | [P1, P2, P3, P4] |
| 5 | P1 | 3 | 1 | Yes | [P2, P3, P4, P1] |
| 6 | P2 | 1 | 0 | **Done** | [P3, P4, P1] |
| 7 | P3 | 3 | 3 | Yes | [P4, P1, P3] |
| 8 | P4 | 2 | 0 | **Done** | [P1, P3] |
| 9 | P1 | 1 | 0 | **Done** | [P3] |
| 10 | P3 | 3 | 0 | **Done** | [] |

**Gantt Chart:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Time} &= 0\;3\;6\;9\;12\;15\;16\;19\;21\;22\;25 \\
\text{Proc} &= P_1\;P_2\;P_3\;P_4\;P_1\;P_2\;P_3\;P_4\;P_1\;P_3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Completion Times**

| Process | Arrival $A_i$ | Burst $B_i$ | Completion $C_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| P1 | 0 | 7 | 22 |
| P2 | 1 | 4 | 16 |
| P3 | 2 | 9 | 25 |
| P4 | 3 | 5 | 19 |

**Step 3: Turnaround Time**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{TAT,1} &= 22 - 0 = 22 \text{ ms} \\
T_{TAT,2} &= 16 - 1 = 15 \text{ ms} \\
T_{TAT,3} &= 25 - 2 = 23 \text{ ms} \\
T_{TAT,4} &= 19 - 3 = 16 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Average Turnaround Time**

$$
\bar{T}_{TAT} = \dfrac{22 + 15 + 23 + 16}{4} = \dfrac{76}{4} = 19 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 5: Waiting Time**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{W,1} &= 22 - 0 - 7 = 15 \text{ ms} \\
T_{W,2} &= 16 - 1 - 4 = 11 \text{ ms} \\
T_{W,3} &= 25 - 2 - 9 = 14 \text{ ms} \\
T_{W,4} &= 19 - 3 - 5 = 11 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Average Waiting Time**

$$
\bar{T}_{W} = \dfrac{15 + 11 + 14 + 11}{4} = \dfrac{51}{4} = 12.75 \text{ ms}
$$

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**a)** Explain the **Liu and Layland Rate Monotonic Scheduling theorem**. Three periodic real-time tasks are to be scheduled: T1 with $C_1 = 2$ ms, $T_1 = 10$ ms; T2 with $C_2 = 3$ ms, $T_2 = 15$ ms; T3 with $C_3 = 1$ ms, $T_3 = 20$ ms. **Check whether the task set is schedulable under RMS.** **(7 marks)**

**b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **components of a Process Control Block (PCB)**. Discuss the role of PCB during **context switching**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question B (a)

**Liu & Layland Theorem (1973):**

For $n$ periodic tasks with shorter periods assigned higher priorities (Rate Monotonic), a **sufficient condition** for schedulability is:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_i}{T_i} \leq n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)
$$

**Step 1: Compute total utilization**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{total} &= \dfrac{C_1}{T_1} + \dfrac{C_2}{T_2} + \dfrac{C_3}{T_3} \\
&= \dfrac{2}{10} + \dfrac{3}{15} + \dfrac{1}{20} \\
&= 0.200 + 0.200 + 0.050 \\
&= 0.450
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Liu & Layland bound for $n=3$**

$$
U_{bound} = 3 \times (2^{1/3} - 1) = 3 \times 0.2599 = 0.7798
$$

**Step 3: Decision**

$$
U_{total} = 0.450 \leq U_{bound} = 0.7798 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Conclusion:** The task set is **guaranteed schedulable** under Rate Monotonic Scheduling.

---

#### Solution to Question B (b)

The **PCB** is the kernel's data structure for each process (refer Section 4.2 diagram).

**Role in Context Switching:**

1. The kernel maintains a **PCB table** in protected kernel memory.
2. On a context switch, the kernel **copies** the current process's register values (PC, SP, general-purpose registers, status flags) into its PCB slot in RAM.
3. The state field is updated (e.g., Running → Ready or Blocked).
4. The scheduler selects the next process; the kernel **restores** the new process's PCB values back into the CPU registers.
5. Execution resumes from the saved PC value — the new process is **unaware** that time has passed.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often **forget to update the state field** of the outgoing process. If you only save registers without changing the state from "Running" to "Ready" / "Blocked," the kernel's state tracking becomes inconsistent and the scheduler will make wrong decisions. **Always show the state update step explicitly** in your answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU 2024 ESE:**

- **Definition:** A process is a **program in execution** = code + execution context + resources.
- **PCB** contains: PID, state, PC, registers, SP, scheduling info, memory info, I/O list, accounting, parent/child.
- **Five States:** New → Ready → Running → (Blocked ↔ Ready) → Terminated.
- **Valid transitions only:** Blocked cannot go directly to Running (must go via Ready).
- **Context Switch** = Save old PCB + Load new PCB = Pure overhead, no useful work.
- **Round-Robin Quantum:** Too small → excessive overhead; Too large → degrades to FCFS.
- **Turnaround Time:** $T_{TAT} = T_{completion} - T_{arrival}$.
- **Waiting Time:** $T_W = T_{TAT} - T_{burst}$.
- **Liu & Layland bound for $n=3$:** $\approx 0.7798$ (≈ 78%).
- **Liu & Layland bound limit:** $\lim_{n \to \infty} n(2^{1/n} - 1) = \ln 2 \approx 0.693$.
- **Process vs Thread:** Processes have **independent address space**; threads **share** it.
- **RTOS terminology:** Most commercial RTOS kernels (FreeRTOS, VxWorks, QNX) use **Task** synonymously with *lightweight thread* — not the heavy OS process.
- **Why embedded systems use processes/tasks:** Concurrency, modularity, determinism, fault isolation.
- **CPU utilization** $U = 1 - P_{idle}$; **Throughput** $\Theta$ = processes completed per unit time.
- **Hard real-time vs soft real-time:** Hard = missing deadline = system failure; Soft = degraded quality only.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Strategy Tip:** For 14-mark questions, always (1) draw a **neat labeled diagram** (3–4 marks), (2) write **numbered steps** (3–4 marks), (3) show **full calculations with units** (4–5 marks), and (4) conclude with a **clear statement of result** (2–3 marks). A well-structured answer with a clean diagram almost always fetches **12+ marks** even if minor calculation errors exist.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT631 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Tasks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of a Task

In the context of Real-Time Operating Systems (RTOS) used in embedded systems, a **Task** (also called a *thread of control* or *lightweight process*) is formally defined as an independent, sequential, single-threaded program execution path that competes for CPU time and is scheduled independently by the kernel.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PEBMT631, a task in Module 4 is treated as a fundamental schedulable entity possessing:
- A unique **Task Control Block (TCB)**
- An associated **priority level**
- An independent **execution context** (register set, stack, program counter)
- A defined **state machine** governing its life-cycle within the kernel

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** A *task* is the basic unit of execution in an RTOS. The CPU executes only ONE task at a time (single-core assumption), but the kernel rapidly switches between tasks to create the illusion of concurrent execution. Each task is represented by a Task Control Block (TCB) stored in kernel memory.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant Chef" Model

Imagine a busy restaurant kitchen with **3 chefs (tasks)** sharing **1 stove (CPU)**. The head chef (kernel/scheduler) decides which chef gets the stove at any given instant based on the order urgency (priority).

- **Chef A** is preparing soup — has low priority, can wait.
- **Chef B** is preparing a steak — medium priority, customer is waiting.
- **Chef C** has a fire alarm going off — highest priority, must run IMMEDIATELY.

Each chef remembers:
- Where they left their knife (program counter)
- Their current recipe step (execution context)
- Their assigned workstation (stack)
- The ingredients on their counter (local variables)

This is exactly how an RTOS handles multiple tasks — the kernel *context-switches* between them, preserving each task's state so it can resume seamlessly.

### 1.3 Why Tasks Matter in Embedded Systems

Embedded systems (pacemakers, anti-lock brakes, drones, industrial controllers) require **deterministic, time-bounded responses**. By structuring the firmware as multiple cooperating tasks, engineers achieve:

- **Modularity** — each task handles one logical function
- **Concurrency** — multiple I/O activities appear to run simultaneously
- **Maintainability** — bugs isolated to specific tasks
- **Real-time guarantees** — deadlines can be formally analyzed

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality:** In KTU 2024 ESE questions, students are expected to know that an "embedded task" differs from a "desktop process" — embedded tasks have **no protection unit privileges**, share a single address space, and rely on the kernel for context management rather than hardware memory protection.

### 1.4 Key Terminology Table (Foundational Glossary)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Task** | Independent thread of execution; smallest unit of work scheduled by kernel |
| **TCB (Task Control Block)** | Kernel data structure storing a task's complete state |
| **Context Switch** | Saving state of one task and loading state of another |
| **Preemption** | Forced CPU handover from a lower-priority task to a higher-priority one |
| **Priority** | Integer value determining scheduling order (lower number = higher priority in most RTOS) |
| **Idle Task** | Lowest-priority task that runs when no other task is ready |
| **System Task** | Kernel-managed task (e.g., idle, interrupt service tail) |

### 1.5 Physical and Performance Constants Used in Task Analysis

- **Context Switch Time (C_s):** Typically **1–10 microseconds** in modern ARM Cortex-M RTOS (e.g., FreeRTOS ≈ **1.18 µs** on Cortex-M4 @ 168 MHz)
- **Tick Rate (f_tick):** Typical values: **1 kHz (1 ms tick)**, **10 kHz (100 µs tick)**
- **Maximum Tasks:** FreeRTOS supports up to **unlimited (RAM-dependent)**; µC/OS-II supports up to **255**
- **Stack Size per Task:** Typically **256 bytes to 4 KB** depending on task complexity
