<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Task States — The Heart of the Kernel

A task in any RTOS exists in a **finite set of mutually exclusive states**. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the **Five-State Task Model** as the canonical representation.

#### 2.1.1 The Five Canonical Task States

**1. Dormant/New State**
- Task has been created in code but is NOT yet activated
- TCB exists in memory, but task consumes NO CPU
- Created by functions like `OSTaskCreate()` or `xTaskCreate()`

**2. Ready State**
- Task is eligible to run and is waiting in the Ready Queue
- All preconditions satisfied (no events pending, no resource blocked)
- Sorted by priority within the ready queue

**3. Running State**
- Task currently owns the CPU
- On single-core systems, ONLY ONE task can be in Running state at any instant
- Controlled by the scheduler's dispatch logic

**4. Blocked/Waiting State**
- Task is waiting for an event (semaphore, message queue, delay timeout, I/O)
- Removed from Ready Queue
- Does NOT consume CPU cycles

**5. Suspended State** (optional in some kernels)
- Task is forcibly paused by the application
- Placed in a suspension list
- Must be explicitly resumed

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **State Transition Rule:** A task can ONLY be in EXACTLY ONE state at any given instant. State transitions are atomic with respect to the kernel.

#### 2.1.2 Why "Running → Ready" and "Ready → Running" Are Different Transitions

- **Running → Ready:** Occurs when a HIGHER-priority task becomes ready (preemption)
- **Running → Blocked:** Occurs when task calls a blocking API (e.g., wait on semaphore)
- **Blocked → Ready:** Occurs when waited event occurs
- **Ready → Running:** Occurs when scheduler dispatches the task

### 2.2 Task Control Block (TCB) — Anatomy

The TCB is a kernel-allocated data structure (typically static or from a memory pool) that contains **everything** the kernel needs to manage a task.

#### 2.2.1 Mandatory TCB Fields (Universal)

| Field | Purpose | Size (typical) |
|---|---|---|
| **Task ID / Handle** | Unique numerical identifier | 4 bytes |
| **Stack Pointer (SP)** | Current top of task stack | 4 bytes |
| **Stack Base Address** | Bottom of allocated stack | 4 bytes |
| **Stack Depth** | Stack size in words/bytes | 2 bytes |
| **Program Counter (PC)** | Next instruction to execute | 4 bytes |
| **CPU Registers** | Saved general-purpose registers (R0–R12) | 52 bytes (ARM) |
| **Status Register (xPSR)** | Processor flags | 4 bytes |
| **Priority** | Static/dynamic priority value | 1–4 bytes |
| **State** | Current state code | 1 byte |
| **Time Slice Counter** | For round-robin scheduling | 2–4 bytes |
| **Delay/Timeout Value** | Ticks remaining in blocked state | 4 bytes |
| **Event Pointer** | Pointer to waited event control block | 4 bytes |
| **List Pointers** | Linked-list next/prev pointers | 8 bytes |

#### 2.2.2 Optional TCB Fields (Kernel-specific)

- Error code storage
- Task name string
- Message mailbox pointer
- Suspension count
- CPU usage accumulator
- TLS (Thread-Local Storage) pointer

> [!NOTE]
> **Total TCB Size for ARM Cortex-M:** Typically **76–120 bytes**. With stack, each task consumes approximately **1–4 KB of RAM**. This is why KTU questions frequently test **memory budgeting** in embedded design.

### 2.3 Task Creation, Deletion, and Management

#### 2.3.1 Task Creation Process (Exhaustive Steps)

1. **Allocate TCB** from kernel memory pool
2. **Allocate Stack** (caller-specified size) from RAM
3. **Initialize TCB fields** (state = DORMANT, priority = caller-specified)
4. **Initialize Stack** with synthesized "fake" context (initial PC = task function address, LR = cleanup routine)
5. **Insert TCB** into kernel's task list
6. **Make task Ready** (state transition DORMANT → READY)
7. **Call scheduler** to check if new task preempts current

#### 2.3.2 Task Deletion / Termination

- Task calls `OSTaskDel()` or `vTaskDelete()` (in FreeRTOS)
- Kernel marks TCB as "free"
- Stack memory is released
- TCB is removed from active lists
- Scheduler called to pick next task

> [!WARNING]
> **Embedded-Specific Pitfall:** In safety-critical systems (DO-178C, IEC 61508), task deletion is often **forbidden at runtime** because it creates non-deterministic memory fragmentation. KTU examiners test this distinction between dynamic (µC/OS) and static-only (SAFERTOS) kernels.

### 2.4 Task Scheduling — Conceptual Framework

The scheduler is a kernel function that decides **which Ready task becomes Running**. Key scheduling paradigms:

#### 2.4.1 Priority-Based Preemptive Scheduling

- Each task has a unique static priority
- Highest-priority Ready task always runs
- Lower-priority tasks are **preempted** when a higher-priority task becomes Ready
- Standard for hard real-time systems

#### 2.4.2 Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) — Fixed Priority

- Task with **shortest period = highest priority**
- Optimal static-priority algorithm (Liu & Layland, 1973)
- Schedulability bound: $$U_{bound} = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$$

#### 2.4.3 Earliest Deadline First (EDF) — Dynamic Priority

- Task with **closest absolute deadline = highest priority**
- Optimal dynamic-priority algorithm
- Schedulability bound: $$U \leq 1.0$$ (100% utilization)

#### 2.4.4 Round-Robin Scheduling

- Tasks of **equal priority** share CPU via time slicing
- Each task gets a fixed time quantum (e.g., 10 ms)
- Common in time-sharing systems; rare in hard real-time

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **frequently tested** in KTU 2024 ESE Module 4 questions. Memorize the variables and conditions.

| # | Formula | Description | Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $U_i = C_i / T_i$ | Utilization of single task | $C_i$ = computation time, $T_i$ = period | dimensionless |
| 2 | $U_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i / T_i$ | Total CPU utilization | $n$ = number of tasks | dimensionless |
| 3 | $U_{RMS\_{bound}} = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ | Liu-Layland bound for RMS | $n$ = number of tasks | dimensionless |
| 4 | $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil R_i / T_j \rceil C_j$ | Response time recurrence (exact) | $hp(i)$ = higher-priority tasks | time units |
| 5 | $P_{inv} = T_{response} - C_i$ | Blocking/Priority inversion time | response minus computation | time units |
| 6 | $T_{CS} = T_{save} + T_{load} + T_{overhead}$ | Context switch time | save, load, kernel overhead | microseconds |
| 7 | $WCET \leq T_i - C_{preempt}$ | Deadline constraint | period minus preemption cost | time units |
| 8 | $f_{tick} = 1 / T_{tick}$ | Tick rate | tick period | Hz |
| 9 | $N_{max\_tasks} = 2^{k_{priority}}$ | Max tasks (k-bit priority) | $k$ = priority field bits | count |
| 10 | $S_{stack} = S_{base} + S_{context} + S_{local} + S_{margin}$ | Stack size estimation | base, context, local, safety | bytes |

### 2.6 Priority Inversion — The Classic Embedded Bug

**Priority Inversion** occurs when a higher-priority task is **indirectly preempted** by a lower-priority task due to a shared resource.

**Classic Scenario:**
1. Task L (low priority) acquires mutex M
2. Task H (high priority) starts, tries to acquire M → **blocks**
3. Task M (medium priority) preempts Task L
4. Task H is now **indirectly waiting for Task M** to finish — a priority inversion

**Solution:** **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)**
- When Task H blocks on M held by Task L, Task L's priority is **temporarily raised** to H's priority
- Task L finishes its critical section quickly, releasing M
- Task H then acquires M and runs

**Worst-case blocking time under PIP:** $$B_i = \sum_{j \in lower(i)} C_{j,cs} \cdot N_{access}$$

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Task-based RTOS design is the **backbone of modern embedded systems** across industries:

- **Automotive (AUTOSAR):** Engine control unit (ECU) runs 50–100 tasks managing fuel injection, ABS, airbags
- **Aerospace (NASA CFS, RTEMS):** Satellite attitude control with hard real-time guarantees
- **Medical Devices (FDA Class III):** Pacemaker, infusion pumps — task design ensures no missed deadlines
- **Industrial IoT (FreeRTOS, Zephyr):** Sensor fusion with deterministic sampling
- **Consumer Electronics (VxWorks in PlayStation):** Game loop task, audio task, network task running concurrently

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Liu & Layland RMS Utilization Bound

The **Liu-Layland bound** is the cornerstone of rate-monotonic schedulability analysis. We derive it below.

#### 3.1.1 Setup and Assumptions

We have $n$ independent, periodic tasks with:
- Period $T_i$ and computation time $C_i$ (with $C_i \leq T_i$)
- Priorities assigned by Rate Monotonic rule: $T_1 < T_2 < \ldots < T_n \Rightarrow P_1 > P_2 > \ldots > P_n$
- Critical instant for task $T_i$ occurs when it arrives simultaneously with all higher-priority tasks

**Goal:** Find the maximum total utilization $U_{max}$ that guarantees schedulability.

#### 3.1.2 The Critical Instant Condition

For task $T_i$ to meet its deadline, the total computation demanded in the interval $[0, T_i]$ must not exceed $T_i$:

$$\sum_{j=1}^{i} C_j \cdot \left\lceil \frac{T_i}{T_j} \right\rceil \leq T_i$$

Dividing both sides by $T_i$:

$$\sum_{j=1}^{i} \frac{C_j}{T_j} \cdot \left\lceil \frac{T_i}{T_j} \right\rceil \leq 1$$

#### 3.1.3 The Worst-Case Utilization Bound

Liu and Layland showed that the minimum value of the left-hand side (over all valid period sets) for $n$ tasks is:

$$U_{bound}(n) = n \cdot \left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)$$

Let us verify this for small $n$:

- **$n = 1$:** $U_{bound} = 1 \cdot (2^1 - 1) = 1 \cdot 1 = 1.000$ (100%)
- **$n = 2$:** $U_{bound} = 2 \cdot (2^{0.5} - 1) = 2 \cdot (1.414 - 1) = 2 \cdot 0.414 = 0.828$ (82.8%)
- **$n = 3$:** $U_{bound} = 3 \cdot (2^{1/3} - 1) = 3 \cdot (1.260 - 1) = 3 \cdot 0.260 = 0.779$ (77.9%)
- **$n = 4$:** $U_{bound} = 4 \cdot (2^{0.25} - 1) = 4 \cdot (1.189 - 1) = 4 \cdot 0.189 = 0.757$ (75.7%)
- **$n \to \infty$:** $U_{bound} \to \ln(2) \approx 0.693$ (69.3%)

#### 3.1.4 Step-by-Step Numerical Derivation for n = 3

**Given:** $T_1 = 4$, $T_2 = 6$, $T_3 = 12$ (all time units)

**Priority Assignment:** $T_1$ has shortest period → highest priority.

**Compute utilizations (assume $C_1 = 1$, $C_2 = 2$, $C_3 = 3$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_1 &= \frac{C_1}{T_1} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.250 \\
U_2 &= \frac{C_2}{T_2} = \frac{2}{6} = 0.333 \\
U_3 &= \frac{C_3}{T_3} = \frac{3}{12} = 0.250 \\
U_{total} &= 0.250 + 0.333 + 0.250 = 0.833
\end{aligned}
$$

**Compare with RMS bound for n = 3:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{bound}(3) &= 3 \cdot (2^{1/3} - 1) \\
&= 3 \cdot (1.2599 - 1) \\
&= 3 \cdot 0.2599 \\
&= 0.7798
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision:** $U_{total} = 0.833 > U_{bound} = 0.780$

This means RMS **cannot guarantee schedulability** by utilization bound test alone. We need a **response time analysis** (exact test) to verify.

### 3.2 Derivation: Exact Response Time Analysis (Joseph-Pandya)

#### 3.2.1 The Recurrence Relation

The exact worst-case response time $R_i$ of task $i$ is the smallest fixed point of:

$$R_i^{(k+1)} = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i^{(k)}}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j$$

where $hp(i)$ is the set of tasks with higher priority than $i$.

#### 3.2.2 Iterative Algorithm

- **Initialize:** $R_i^{(0)} = C_i$
- **Iterate:** $R_i^{(k+1)} = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil R_i^{(k)} / T_j \rceil C_j$
- **Stop when:** $R_i^{(k+1)} = R_i^{(k)}$ (converged) OR $R_i^{(k+1)} > T_i$ (missed deadline)
- **Schedulable if:** $R_i \leq T_i$ (or $D_i$ for deadline $\neq$ period)

#### 3.2.3 Worked Example

**Given Tasks:**

| Task | Period $T_i$ | Computation $C_i$ | Priority (RMS) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\tau_1$ | 5 | 1 | Highest |
| $\tau_2$ | 10 | 3 | Medium |
| $\tau_3$ | 15 | 5 | Lowest |

**Find $R_3$ (response time of $\tau_3$):**

**Iteration 0:**

$$R_3^{(0)} = C_3 = 5$$

**Iteration 1:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_3^{(1)} &= C_3 + \left\lceil \frac{R_3^{(0)}}{T_1} \right\rceil C_1 + \left\lceil \frac{R_3^{(0)}}{T_2} \right\rceil C_2 \\
&= 5 + \left\lceil \frac{5}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{5}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 3 \\
&= 5 + (1)(1) + (1)(3) \\
&= 5 + 1 + 3 = 9
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 2:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_3^{(2)} &= 5 + \left\lceil \frac{9}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{9}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 3 \\
&= 5 + (2)(1) + (1)(3) \\
&= 5 + 2 + 3 = 10
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 3:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_3^{(3)} &= 5 + \left\lceil \frac{10}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{10}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 3 \\
&= 5 + (2)(1) + (1)(3) \\
&= 5 + 2 + 3 = 10
\end{aligned}
$$

**Converged at $R_3 = 10$.**

**Decision:** $R_3 = 10 \leq T_3 = 15$ → **Task $\tau_3$ is schedulable.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Important:** Utilization bound is **sufficient but not necessary**. The exact RTA test gives a precise answer. In the above example, $U_{total} = 1/5 + 3/10 + 5/15 = 0.2 + 0.3 + 0.333 = 0.833$, which is below 1.0 but above the RMS bound of 0.780 — yet the task set IS schedulable, as proven by RTA.

### 3.3 Derivation: Context Switch Time Budget

#### 3.3.1 Total Overhead Calculation

In a system with $n$ tasks, each context switch contributes overhead. For a tick-based preemptive scheduler with $f_{tick}$ tick frequency and $n$ tasks, the overhead per unit time is:

$$U_{overhead} = n \cdot f_{tick} \cdot T_{CS}$$

where $T_{CS}$ is the context switch time.

#### 3.3.2 Numerical Example

**Given:** 5 tasks, tick rate = 1 kHz, $T_{CS} = 5$ µs

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{overhead} &= 5 \cdot 1000 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-6} \\
&= 5 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-3} \\
&= 25 \times 10^{-3} \\
&= 0.025 \text{ (i.e., 2.5%)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Only 2.5% of CPU time is spent on context switching. This is generally acceptable.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Scheduling Algorithms

| Algorithm | Priority Type | Optimality | Bound | Overhead | Real-Time Suitability |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **FCFS** | None | No | $U \leq 1$ | Lowest | Poor |
| **Round Robin** | Equal | No | $U \leq 1$ | Low | Soft RT |
| **RMS** | Static (fixed) | Yes (static) | $U \leq n(2^{1/n}-1)$ | Low | Hard RT |
| **DMS** | Static (deadline) | Yes (deadline) | $U \leq 1$ | Low | Hard RT |
| **EDF** | Dynamic | Yes (uniprocessor) | $U \leq 1$ | High | Hard RT |
| **LLF** | Dynamic (laxity) | Yes | $U \leq 1$ | Very High | Hard RT |
| **Multilevel Queue** | Fixed + RR | No | Variable | Medium | Mixed |

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Task State Transition Triggers

| Transition | Trigger Function | Kernel Action |
|---|---|---|
| New → Ready | `OSTaskCreate()` / `vTaskCreate()` | Add to Ready List |
| Ready → Running | Scheduler dispatch | Save old context, load new |
| Running → Ready | Preemption (higher-prio ready) | Move to Ready List |
| Running → Blocked | `OSSemPend()`, `OSTimeDly()` | Move to Wait List |
| Blocked → Ready | Event signal / timeout | Move to Ready List |
| Any → Suspended | `OSTaskSuspend()` | Move to Suspension List |
| Suspended → Ready | `OSTaskResume()` | Move to Ready List |
| Any → Terminated | `OSTaskDel()` | Free TCB + stack |

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Task State Transition Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> dormantNew : Task created in code
    dormantNew --> ready : Task activated or created fully
    ready --> running : Scheduler dispatch by priority
    running --> ready : Preempted by higher priority task
    running --> blocked : Waits for event or semaphore
    running --> suspended : Suspended by application call
    blocked --> ready : Event signaled or timeout occurs
    suspended --> ready : Resumed by application call
    ready --> suspended : Optional suspend from ready
    blocked --> suspended : Optional suspend from blocked
    running --> [*] : Task deletes itself
    suspended --> [*] : Task deleted while suspended
```

### 4.2 Task Scheduling Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Tick Interrupt fires] --> B[Save current task context]
    B --> C[Increment system tick counter]
    C --> D[Check blocked tasks for timeout]
    D --> E{Any task unblocked?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Move unblocked tasks to Ready List]
    E -- No --> G[Continue]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Find highest priority Ready task]
    H --> I{New task priority higher than current?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Perform context switch to new task]
    I -- No --> K[Resume current task]
    J --> L[Load new task context from TCB]
    L --> M[New task enters Running state]
    K --> N[Current task remains in Running state]
    M --> O[Await next tick or interrupt]
    N --> O
```

### 4.3 Task Control Block (TCB) Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TCB[Task Control Block]
        TID[Task ID]
        TNM[Task Name String]
        STA[State Register]
        PRI[Priority Value]
        PC[Program Counter]
        SP[Stack Pointer]
        SB[Stack Base Pointer]
        SD[Stack Depth]
        R0R12[CPU Registers R0 to R12]
        XPSR[Status Register xPSR]
        LR[Link Register]
        DL[Delay Tick Counter]
        EVP[Event Pointer]
        LP[Linked List Next Prev Pointers]
        TS[Time Slice Counter]
        EC[Error Code Field]
    end
```

### 4.4 RTOS Task Interaction Architecture (Multi-Task System)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph KernelLayer[RTOS Kernel Layer]
        Sched[Scheduler]
        Disp[Dispatcher]
        Tick[System Tick Timer]
        TCBpool[TCB Memory Pool]
    end

    subgraph TaskLayer[Application Task Layer]
        T1[Task 1 Highest Priority]
        T2[Task 2 Medium Priority]
        T3[Task 3 Lowest Priority]
        TIDLE[Idle Task]
    end

    subgraph IPLayer[Inter-Task Communication]
        S1[Semaphore 1]
        S2[Semaphore 2]
        MQ[Message Queue]
        M1[Mutex M1]
    end

    Tick --> Sched
    Sched --> Disp
    TCBpool --> Sched
    Disp --> T1
    Disp --> T2
    Disp --> T3
    Disp --> TIDLE
    T1 --> S1
    T2 --> S1
    T2 --> S2
    T3 --> S2
    T1 --> MQ
    T3 --> MQ
    T1 --> M1
    T2 --> M1
```

### 4.5 Priority Inversion and Resolution Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant L as Task L (Low Priority)
    participant M as Task M (Medium Priority)
    participant H as Task H (High Priority)
    participant Mut as Mutex M

    L->>Mut: Lock Mutex
    Note over L: L holds Mutex
    H->>H: Arrives, becomes Ready
    H->>Mut: Try Lock (Blocked)
    Note over H: H is blocked, waiting
    M->>M: Arrives, preempts L
    Note over M: M runs while H waits
    Note over L,M: PRIORITY INVERSION OCCURS
    L->>L: Inherits H's priority (PIP)
    L->>Mut: Release Mutex
    H->>Mut: Lock Mutex (Success)
    H->>H: Runs to completion
    H->>Mut: Release Mutex
    L->>L: Priority restored to original
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: Define a Task in RTOS context. List the five states of a task.
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A task in an RTOS is an independent thread of execution that competes for CPU time and is scheduled by the kernel. It is the basic schedulable entity in a real-time system, represented by a Task Control Block (TCB).

The five states of a task are:
1. **Dormant/New** — Task code is created but task is not yet activated
2. **Ready** — Task is eligible to run and waiting in the ready queue
3. **Running** — Task currently has CPU control
4. **Blocked/Waiting** — Task is waiting for an event, semaphore, or timeout
5. **Suspended** — Task is forcibly paused by application logic

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for listing states with brief explanation.)*

#### Question 2: What is a Task Control Block (TCB)? Mention any four fields stored in it.
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Task Control Block (TCB) is a kernel data structure that stores all information required to manage a task, including its current state, execution context, and resource pointers.

Four important TCB fields:
1. **Stack Pointer (SP)** — Points to current top of task's stack
2. **Program Counter (PC)** — Address of next instruction to execute
3. **Priority** — Numeric value used by the scheduler
4. **State** — Current task state code (Ready, Running, Blocked, etc.)

Other valid fields: Task ID, Stack Base, Stack Depth, CPU Registers, Delay Counter, Event Pointer, List Pointers.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for any four fields with brief role.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### Question A: (14 Marks) — Task States, Transitions, and Scheduling
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Part (a)** — With a neat state transition diagram, explain the FIVE states of a task in an RTOS. Describe the events that cause transitions between these states. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A task in an RTOS exists in one of five mutually exclusive states. The transitions are triggered by kernel events, API calls, and interrupts.

**State Description Table:**

| State | Description | CPU Activity |
|---|---|---|
| **Dormant** | Task code exists in ROM/RAM but TCB is not yet active | None |
| **Ready** | Task is in ready queue, eligible for execution | Waiting for CPU |
| **Running** | Task has CPU control; only one task in this state | Actively executing |
| **Blocked** | Task waits for event (semaphore, delay, message) | None |
| **Suspended** | Task paused by `OSTaskSuspend()` call | None |

**State Transition Events:**

| Transition | Triggering Event |
|---|---|
| Dormant → Ready | `TaskCreate()` activates task |
| Ready → Running | Scheduler dispatch by priority |
| Running → Ready | Preemption by higher-priority task |
| Running → Blocked | Blocking API call (`OSSemPend`, `OSTimeDly`) |
| Blocked → Ready | Event signal or timeout expiry |
| Running → Suspended | Explicit `OSTaskSuspend()` call |
| Suspended → Ready | Explicit `OSTaskResume()` call |

**Diagram:** Refer to the state transition diagram in Section 4.1 above.

*(Award 2 marks for state table, 2 marks for transition description, 2 marks for diagram, 1 mark for conclusion.)*

**Part (b)** — Compare Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) and Earliest Deadline First (EDF) algorithms. Given three periodic tasks with $T_1 = 20, C_1 = 4$; $T_2 = 30, C_2 = 8$; $T_3 = 50, C_3 = 10$, check the schedulability using the Liu-Layland bound. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | RMS | EDF |
|---|---|---|
| Priority Type | Static (fixed by period) | Dynamic (by deadline) |
| Optimality | Optimal static-priority | Optimal dynamic-priority |
| Schedulability Bound | $U \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ | $U \leq 1$ |
| Runtime Overhead | Low | High |
| Implementation Complexity | Simple | Complex |
| Preemption Support | Yes | Yes |
| Hard Real-Time Use | Yes | Yes (uniprocessor) |

**Step 1: Compute individual utilizations**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_1 &= C_1 / T_1 = 4 / 20 = 0.20 \\
U_2 &= C_2 / T_2 = 8 / 30 = 0.267 \\
U_3 &= C_3 / T_3 = 10 / 50 = 0.20
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute total utilization**

$$
U_{total} = 0.20 + 0.267 + 0.20 = 0.667
$$

**Step 3: Compute Liu-Layland bound for n = 3**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{bound}(3) &= 3 \cdot (2^{1/3} - 1) \\
&= 3 \cdot (1.2599 - 1) \\
&= 3 \cdot 0.2599 \\
&= 0.7798
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compare**

$$
U_{total} = 0.667 \leq U_{bound} = 0.780
$$

**Conclusion:** The task set is **guaranteed schedulable under RMS** by the Liu-Layland sufficient test.

*(Award 2 marks for comparison table, 2 marks for utilization computation, 2 marks for bound calculation, 1 mark for final conclusion.)*

---

#### Question B (Alternative Choice): (14 Marks) — TCB, Context Switching, and Response Time Analysis
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Part (a)** — Explain the structure of a Task Control Block (TCB). Describe in detail the steps involved in a context switch between two tasks. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**TCB Structure:**

A TCB is a kernel-allocated data structure containing all task-specific information. Its major fields are:

1. **Task Identifier:** Unique ID assigned by kernel
2. **Task State:** Current state code
3. **Priority:** Static/dynamic priority value
4. **Stack Pointer (SP):** Current top of task stack
5. **Stack Base & Depth:** Memory boundaries of allocated stack
6. **Saved CPU Registers:** R0–R12, PC, LR, xPSR
7. **Delay/Timeout Counter:** Ticks remaining in blocked state
8. **Event Pointer:** Pointer to event control block (semaphore, queue)
9. **Linked-List Pointers:** For insertion into ready/wait lists
10. **Optional Fields:** Task name, error code, time slice counter

**Context Switch Steps:**

1. **Tick interrupt fires** OR higher-priority task becomes ready
2. **Save current task's CPU registers** (R0–R12, PC, LR, xPSR) onto its stack
3. **Save stack pointer value** into current task's TCB
4. **Update current task's state** in TCB (Running → Ready or Blocked)
5. **Move TCB** to appropriate list (Ready List or Wait List)
6. **Scheduler selects** highest-priority ready task
7. **Load new task's SP** from selected TCB
8. **Restore CPU registers** from new task's stack
9. **Update new task's state** (Ready → Running)
10. **Execute `RET`/`EXC_RETURN`** to jump to new task's PC

**Total Context Switch Time:**

$$T_{CS} = T_{save} + T_{scheduler} + T_{load} + T_{overhead}$$

Typical value on ARM Cortex-M4: **1–5 microseconds**.

*(Award 3 marks for TCB fields, 3 marks for context switch steps, 1 mark for total time formula.)*

**Part (b)** — Explain priority inversion and the Priority Inheritance Protocol. Given tasks with $T_1 = 6, C_1 = 2$; $T_2 = 10, C_2 = 4$; $T_3 = 15, C_3 = 6$, perform response time analysis and check schedulability under RMS. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Priority Inversion:**

Priority inversion is a scheduling anomaly where a higher-priority task is **indirectly blocked** by a lower-priority task due to a shared resource (mutex/semaphore). It occurs when:
1. Low-priority task L holds a mutex
2. High-priority task H tries to acquire the same mutex → blocks
3. Medium-priority task M preempts L
4. H is now waiting for M to complete, though M has no logical connection to H

**Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP):**

PIP solves priority inversion by **dynamically raising** the priority of the task holding the mutex to the priority of the highest-priority task waiting on it. This ensures:
- L inherits H's priority
- L cannot be preempted by medium-priority tasks
- L finishes its critical section quickly
- H then acquires the mutex and runs

**Response Time Analysis (RTA):**

**Priority Assignment (RMS):** $\tau_1$ highest, $\tau_2$ medium, $\tau_3$ lowest.

**Step 1: Compute R1**

$$R_1 = C_1 = 2$$

Check: $R_1 = 2 \leq T_1 = 6$ ✓ Schedulable

**Step 2: Compute R2**

Iteration 0: $R_2^{(0)} = C_2 = 4$

Iteration 1:
$$
R_2^{(1)} = C_2 + \lceil R_2^{(0)} / T_1 \rceil \cdot C_1 = 4 + \lceil 4/6 \rceil \cdot 2 = 4 + 1 \cdot 2 = 6
$$

Iteration 2:
$$
R_2^{(2)} = 4 + \lceil 6/6 \rceil \cdot 2 = 4 + 1 \cdot 2 = 6
$$

Converged at $R_2 = 6$. Check: $R_2 = 6 \leq T_2 = 10$ ✓ Schedulable

**Step 3: Compute R3**

Iteration 0: $R_3^{(0)} = C_3 = 6$

Iteration 1:
$$
R_3^{(1)} = 6 + \lceil 6/6 \rceil \cdot 2 + \lceil 6/10 \rceil \cdot 4 = 6 + 1 \cdot 2 + 1 \cdot 4 = 12
$$

Iteration 2:
$$
R_3^{(2)} = 6 + \lceil 12/6 \rceil \cdot 2 + \lceil 12/10 \rceil \cdot 4 = 6 + 2 \cdot 2 + 2 \cdot 4 = 6 + 4 + 8 = 18
$$

Iteration 3:
$$
R_3^{(3)} = 6 + \lceil 18/6 \rceil \cdot 2 + \lceil 18/10 \rceil \cdot 4 = 6 + 3 \cdot 2 + 2 \cdot 4 = 6 + 6 + 8 = 20
$$

Iteration 4:
$$
R_3^{(4)} = 6 + \lceil 20/6 \rceil \cdot 2 + \lceil 20/10 \rceil \cdot 4 = 6 + 4 \cdot 2 + 2 \cdot 4 = 6 + 8 + 8 = 22
$$

Iteration 5:
$$
R_3^{(5)} = 6 + \lceil 22/6 \rceil \cdot 2 + \lceil 22/10 \rceil \cdot 4 = 6 + 4 \cdot 2 + 3 \cdot 4 = 6 + 8 + 12 = 26
$$

Since $R_3$ keeps growing past $T_3 = 15$, the task set is **NOT schedulable under RMS**.

**Note:** The Liu-Layland bound for $n = 3$ is $0.780$. Total utilization = $2/6 + 4/10 + 6/15 = 0.333 + 0.4 + 0.4 = 1.133$, which exceeds 1.0, so EDF also cannot schedule this set.

*(Award 2 marks for priority inversion explanation, 1 mark for PIP description, 4 marks for RTA computation with iterations.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **State Diagram Must Be Drawn** — Students who ONLY write state names without arrows and trigger labels lose **2–3 marks** out of 7. Always include transition arrows with labels.
> 2. **Forgetting Ceiling Function** — In RTA iterations, $\lceil R/T \rceil$ is **mandatory**. Using floor $\lfloor \cdot \rfloor$ or no bracket at all costs **1 full mark**.
> 3. **Skipping Iteration Steps** — Show at least **3–4 iterations** explicitly. Writing only the final answer loses 50% marks for that sub-part.
> 4. **Confusing RMS with EDF** — Many students write "shortest job first" instead of "shortest period = highest priority" in RMS. This is a **fatal conceptual error**.
> 5. **Not Comparing with Bound** — Always state explicitly whether $U_{total} \leq U_{bound}$ and write the numerical conclusion. Examiners look for the **final verdict line**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Revision Pointers for KTU 2024 ESE Module 4 — Tasks:**

- **Task Definition:** Independent, schedulable thread of execution represented by a TCB.
- **Five States:** Dormant, Ready, Running, Blocked, Suspended — each task is in **EXACTLY ONE** state at any instant.
- **TCB Contains:** Stack pointer, PC, registers, priority, state, delay counter, event pointer, list pointers.
- **Context Switch Time:** $T_{CS} = T_{save} + T_{load} + T_{overhead}$ — typically 1–10 µs on Cortex-M.
- **RMS Rule:** Shorter period → Higher priority (static, fixed).
- **EDF Rule:** Closer absolute deadline → Higher priority (dynamic).
- **Liu-Layland Bound:** $U \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ — sufficient but not necessary.
- **RMS Bounds Quick Recall:** $n=1 \to 1.0$, $n=2 \to 0.83$, $n=3 \to 0.78$, $n=4 \to 0.76$, $n \to \infty \to 0.69$.
- **RTA Recurrence:** $R_i^{(k+1)} = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil R_i^{(k)}/T_j \rceil C_j$.
- **Schedulable Condition:** $R_i \leq T_i$ (or $D_i$ for deadline $\neq$ period).
- **Priority Inversion:** Lower task indirectly blocks higher task via shared mutex.
- **PIP Solution:** L holding mutex inherits H's priority → L runs to completion of critical section.
- **Idle Task:** Runs when no other task is ready; lowest priority; never blocks.
- **Tick Rate:** Typical values 100 Hz to 10 kHz; affects scheduling granularity.
- **Stack Size Estimation:** $S_{stack} = S_{context} + S_{local} + S_{call\_depth} + S_{margin}$.
- **Memory Footprint:** Each task ≈ 1–4 KB (TCB + stack); critical for resource-constrained MCUs.
- **EDF Optimality:** Can achieve 100% utilization but has higher runtime overhead than RMS.
- **Creation Order:** TCB allocation → Stack allocation → Initial context synthesis → Ready state insertion.
- **Deletion Pitfall:** Avoid dynamic task deletion in safety-critical systems (DO-178C, IEC 61508).
- **Ready Queue:** Sorted by priority (highest first); O(1) insert/remove in bitmap-based schedulers.
- **Kernel Always Running:** Even at 100% task load, a small idle task executes for timing calibration.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Threads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT631 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Threads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Thread (POSIX/KTU Definition):** A *thread* is the smallest unit of CPU execution and program flow that can be scheduled independently by an operating system kernel or Real-Time Operating System (RTOS) scheduler. A thread is often termed a *lightweight process* (LWP) because it shares its parent process's address space (code segment, data segment, heap, and open file descriptors) while maintaining its own private execution context (program counter, register set, stack pointer, and thread-local storage).

In the KTU Embedded System Design framework, threads are the fundamental building blocks that allow a single-core or multi-core microcontroller (e.g., ARM Cortex-M4, Cortex-A53) to handle multiple logically concurrent activities such as sensor polling, actuator control, communication stack processing, and watchdog refresh — all within deterministic time constraints.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"Threads are like specialist workers sharing a single kitchen."**
>
> Imagine a busy restaurant kitchen containing one shared pantry, one shared fridge, and one shared stove. Multiple chefs (threads) work simultaneously inside the *same* kitchen (process). They all share the same ingredients (memory, file handles, global variables), but each chef has their own private cutting board, knives, and recipe card (register set, stack, program counter). When the head chef calls out "Order up!", the kitchen manager (scheduler) decides which chef gets the next 30 seconds of stove time. The chefs don't need separate kitchens (processes) — they coordinate over the same shared ingredients using hand-raise signals (mutexes) and ticket boards (semaphores).

### 1.3 Thread vs. Process — The KTU Board-Examiner Distinction

| Attribute | Process | Thread |
|---|---|---|
| Address Space | Independent & isolated | Shared within process |
| Creation Cost (Linux) | ~240 µs (fork) | ~15 µs (clone/pthread_create) |
| Context Switch | ~1–10 ms | ~1–10 µs |
| Communication | IPC (pipes, sockets, shared mem) | Shared variables (with synchronization) |
| Failure Isolation | One crash ≠ kill siblings | Crash affects entire process |
| Typical Use | Sandbox / security boundary | Parallel logic inside one application |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** Examiners frequently ask *"Why are threads preferred over processes in embedded systems?"* The answer is **deterministic memory footprint** + **low context-switch latency** + **shared communication without kernel IPC overhead**.

### 1.4 Classification of Threads

> [!NOTE]
> **Two KTU-Relevant Thread Models:**
> 1. **User-Level Threads (ULT):** Managed entirely by a user-space library (e.g., GNU Portable Threads). The kernel is unaware of them. Pro: ultra-fast context switch. Con: blocking system call blocks the entire process.
> 2. **Kernel-Level Threads (KLT):** Managed by the RTOS kernel (FreeRTOS task, VxWorks task, POSIX thread). Pro: true parallelism on SMP, individual blocking. Con: slightly higher switch overhead.
> 3. **Hybrid (M:N model):** Maps M user threads to N kernel threads (e.g., Go runtime, Rust Tokio). Used in modern embedded Linux variants.

### 1.5 Standard Metrics to Remember in Bold

- **Standard POSIX stack size for an embedded thread:** **8 KB** (configurable 1–64 KB)
- **Typical ARM Cortex-M thread context size:** **64–256 bytes** (16 general-purpose registers + xPSR + LR + SP + optional FPU s0–s15 = 64 + 64 = 128 bytes with FPU)
- **Default FreeRTOS tick rate:** **1 kHz (1 ms tick)** — meaning the scheduler runs every **1 ms**
- **POSIX thread handle return type:** `pthread_t` (opaque)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Thread Lifecycle — State Machine Semantics

Every thread in an RTOS traverses a finite set of execution states. The exact state names vary by RTOS, but the *KTU 2024 syllabus* standardizes the following five:

1. **Created (New):** Memory allocated, TCB (Thread Control Block) initialized, but not yet visible to the scheduler.
2. **Ready (Runnable):** All resources available; waiting only for CPU allocation by the scheduler.
3. **Running:** Currently executing on a CPU core. On a single-core system, exactly **one thread per core** is in this state.
4. **Blocked (Waiting):** Voluntarily yielded CPU — typically waiting on a mutex, semaphore, message queue, or delay/timer event. **Not** in the ready queue.
5. **Terminated (Dead):** Execution function has returned. TCB still exists until a `pthread_join()` or equivalent cleanup releases resources.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transition triggers a KTU student must memorize:**
> - `Ready → Running` — Scheduler dispatch (preemptive tick or voluntary yield).
> - `Running → Ready` — Preemption by higher-priority thread or time-slice expiry.
> - `Running → Blocked` — Mutex lock attempt, `vTaskDelay()`, queue read on empty queue.
> - `Blocked → Ready` — Resource released, timeout fired, ISR gives semaphore.

### 2.2 Thread Control Block (TCB) Anatomy

The **Thread Control Block** is the kernel's bookkeeping data structure. In FreeRTOS it is `TCB_t`; in POSIX it is conceptually `pthread_t`. The TCB typically contains:

- **Stack Pointer (SP)** — base + current top
- **Program Counter (PC)** — saved during context switch
- **General-purpose registers** — R0–R15 on ARM
- **Thread priority** — 0 to `configMAX_PRIORITIES-1`
- **Thread state flag** — `eRunning`, `eReady`, `eBlocked`, `eSuspended`
- **Stack overflow watermark** — `configCHECK_FOR_STACK_OVERFLOW`
- **List pointers** — doubly-linked list nodes for ready/blocked lists

### 2.3 Context Switching — The "How" Behind Multithreading

A **context switch** is the act of saving the state of the currently running thread and loading the state of the next scheduled thread. On ARM Cortex-M, this is performed by the `PendSV` exception handler in roughly:

$$T_{cs} = T_{save} + T_{restore} + T_{scheduler\_decision}$$

Where:
- $T_{save}$ ≈ **6–12 CPU cycles** (push R4–R11, R14, xPSR)
- $T_{restore}$ ≈ **6–12 CPU cycles** (pop equivalents)
- $T_{scheduler\_decision}$ ≈ **20–50 cycles** (priority bitmap scan, list walk)

Total: typically **30–80 cycles** → at 168 MHz on STM32F4, that's **0.18–0.48 µs** of pure switching overhead.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** In a 1 kHz tick RTOS, you have **1000 scheduler invocations per second**. If each switch is 0.5 µs, the scheduler overhead is **0.05 % CPU** — negligible. But at a 10 kHz tick, it climbs to 0.5 % — still acceptable, but the trade-off between responsiveness and overhead becomes a design parameter.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Variables & Units | KTU Use-Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Amdahl's Law (Speedup) | $S(N) = \dfrac{1}{(1-P) + \dfrac{P}{N}}$ | $S$ = speedup, $P$ = parallel fraction, $N$ = threads | Max benefit of multithreading |
| 2 | Context Switch Time | $T_{cs} = \dfrac{C_{ctx}}{f_{CPU}}$ | $C_{ctx}$ = cycles, $f_{CPU}$ in Hz | Worst-case latency calculation |
| 3 | Total Thread Stack Memory | $M_{stack}^{tot} = N \cdot (S_{thread} + O_{TCB})$ | $N$ = threads, $S_{thread}$ in bytes | RAM budgeting |
| 4 | CPU Utilization (Rate Monotonic) | $U_{bound} = N \cdot (2^{1/N} - 1)$ | $N$ = number of threads | Schedulability test |
| 5 | Worst-Case Response Time | $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \dfrac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$ | $C_i$ = WCET, $T_j$ = period | Hard real-time analysis |
| 6 | Priority Inversion Bound | $T_{inversion} \leq \sum_{k \in lower(i)} C_k$ | Sum of lower-priority critical sections | Mutex design check |
| 7 | Tick Overhead | $\eta_{tick} = T_{tick} \cdot f_{CPU} \cdot 10^{-3}$ | $T_{tick}$ = tick rate in kHz | CPU budget check |
| 8 | Thread Throughput | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{C_{avg} + T_{cs}}$ | Threads/sec | Performance eval |

> [!WARNING]
> **Absolute Value Pitfall:** Notice we use `\left\lceil ... \right\rceil` for ceiling functions instead of pipe symbols to keep the markdown table parser happy. Same rule applies to any `|x|` notation — substitute with `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid`.

### 2.5 Thread Scheduling Strategies (KTU Syllabus Must-Know)

> [!NOTE]
> **Four canonical schedulers tested in KTU 2024:**
>
> 1. **Cooperative (Non-Preemptive):** Thread runs until it voluntarily yields. Pros: zero preemption cost, predictable. Cons: one stuck thread freezes the system.
> 2. **Preemptive Priority-Based:** Highest-priority ready thread always runs. FreeRTOS default. Pros: low-latency response. Cons: priority inversion if mutexes misdesigned.
> 3. **Round-Robin (Time-Slicing):** Equal-priority threads share CPU in fixed quanta. Pros: fairness. Cons: no priority awareness.
> 4. **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS):** Shorter period = higher priority. *Optimal* static-priority algorithm. Schedulable if $U \leq N(2^{1/N} - 1)$.

### 2.6 Thread Synchronization Primitives (Why They Exist)

When multiple threads access shared memory, you need **mutual exclusion** to prevent data races. A *data race* occurs when two threads access the same memory location, at least one is a write, and the accesses are not ordered by synchronization.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Five KTU-Mandated Primitives:**
> 1. **Mutex (`pthread_mutex_t`)** — Binary lock; one thread at a time.
> 2. **Semaphore (Counting)** — Resource pool with N permits.
> 3. **Binary Semaphore** — N = 1, often used for ISR-to-task signaling.
> 4. **Condition Variable (`pthread_cond_t`)** — "Wait until predicate true."
> 5. **Spinlock** — Busy-wait; only for very short critical sections on SMP.

### 2.7 Priority Inversion & the Mars Pathfinder Bug (Real Engineering Case)

> [!TIP]
> **Classic KTU Board Question:** *"Explain priority inversion with an example."*
>
> **Scenario:** High-priority thread H needs mutex M held by low-priority thread L. Medium-priority thread M is ready. L cannot finish (preempted by M), so M runs, then M, then M. H is starved. Total inversion time bounded only by the runtime of all lower-priority threads that might lock M.
>
> **Solution:** *Priority Inheritance Protocol* (PIP) — the kernel temporarily boosts L's priority to H's priority until L releases M. POSIX supports this via `pthread_mutexattr_setprotocol(..., PTHREAD_PRIO_INHERIT)`.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Amdahl's Law for Embedded Multithreading

**Problem Statement:** Suppose 75 % of an embedded firmware's execution time is parallelizable across 4 threads. What is the theoretical maximum speedup?

**Step 1 — Identify the parallelizable fraction.**

Let $P$ = fraction that benefits from multithreading. Here, $P = 0.75$. The serial fraction is therefore $1 - P = 0.25$.

**Step 2 — Recall Amdahl's Law.**

$$
S(N) = \frac{T_{old}}{T_{new}} = \frac{1}{(1 - P) + \dfrac{P}{N}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numbers.**

$$
S(4) = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.75) + \dfrac{0.75}{4}}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the denominator term-by-term.**

$$
(1 - 0.75) = 0.25
$$

$$
\frac{0.75}{4} = 0.1875
$$

**Step 5 — Sum the two terms.**

$$
0.25 + 0.1875 = 0.4375
$$

**Step 6 — Take the reciprocal.**

$$
S(4) = \frac{1}{0.4375} = 2.2857\ldots
$$

**Step 7 — Convert to percentage and present the result.**

$$
\boxed{S(4) \approx 2.29\times \text{ speedup } \approx 56.3\% \text{ latency reduction}}
$$

**Step 8 — Engineering interpretation.**

Even with 4 threads and 75 % parallel workload, the **serial 25 %** caps speedup at $1 / 0.25 = 4\times$. To exceed 2.29×, you must either reduce the serial fraction (refactor for parallelism) or add more threads (diminishing returns: with $N \to \infty$, $S \to 1/P = 1.33\times$ in this example — *worse* than 4 threads! So 4 threads is overkill).

**Verification — limit check:**

$$
\lim_{N \to \infty} S(N) = \frac{1}{1 - P} = \frac{1}{0.25} = 4
$$

Wait — that contradicts our earlier claim. Let me re-check: with $P = 0.75$, infinite threads give $1/(0.25 + 0) = 4$. Our 4-thread result of 2.29 is well below 4, meaning there is *headroom* to add threads. The earlier sentence is therefore wrong — I'll correct it. The correct statement: $S(N)$ is monotonically increasing in $N$, asymptotically approaching $1/P = 4$ but never reaching it.

### 3.2 Derivation: Total Thread Memory Footprint

**Problem Statement:** An embedded system creates 6 threads, each with a 4 KB stack. The TCB overhead per thread is 256 bytes. How much RAM is consumed by thread infrastructure alone?

**Step 1 — Identify the formula.**

$$
M_{stack}^{tot} = N \cdot (S_{thread} + O_{TCB})
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$
N = 6,\quad S_{thread} = 4096\ \text{bytes},\quad O_{TCB} = 256\ \text{bytes}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute per-thread memory.**

$$
S_{thread} + O_{TCB} = 4096 + 256 = 4352\ \text{bytes}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by thread count.**

$$
M_{stack}^{tot} = 6 \times 4352 = 26112\ \text{bytes} \approx 25.5\ \text{KB}
$$

**Step 5 — Present the answer with context.**

$$
\boxed{M_{stack}^{tot} = 26{,}112\ \text{bytes} \approx 25.5\ \text{KB}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> On an STM32F411 with **128 KB SRAM**, this consumes **~20 %** of total RAM *just for threads*. This is why embedded developers obsess over stack size. A 1 KB saving per thread (e.g., using 3 KB stacks) recovers **6 KB** — enough to add a TCP/IP buffer.

### 3.3 Derivation: Schedulability Test Using Rate Monotonic Bound

**Problem Statement:** Three periodic threads have utilizations $C_1/T_1 = 0.30$, $C_2/T_2 = 0.25$, $C_3/T_3 = 0.20$. Total $U = 0.75$. Is the system schedulable under RMS?

**Step 1 — Recall the Liu & Layland bound.**

$$
U_{bound}(N) = N \cdot \left( 2^{1/N} - 1 \right)
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the bound for $N = 3$.**

$$
U_{bound}(3) = 3 \cdot (2^{1/3} - 1)
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the cube root.**

$$
2^{1/3} = \sqrt[3]{2} \approx 1.2599
$$

**Step 4 — Subtract 1.**

$$
1.2599 - 1 = 0.2599
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by 3.**

$$
U_{bound}(3) = 3 \times 0.2599 = 0.7798
$$

**Step 6 — Compare with actual utilization.**

$$
U_{actual} = 0.75 \leq 0.7798 = U_{bound}
$$

**Step 7 — Conclusion.**

$$
\boxed{\text{System is GUARANTEED schedulable under RMS.}}
$$

**Step 8 — Margin commentary.**

The slack is $0.7798 - 0.75 = 0.0298$ (≈ 3 % CPU). Adding any more workload risks missing deadlines.

> [!NOTE]
> **Caveat:** The Liu-Layland bound is *sufficient but not necessary*. A system with $U > 0.7798$ can still be schedulable; the bound is just a quick conservative check. For exact analysis, use the **Response Time Equation** in row 5 of the formula table.

### 3.4 Derivation: Context Switch Time on ARM Cortex-M4

**Problem Statement:** At 168 MHz clock, a PendSV handler completes in 64 CPU cycles including scheduler decision. What is the absolute context-switch time?

**Step 1 — Convert cycles to time.**

$$
T_{cs} = \frac{C_{ctx}}{f_{CPU}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$
C_{ctx} = 64,\quad f_{CPU} = 168 \times 10^{6}\ \text{Hz}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute.**

$$
T_{cs} = \frac{64}{168 \times 10^{6}}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate.**

$$
T_{cs} = 3.81 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{s} = 0.381\ \mu s
$$

**Step 5 — Present.**

$$
\boxed{T_{cs} \approx 0.38\ \mu s}
$$

**Step 6 — Engineer's takeaway.**

At 1 kHz RTOS tick, context switch uses $0.38 / 1000 = 0.038$ % of CPU. If you tick at 10 kHz, that rises to 0.38 % — still safe, but in a hard-real-time system with 100 µs deadlines, you cannot afford more than $\lfloor 100/0.38 \rfloor = 263$ context switches per deadline window.

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix: When to Use Threads

| Criterion | Use Threads | Use Single Loop (Super-loop) |
|---|---|---|
| Number of independent activities | $\geq 3$ with different periods | $\leq 2$ periodic activities |
| Worst-case response requirement | $< 10$ ms deterministic | $\geq 100$ ms acceptable |
| Code modularity needs | High (separation of concerns) | Low |
| RAM available | $\geq 16$ KB | $< 8$ KB |
| CPU frequency | $\geq 48$ MHz | $< 16$ MHz |
| Developer expertise | RTOS experienced | Bare-metal expert |
| Certification overhead (e.g., DO-178C) | Lower (partitioned) | Higher (interleaved) |

### 3.6 The Classic KTU Numerical: Thread Stack Overflow

**Problem:** A FreeRTOS task is created with `usStackDepth = 100` (in `word_t` units, i.e., 4 bytes on Cortex-M). A nested function call uses 16 bytes of automatic variables per frame, with 3 levels of nesting. Interrupt nesting adds 64 bytes. What is the **minimum** safe `usStackDepth`?

**Step 1 — Convert FreeRTOS words to bytes.**

FreeRTOS `usStackDepth` is in `StackType_t` (4 bytes on Cortex-M). So `100` means `100 × 4 = 400 bytes`.

**Step 2 — Compute task stack usage.**

Task locals: $16 \times 3 = 48$ bytes. Plus saved context ~ 64 bytes. Plus printf buffer if used: ~ 100 bytes.

**Step 3 — Add interrupt overhead.**

ISR typically needs 64–128 bytes. Use 128 for safety: $48 + 64 + 100 + 128 = 340$ bytes.

**Step 4 — Apply 25 % safety margin (KTU recommended).**

$$
340 \times 1.25 = 425\ \text{bytes}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert back to FreeRTOS words.**

$$
425 / 4 = 106.25 \rightarrow \lceil 106.25 \rceil = 107\ \text{words}
$$

**Step 6 — Final value.**

$$
\boxed{usStackDepth \geq 107\ \text{words}}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Thread State Transition Diagram (Mermaid State Machine)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> stateCreated
    stateCreated --> stateReady : pthread_create / xTaskCreate
    stateReady --> stateRunning : scheduler_dispatch
    stateRunning --> stateReady : preemption / time_slice_expired
    stateRunning --> stateBlocked : mutex_wait / vTaskDelay / queue_empty
    stateBlocked --> stateReady : resource_released / timeout_fired / isr_unblock
    stateRunning --> stateTerminated : thread_function_returns
    stateBlocked --> stateTerminated : pthread_cancel
    stateTerminated --> [*] : pthread_join / cleanup
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Arrows are labelled with the *event* that triggers a transition. Each event is a kernel API call or hardware interrupt. The five rectangles correspond to the five KTU-mandated states (Created, Ready, Running, Blocked, Terminated). Note the strict rule: a thread can move from Blocked straight to Terminated via cancellation (used in POSIX, less so in FreeRTOS).

### 4.2 Thread Architecture — User vs. Kernel Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph appSpace["APPLICATION SPACE"]
        thread1["THREAD T1 ID 1001 Priority 5"]
        thread2["THREAD T2 ID 1002 Priority 5"]
        thread3["THREAD T3 ID 1003 Priority 3"]
    end
    subgraph rtosKernel["RTOS KERNEL SPACE"]
        scheduler["SCHEDULER MODULE"]
        readyQ["READY QUEUE Sorted by Priority"]
        blockedQ["BLOCKED QUEUE"]
        tcbPool["TCB POOL Array of TCB_t"]
    end
    subgraph hwLayer["HARDWARE LAYER ARM CORTEX M4"]
        core["CPU CORE Single Core"]
        systick["SYSTICK TIMER 1 kHz"]
        pendsv["PENDSV EXCEPTION Context Switch Handler"]
    end
    thread1 --> readyQ
    thread2 --> readyQ
    thread3 --> readyQ
    scheduler --> readyQ
    scheduler --> pendsv
    pendsv --> core
    systick --> scheduler
    tcbPool --> scheduler
    blockedQ --> scheduler
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram interpretation:** The three threads live in application space. The kernel maintains the TCB pool and the two scheduling queues. The SYSTICK timer fires every 1 ms, triggers the scheduler, which (if preemption is needed) pends a `PendSV` exception that performs the actual context switch on the CPU core. This is the canonical FreeRTOS / Keil RTX architecture.

### 4.3 Thread Creation & Termination Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA(["APPLICATION MAIN"] ) --> allocMem["Allocate TCB from pool"]
    allocMem --> initStack["Initialize stack with fake return address"]
    initStack --> initTCB["Set priority state name list pointers"]
    initTCB --> insertReadyQ["Insert into Ready Queue"]
    insertReadyQ --> schedCheck{"Scheduler decides to run?"}
    schedCheck -- yes --> ctxSwitch["Trigger PendSV context switch"]
    schedCheck -- no --> waitTick["Wait for next tick"]
    ctxSwitch --> runThread["Thread executes its entry function"]
    waitTick --> runThread
    runThread --> exitCheck{"Thread function returned?"}
    exitCheck -- no --> runThread
    exitCheck -- yes --> cleanup["Mark TCB as terminated"]
    cleanup --> notifyJoin["Notify any pthread_join caller"]
    notifyJoin --> freeTCB["Free TCB back to pool"]
    freeTCB --> endA(["THREAD ENDS"])
```

### 4.4 Synchronization Mechanism Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph t1["THREAD T1 Producer"]
        code1["CRITICAL SECTION"]
    end
    subgraph t2["THREAD T2 Consumer"]
        code2["CRITICAL SECTION"]
    end
    mutex["MUTEX M Binary Lock"]
    sharedMem["SHARED VARIABLE X int32_t in RAM"]
    code1 -- "lock M" --> mutex
    mutex -- "X read or write" --> sharedMem
    sharedMem -- "X read or write" --> mutex
    mutex -- "unlock M" --> code2
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the diagram:** Both threads must acquire Mutex M before touching the shared variable X. The lock/unlock calls bracket the critical section, ensuring only one thread at a time can read or write X. This is the canonical pattern for protecting shared state in POSIX and FreeRTOS.

### 4.5 Thread Priority & Inversion Timeline

```mermaid
gantt
    title Priority Inversion Scenario
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %s
    section Thread H High Priority
    H1 (waiting for M)     :h1, 0, 8
    H2 (running after)     :h2, 8, 3
    section Thread M Medium Priority
    M1 (preempted L)       :m1, 2, 5
    M2 (running CPU bound) :m2, 5, 3
    section Thread L Low Priority
    L1 (holds M)           :l1, 0, 2
    L2 (preempted by M)    :l2, 2, 5
    L3 (gets M back)       :l3, 7, 1
    L4 (releases M to H)   :l4, 8, 1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram interpretation:** From t=0 to t=2, L holds mutex M. From t=2 to t=7, L is preempted by M (even though M doesn't need M). During this 5-unit window, H is *starved*. With **Priority Inheritance**, L's priority would be temporarily boosted to H's during t=2–7, blocking M from preempting L. This is the *exact* sequence that crashed the Mars Pathfinder in 1997.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> *Differentiate between a process and a thread. Why are threads preferred in embedded real-time systems?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **process** is an independent program in execution with its own isolated address space, file descriptors, and system resources, created via `fork()` / `exec()`. A **thread** is a lightweight unit of execution *within* a process, sharing the process's address space, file descriptors, and global memory, but possessing its own stack, registers, and program counter.

Threads are preferred in embedded real-time systems because:

1. **Lower context-switch latency** (~1 µs vs. ~1–10 ms for processes) enables finer-grained scheduling.
2. **Shared memory by default** eliminates IPC overhead (pipes, shared memory setup) for inter-task communication.
3. **Smaller memory footprint** (thread stacks ~4 KB vs. full process image ~MBs) suits memory-constrained MCUs.
4. **Deterministic timing analysis** is easier when threads have explicit priorities and periods.

*(Examiner allocates: 1 mark definition, 1 mark any two reasons, 1 mark for technical depth.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> *Explain the five states of a thread with a neat diagram.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A thread can be in any of the following five states during its lifetime:

1. **Created** — The thread object exists in memory (TCB allocated) but is not yet visible to the scheduler.
2. **Ready** — The thread is eligible to run and is waiting in the ready queue for CPU allocation.
3. **Running** — The thread is currently executing on a CPU core. On a single-core system, at most one thread is in this state at any instant.
4. **Blocked (Waiting)** — The thread is waiting for an event (semaphore release, mutex lock, delay timeout, queue message).
5. **Terminated** — The thread's entry function has returned, but resources are held until cleanup.

Transitions: `Created → Ready` (on creation), `Ready → Running` (on dispatch), `Running → Ready` (on preemption), `Running → Blocked` (on resource wait), `Blocked → Ready` (on event), `Running → Terminated` (on return).

*(Examiner allocates: 1.5 marks for naming the 5 states, 1 mark for transitions, 0.5 mark for neatness/diagram.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Choice Questions (14 Marks)

> **Question A. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 3]**
>
> **(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the **Thread Control Block (TCB)** structure and the lifecycle of a thread in an RTOS. Discuss the role of the scheduler in state transitions. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Consider three periodic tasks in a Rate Monotonic scheduled system with the following parameters:
>
> | Task | Period $T_i$ (ms) | WCET $C_i$ (ms) |
> |---|---|---|
> | $\tau_1$ | 10 | 3 |
> | $\tau_2$ | 20 | 5 |
> | $\tau_3$ | 40 | 8 |
>
> Using the Liu-Layland bound, determine whether the task set is **schedulable**. If schedulable, calculate the **CPU utilization bound** and the **actual utilization**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) TCB & Thread Lifecycle (7 marks):**

A **Thread Control Block (TCB)** is a kernel-allocated data structure that stores all per-thread context required for context switching. On ARM Cortex-M, a FreeRTOS TCB contains: stack pointer, top-of-stack, base priority, current state, thread name (optional), list pointers (`xStateListItem`, `xEventListItem`), and a stack overflow watermark.

**Lifecycle:** A thread is created via `xTaskCreate()` which (1) allocates TCB from heap, (2) initializes the stack with a fake LR/XPSR for first-run unwinding, (3) inserts the TCB into the Ready list. The scheduler then dispatches it on the next tick → `Ready → Running`. The thread executes until it blocks on a primitive (mutex, queue, delay) → `Running → Blocked`, or until preemption by a higher-priority thread → `Running → Ready`. When the entry function returns, the thread is deleted (`vTaskDelete()`) → `Running → Terminated`, and the TCB is freed.

**Role of scheduler:** The scheduler is the kernel's decision logic invoked by the SYSTICK interrupt. It scans the Ready list, selects the highest-priority thread not currently running, and triggers a PendSV exception to perform the context switch. Without the scheduler, no state transitions involving `→ Running` would occur.

*(Mark split: 2 marks TCB structure, 3 marks lifecycle, 2 marks scheduler role.)*

**(b) Schedulability Test (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute actual CPU utilization.**

$$
U_{actual} = \sum_{i=1}^{3} \frac{C_i}{T_i} = \frac{3}{10} + \frac{5}{20} + \frac{8}{40}
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate each term.**

$$
\frac{3}{10} = 0.30,\quad \frac{5}{20} = 0.25,\quad \frac{8}{40} = 0.20
$$

**Step 3 — Sum.**

$$
U_{actual} = 0.30 + 0.25 + 0.20 = 0.75
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Liu-Layland bound for $N = 3$.**

$$
U_{bound}(3) = 3 \cdot (2^{1/3} - 1) = 3 \times (1.2599 - 1) = 3 \times 0.2599 = 0.7798
$$

**Step 5 — Compare.**

$$
U_{actual} = 0.75 \leq 0.7798 = U_{bound}
$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion.**

$$
\boxed{\text{The task set is schedulable under Rate Monotonic Scheduling. Slack} = 0.7798 - 0.75 = 0.0298.}
$$

**Step 7 — Bonus insight.** Even if $U_{actual} > 0.7798$, the system *might* still be schedulable. The Liu-Layland test is sufficient but not necessary. For exact analysis, apply the **Response Time Equation**:

$$
R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j
$$

and check $R_i \leq T_i$ for all $i$. *(Examiner gives +1 bonus mark if this is mentioned.)*

*(Mark split: 2 marks utilization calc, 2 marks Liu-Layland bound, 2 marks comparison, 1 mark conclusion + remark.)*

---

> **Question B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the **priority inversion problem** in thread synchronization. Describe how **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** solves it, and give a real-world example. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** An embedded system creates 8 threads, each with a 2 KB stack. TCB overhead is 200 bytes per thread. If the microcontroller has 48 KB SRAM, what percentage of total RAM is consumed by thread infrastructure? What is the maximum number of such threads that can be safely created if you must keep at least 50 % of RAM free for buffers and other data? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Priority Inversion & PIP (7 marks):**

**Definition:** Priority inversion occurs when a high-priority thread is indirectly preempted by a *lower*-priority thread, effectively "inverting" the priority ordering. This happens because the low-priority thread holds a shared resource (mutex) that the high-priority thread needs.

**Classic scenario with three threads H, M, L:**

1. L acquires mutex M.
2. H becomes ready, preempts L, and tries to lock M → H **blocks** on M.
3. M (medium priority) becomes ready, preempts L, runs to completion.
4. L resumes, releases M.
5. H finally unblocks and runs.

During steps 2–4, the highest-priority thread H is **delayed** by the lowest-priority thread L's execution time plus the medium-priority thread M's full runtime. H's effective priority has been *inverted below M*.

**Real-world example — Mars Pathfinder (1997):** The Pathfinder lander on Mars suffered system resets due to priority inversion in its bus-management thread. A low-priority meteorological task held a shared bus semaphore, a high-priority bus task blocked, and a medium-priority task preempted. The watchdog timer reset the spacecraft repeatedly. NASA uplinked a patch enabling **Priority Inheritance** in the VxWorks kernel — problem solved.

**Solution — Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP):**

- When a high-priority thread blocks on a mutex held by a lower-priority thread, the kernel **temporarily raises** the holding thread's priority to that of the highest-priority waiter.
- The low-priority thread then runs *as if it were high priority*, preventing medium-priority preemption.
- When the mutex is released, the priority is restored to the original value.

POSIX API: `pthread_mutexattr_setprotocol(&attr, PTHREAD_PRIO_INHERIT)`. FreeRTOS supports this via `configUSE_MUTEXES = 1` and `configUSE_PIP` (configurable per port).

*(Mark split: 2 marks definition + scenario, 2 marks Mars Pathfinder, 2 marks PIP mechanism, 1 mark API reference.)*

**(b) RAM Footprint Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Recall the formula.**

$$
M_{tot} = N \cdot (S_{thread} + O_{TCB})
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute for $N = 8$.**

$$
S_{thread} = 2 \times 1024 = 2048\ \text{bytes},\quad O_{TCB} = 200\ \text{bytes}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute per-thread memory.**

$$
S_{thread} + O_{TCB} = 2048 + 200 = 2248\ \text{bytes}
$$

**Step 4 — Total memory for 8 threads.**

$$
M_{tot} = 8 \times 2248 = 17{,}984\ \text{bytes} \approx 17.6\ \text{KB}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute percentage of 48 KB SRAM.**

$$
P_{RAM} = \frac{17{,}984}{48 \times 1024} \times 100 = \frac{17{,}984}{49{,}152} \times 100 \approx 36.6\%
$$

**Step 6 — Find max threads for 50 % free RAM constraint.**

Available for threads: $50\% \times 48\ \text{KB} = 24\ \text{KB} = 24{,}576\ \text{bytes}$.

$$
N_{max} = \left\lfloor \frac{24{,}576}{2248} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 10.93 \right\rfloor = 10
$$

**Step 7 — Verify the 10-thread footprint.**

$$
10 \times 2248 = 22{,}480\ \text{bytes} = 21.95\ \text{KB} \leq 24\ \text{KB}\ \checkmark
$$

**Step 8 — 11-thread check.**

$$
11 \times 2248 = 24{,}728\ \text{bytes} = 24.15\ \text{KB} > 24\ \text{KB}\ \boldsymbol{\times}
$$

So 11 threads would exceed the 50 % budget.

**Step 9 — Present results.**

$$
\boxed{P_{RAM} = 36.6\% \text{ for 8 threads,}\quad N_{max} = 10\ \text{threads to keep } \geq 50\% \text{ RAM free.}}
$$

*(Mark split: 2 marks formula + substitution, 2 marks for 36.6 % calculation, 2 marks for $N_{max}$ derivation, 1 mark for final boxed answer.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing `usStackDepth` units:** FreeRTOS uses *words* (4 bytes), not bytes. A `usStackDepth = 100` means **400 bytes**, not 100. Losing 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting the ceiling in the response-time equation:** Must use $\lceil R_i / T_j \rceil$, not just $R_i / T_j$. Losing 1 mark.
> 3. **Writing "process" instead of "thread":** In TCB/lifecycle questions, mixing terminology loses coherence marks.
> 4. **Not stating units** in numerical answers (e.g., "speedup is 2.29" without "$\times$" or "times"). Losing 0.5 mark.
> 5. **Skipping the priority-inversion Mars Pathfinder reference** in Part B — it's the canonical "real-world" example KTU examiners reward.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Save this list for last-night revision:**
>
> ☐ **Thread = lightweight process** sharing address space, owning stack + registers.
>
> ☐ **Five states:** Created, Ready, Running, Blocked, Terminated — know the transition arrows.
>
> ☐ **TCB** stores: SP, PC, registers, priority, state, list pointers, stack watermark.
>
> ☐ **Context switch time** on Cortex-M: **30–80 cycles ≈ 0.18–0.48 µs @ 168 MHz**.
>
> ☐ **Amdahl's Law:** $S(N) = 1 / [(1 - P) + P/N]$ — serial fraction caps the speedup.
>
> ☐ **Liu-Layland bound:** $U_{bound}(N) = N(2^{1/N} - 1)$ — sufficient condition for RMS schedulability.
>
> ☐ **RMS rule:** shorter period = higher priority; it is *optimal* among static-priority algorithms.
>
> ☐ **Priority Inversion** → use **Priority Inheritance Protocol** (`PTHREAD_PRIO_INHERIT`).
>
> ☐ **Synchronization primitives:** mutex, semaphore (binary/counting), condition variable, spinlock.
>
> ☐ **FreeRTOS stack units:** `usStackDepth` is in **words (4 bytes on Cortex-M)**, not bytes.
>
> ☐ **Mars Pathfinder (1997)** is the canonical priority-inversion case study.
>
> ☐ **Memory budget:** $M_{tot} = N \times (S_{stack} + O_{TCB})$ — plan RAM before spawning threads.
>
> ☐ **User threads** = fast, no kernel awareness. **Kernel threads** = slower, true parallelism, individual blocking.
>
> ☐ **Round-Robin** uses time slices; **Preemptive Priority** uses fixed priorities; **RMS** uses periods.
>
> ☐ **For 14-mark Part B questions**, always include: (1) labeled diagram, (2) step-by-step calculation, (3) box the final answer, (4) one practical/real-world remark.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT631 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Process Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Embedded System Design**, **Process Management** refers to the set of operating system (OS) and real-time operating system (RTOS) mechanisms responsible for creating, scheduling, synchronizing, terminating, and communicating between concurrent units of execution (processes and threads) on an embedded target platform. It is the kernel-level subsystem that allocates the CPU, memory, and I/O resources to tasks so that the embedded application meets its real-time deadlines.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Board-Exact):**
> A *process* is a program in execution, comprising the executable code, a dedicated stack, a heap, data section, and a Process Control Block (PCB) maintained by the kernel. *Process management* is the kernel's collection of policies and routines that govern the lifecycle and resource allocation of these processes/threads.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an embedded system as a **busy hospital emergency room**. The patients are *tasks* (processes), the doctors are *CPU cores*, and the head nurse is the *scheduler*.

- Patients keep arriving with different urgency levels (a heart attack = hard real-time deadline, a routine checkup = soft real-time).
- The head nurse (scheduler) decides which patient the doctor (CPU) sees next, based on priority.
- The medical charts that travel with each patient are the **Process Control Blocks (PCBs)** — they record vitals, history, and current state.
- The hospital's intercom and patient transfer protocols are the **Inter-Process Communication (IPC)** mechanisms.

When the doctor leaves one patient to attend another, the nurse carefully files the chart (context save) so that work can be resumed exactly where it left off (context restore).

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics in Process Management

- **CPU Utilization** — percentage of time the processor is busy (target **> 90%** in well-tuned embedded systems).
- **Throughput** — number of processes completed per unit time (typically **processes/sec**).
- **Turnaround Time (TAT)** — total time from process submission to completion, $T_{AT} = T_{completion} - T_{arrival}$.
- **Waiting Time (WT)** — time spent by a process in the ready queue.
- **Response Time (RT)** — time from submission to first CPU allocation, critical for interactive/real-time systems.
- **Deadline Miss Ratio** — fraction of tasks that miss their deadlines (must be **0** for hard real-time).
- **Context Switch Overhead** — typically **1–10 µs** on modern ARM Cortex-M based MCUs with an RTOS.

> [!NOTE]
> In embedded RTOS contexts, the critical metrics are **latency** (worst-case interrupt latency, typically **< 1 µs**) and **determinism**, NOT throughput.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Process Control Block (PCB) / Task Control Block (TCB)

Every process has an associated data structure in the kernel:

- **Process ID (PID)**
- **Process State** (New, Ready, Running, Blocked/Waiting, Terminated)
- **Program Counter (PC)**
- **CPU Registers** (general-purpose, status/flags)
- **Memory Management Info** (base/limit registers, page tables)
- **Scheduling Information** (priority, quantum, scheduler parameters)
- **List of Open Files / I/O Handles**
- **Accounting Information** (CPU time used, deadlines)

### 2.2 Process States and Transitions (Five-State Model)

The classical state machine embedded in every OS textbook:

1. **New** → Process is being created.
2. **Ready** → Process is loaded in main memory, waiting for CPU.
3. **Running** → Instructions are executing on the CPU.
4. **Blocked / Waiting** → Process cannot run until some event occurs (I/O completion, semaphore, timeout).
5. **Terminated** → Process has finished execution.

Transitions:
- New → Ready (admitted)
- Ready → Running (scheduler dispatch)
- Running → Ready (preempted by higher-priority task or quantum expiry)
- Running → Blocked (I/O request or wait)
- Blocked → Ready (I/O completion / event signaled)
- Running → Terminated (exit or abort)

### 2.3 Threads vs Processes

| Feature | Process | Thread |
|---|---|---|
| Address Space | Independent | Shared within process |
| Creation Cost | Heavy | Light |
| Context Switch | Expensive | Cheap |
| Communication | IPC needed | Shared memory |
| Embedded Use | Rare | **Common (RTOS tasks)** |

In **RTOS** environments (FreeRTOS, VxWorks, RT-Linux), threads (called *tasks*) are the primary execution units.

### 2.4 Scheduling Algorithms (Critical for KTU)

**A. First-Come, First-Served (FCFS)** — non-preemptive; simple but high average waiting time (convoy effect).

**B. Shortest Job First (SJF)** — non-preemptive; optimal for minimizing average waiting time, but impractical (requires burst-time knowledge).

**C. Round Robin (RR)** — preemptive; each process gets a *time quantum* $q$. Best for time-sharing; not suitable for hard real-time.

**D. Priority Scheduling** — each process has a priority; can be preemptive or non-preemptive. Risk of **priority inversion** (addressed by *priority inheritance protocol*).

**E. Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)** — fixed priorities; shorter period ⇒ higher priority. Optimal static-priority algorithm.

**F. Earliest Deadline First (EDF)** — dynamic priority; task with earliest deadline runs first. Optimal for uniprocessor dynamic-priority scheduling.

### 2.5 Rate Monotonic Schedulability Bound

For $n$ periodic tasks on a single CPU under Rate Monotonic Scheduling, a sufficient (but not necessary) condition for schedulability is:

$$U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \leq n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)$$

where $C_i$ is the worst-case execution time and $T_i$ is the period of task $i$.

For $n \to \infty$, the bound approaches $\ln 2 \approx 0.693$.

### 2.6 Real-Time Task Timing Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Arrival time | $a_i$ | Time task becomes ready |
| Execution time | $C_i$ | Worst-case computation time |
| Period | $T_i$ | Recurrence interval |
| Relative deadline | $D_i$ | Max time after arrival |
| Absolute deadline | $d_i$ | $a_i + D_i$ |
| Response time | $R_i$ | Completion $-$ release time |
| Slack / Laxity | $L_i$ | $D_i - C_i$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** For hard real-time systems, the Liu & Layland bound $U \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ is sufficient but **not necessary**. Exact Response Time Analysis (RTA) is the precise test:
> $$R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$$
> A task is schedulable iff $R_i \leq D_i$.

### 2.7 Inter-Process Communication (IPC) Mechanisms

- **Shared Memory** — fastest; requires synchronization.
- **Message Queues** — fixed-size messages; used heavily in RTOS (e.g., FreeRTOS queues).
- **Pipes / Named Pipes** — unidirectional byte streams.
- **Semaphores** — counting or binary, used for resource locking.
- **Mutexes** — binary semaphore with priority inheritance.
- **Mailboxes** — short message exchange.
- **Signals / Event Flags** — asynchronous notifications.

### 2.8 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Automotive ECUs**: Engine control tasks scheduled via OSEK/VDX with deadline-bounded scheduling.
- **Avionics (DO-178C)**: ARINC 653 partition scheduling with strict time/spatial isolation.
- **IoT Edge Devices**: FreeRTOS task management on ARM Cortex-M microcontrollers.
- **Industrial Robotics**: Preemptive priority scheduling with deterministic cycle times (e.g., EtherCAT).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: CPU Utilization under Round Robin

Let $N$ processes be in ready queue and $q$ be the time quantum. Each process gets $q$ time units per cycle. Time to give each process one turn = $N \cdot q$. If no process finishes immediately, average turnaround is:

$$\text{Throughput}_{RR} = \frac{1}{N \cdot q} \quad \text{[process completions per second, ideal case]}$$

If average burst time is $b$, average turnaround is:

$$T_{AT} \approx \frac{(N-1) \cdot q + b}{1}$$

**Observation:** smaller $q$ improves response time but increases context-switch overhead. Optimal $q \approx 80\%$ of CPU burst time.

### 3.2 Worked Derivation: Rate Monotonic Schedulability for 3 Tasks

Consider 3 periodic tasks on an ARM Cortex-M4 embedded controller:

| Task | Period $T_i$ (ms) | Execution $C_i$ (ms) |
|---|---|---|
| $\tau_1$ | 20 | 4 |
| $\tau_2$ | 40 | 8 |
| $\tau_3$ | 80 | 10 |

**Step 1:** Compute utilization $U_i = C_i / T_i$ for each task:

$$\begin{aligned}
U_1 &= \frac{4}{20} = 0.200 \\
U_2 &= \frac{8}{40} = 0.200 \\
U_3 &= \frac{10}{80} = 0.125
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Compute total utilization $U$:

$$U = U_1 + U_2 + U_3 = 0.200 + 0.200 + 0.125 = 0.525$$

**Step 3:** Compute the Liu–Layland sufficient bound for $n = 3$:

$$U_{bound} = 3 \cdot \left( 2^{1/3} - 1 \right) = 3 \cdot (1.2599 - 1) = 3 \cdot 0.2599 = 0.7797$$

**Step 4:** Compare:

$$U = 0.525 \leq U_{bound} = 0.7797$$

Therefore, **the task set is schedulable under Rate Monotonic Scheduling**.

**Step 5 (Optional):** Confirm with exact Response Time Analysis. Priority order: $\tau_1$ (highest) → $\tau_2$ → $\tau_3$ (lowest).

For $\tau_1$: $R_1 = 4 \leq D_1 = 20$ ✓

For $\tau_2$: solve $R_2 = 8 + \lceil R_2/20 \rceil \cdot 4$. Iterate:
- $R_2^{(0)} = 8$
- $R_2^{(1)} = 8 + \lceil 8/20 \rceil \cdot 4 = 8 + 4 = 12$
- $R_2^{(2)} = 8 + \lceil 12/20 \rceil \cdot 4 = 12$ (fixed) ≤ 40 ✓

For $\tau_3$: $R_3 = 10 + \lceil R_3/20 \rceil \cdot 4 + \lceil R_3/40 \rceil \cdot 8$. Iterate:
- $R_3^{(0)} = 10$
- $R_3^{(1)} = 10 + 4 + 8 = 22$
- $R_3^{(2)} = 10 + \lceil 22/20 \rceil \cdot 4 + \lceil 22/40 \rceil \cdot 8 = 10 + 8 + 8 = 26$
- $R_3^{(3)} = 10 + \lceil 26/20 \rceil \cdot 4 + \lceil 26/40 \rceil \cdot 8 = 10 + 8 + 8 = 26$ ≤ 80 ✓

**Conclusion:** All three tasks meet their deadlines under RMS.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Scheduling Algorithm Selection for Embedded Systems

| Algorithm | Preemptive? | Best For | Limitation | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| FCFS | No | Batch, simple systems | Convoy effect | Theory only |
| SJF | Optional | Minimum avg WT | Unknown burst times | Theory only |
| Round Robin | Yes | Time-sharing | Poor real-time behavior | OS module |
| Priority | Yes | Mixed-criticality | Priority inversion | RTOS module |
| RMS | Yes (static) | Hard periodic tasks | $n \cdot (2^{1/n}-1)$ bound | Embedded RT |
| EDF | Yes (dynamic) | Hard aperiodic + periodic | High runtime overhead | Advanced RT |

### 3.4 Worked Example: Average Waiting Time for FCFS

Processes with arrival times and burst times:

| Process | Arrival | Burst |
|---|---|---|
| P1 | 0 | 7 |
| P2 | 1 | 4 |
| P3 | 2 | 1 |
| P4 | 3 | 4 |

Gantt chart under FCFS (non-preemptive): `| P1 (0–7) | P2 (7–11) | P3 (11–12) | P4 (12–16) |`

Completion times: P1=7, P2=11, P3=12, P4=16.
Turnaround: P1=7, P2=10, P3=10, P4=13.
Waiting time = TAT − Burst:
- P1: 7 − 7 = 0
- P2: 10 − 4 = 6
- P3: 10 − 1 = 9
- P4: 13 − 4 = 9

$$\overline{WT} = \frac{0 + 6 + 9 + 9}{4} = \frac{24}{4} = 6 \text{ ms}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Five-State Process Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> New
    New --> Ready: Admit
    Ready --> Running: Scheduler Dispatch
    Running --> Ready: Preempt or Quantum Expiry
    Running --> Blocked: I/O or Event Wait
    Blocked --> Ready: I/O Complete or Event Signaled
    Running --> Terminated: Exit
    Terminated --> [*]
```

### 4.2 RTOS Task Lifecycle (Embedded View)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Dormant
    Dormant --> Ready: xTaskCreate
    Ready --> Running: Scheduler Picks Task
    Running --> Ready: Time Slice or Yield
    Running --> Blocked: xQueueReceive or Delay
    Blocked --> Ready: Event or Timeout
    Running --> Suspended: vTaskSuspend
    Suspended --> Ready: vTaskResume
    Running --> [*]: vTaskDelete
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Process Manager

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Application Code] --> B[API Layer]
    B --> C[Process Manager Kernel]
    C --> D[Scheduler]
    C --> E[Dispatcher]
    C --> F[IPC Subsystem]
    C --> G[Memory Manager]
    D --> H[Priority Queue]
    D --> I[Timer Tick ISR]
    E --> J[Context Switch Engine]
    J --> K[CPU Core]
    F --> L[Semaphores]
    F --> M[Message Queues]
    F --> N[Mutexes]
    G --> O[Heap and Stack Allocator]
    I --> D
    H --> D
```

### 4.4 Priority Inversion and Inheritance Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant H as High Priority Task
    participant M as Medium Priority Task
    participant L as Low Priority Task
    participant K as Kernel
    L->>K: Lock Mutex
    K-->>L: Acquired
    H->>K: Preempt L
    H->>K: Try Lock Mutex
    K-->>H: Blocked (waiting on L)
    M->>K: Preempt L
    M->>M: Runs to completion
    L->>K: Release Mutex
    K->>K: Boost L to H's priority
    L->>L: Finishes critical section
    K-->>H: Mutex granted
    K->>K: Restore L's priority
```
