T3 runs $15 \to 16$. **Gantt Chart:** $$ \begin{aligned} &\vert 0 \;\: 2 \;\: \quad\quad 6 \;\: \quad 10 \quad 15 \quad 16 \vert \\ &\vert T1 \vert T2 \vert T4 \vert T1 \vert T3 \vert \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — Compute TAT, WT:** | Task | $r_i$ | $C_i$ | $CT_i$ | $TAT = CT - r$ | $WT = TAT - C$ | |------|-------|-------|--------|----------------|----------------| | T1 | 0 | 7 | 15 | 15 | 8 | | T2 | 2 | 4 | 6 | 4 | 0 | | T3 | 4 | 1 | 16 | 12 | 11 | | T4 | 5 | 4 | 10 | 5 | 1 | $$ AWT = \frac{8 + 0 + 11 + 1}{4} = \frac{20}{4} = 5.0 \text{ ms} $$ $$ ATT = \frac{15 + 4 + 12 + 5}{4} = \frac{36}{4} = 9.0 \text{ ms} $$ --- **Part (b) — Context-Switch Analysis (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Count context switches:** A switch occurs every time the running task changes. 1. $t = 0$: Idle $\to$ T1 (1st switch) 2. $t = 2$: T1 $\to$ T2 (2nd switch) 3. $t = 6$: T2 $\to$ T4 (3rd switch) 4. $t = 10$: T4 $\to$ T1 (4th switch) 5. $t = 15$: T1 $\to$ T3 (5th switch) 6. $t = 16$: T3 $\to$ Idle (6th switch) **Total Context Switches = 6** > Some textbooks count only *forced* (involuntary) switches: that gives **4** (T1$\to$T2, T2$\to$T4, T4$\to$T1, T1$\to$T3). For KTU, the inclusive count (6) is generally accepted. (Mention both for safety.) **Step 2 — Total Overhead:** $$ \Xi_{total} = 6 \times \xi = 6 \times 0.5 \text{ ms} = 3.0 \text{ ms} $$ **Step 3 — Effective CPU Useful Time:** $$ T_{useful} = 16 - 3 = 13 \text{ ms}, \quad \eta = \frac{13}{16} = 81.25\% $$ So **3 ms** of the 16 ms total makespan is overhead. In a hard real-time system, this overhead must be **budgeted inside $C_i$** to remain schedulable. --- ### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] **a)** Explain **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)** with a suitable example of 3 periodic tasks. Verify schedulability using the **Liu-Layland bound** and **exact response-time analysis**. (7 marks) **b)** Compare RMS with **Earliest Deadline First (EDF)** in terms of priority assignment, schedulability bound, runtime overhead, and best-use scenario. (7 marks) --- #### Solution to Question B: **Part (a) — RMS with 3 tasks (7 marks):** **Algorithm Definition (1 mark):** RMS assigns a *fixed, static* priority to each task. The priority is inversely proportional to the period $T_i$. The task with the smallest period gets the highest priority. **Worked Example (3 marks):** Consider the following task set: | Task | $C_i$ | $T_i = D_i$ | Priority (assigned) | |------|-------|-------------|---------------------| | $\tau_1$ | 1 ms | 4 ms | **Highest** (1) | | $\tau_2$ | 2 ms | 6 ms | Middle (2) | | $\tau_3$ | 3 ms | 10 ms | Lowest (3) | **Step 1 — Liu-Layland bound check (1.5 marks):** $$ U = \frac{1}{4} + \frac{2}{6} + \frac{3}{10} = 0.2500 + 0.3333 + 0.3000 = 0.8833 $$ $$ U_{bound}(3) = 3 \cdot (2^{1/3} - 1) \approx 0.7797 $$ Since $U = 0.8833 > 0.7797$, the *sufficient* test is **inconclusive**. We must perform exact analysis. **Step 2 — Exact Response-Time Analysis (1.5 marks):** As derived in §3.3, $R_1 = 1$, $R_2 = 3$, $R_3 = 10$ ms. Since $R_i \leq D_i$ for all tasks, the system is **schedulable under RMS**. **Conclusion:** RMS *can* schedule task sets with $U > 0.693$ — the Liu-Layland bound is **sufficient, not necessary**. --- **Part (b) — RMS vs EDF Comparison (7 marks):** | Criterion | **RMS** | **EDF** | |---|---|---| | **Priority Assignment (1.5 marks)** | Static, based on period ($P_i \propto 1/T_i$). Fixed at design time. | Dynamic, based on *current absolute deadline* at each scheduling decision. | | **Schedulability Bound (2 marks)** | $U \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$, max $\approx 0.693$ for large $n$. Sufficient only. | $U \leq 1.0$ is **necessary and sufficient** on uniprocessor. | | **Runtime Overhead (1.5 marks)** | Low: O(1) priority lookup with bit-mask or fixed array. Ideal for bare-metal/automotive. | Higher: requires sorted ready queue (e.g., min-heap) to find earliest deadline ⇒ O(log n) per dispatch. | | **Transient Overload Behavior (1 mark)** | Predictable: low-priority tasks miss first. Good for graceful degradation. | *Domino effect*: any task missing deadline can cascade. | | **Best-Use Scenario (1 mark)** | Hard real-time with *static* workload (AUTOSAR, avionics DO-178C). | Dynamic / mixed-criticality systems (multi-media, soft real-time). | --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Traps in KTU 2024 Evaluation:** > 1. **Forgetting to add a row for the arrival of T2 at t=2** in pre-emptive Gantt charts — students often draw FCFS-style continuous execution. **Deduction: up to 2 marks.** > 2. **Confusing Turnaround Time vs Response Time.** $TAT_i = CT_i - r_i$ ; $RT_i = (first\ start\ time) - r_i$. RT $\leq$ TAT always. > 3. **Using the wrong direction of priority convention.** If the problem says "lower number = higher priority", you must follow it. Mention it in the solution. > 4. **Applying Liu-Layland as if it were a necessary test.** If $U > 0.693$, the test is *inconclusive*, not a failure. Use Response-Time Analysis to conclude. > 5. **Ignoring context-switch overhead in schedulability.** A real-time system may be theoretically schedulable but fail in practice because $\xi$ is not included in $C_i$. > 6. **Starvation discussion missing** in Priority Scheduling — always mention it; it's a favorite 2-mark follow-up. > 7. **Mixing pre-emptive and non-pre-emptive rules** in the same solution. State your assumption at the top of the answer. --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [x] **Non-Pre-emptive** scheduling runs a task to completion or until voluntary block — *no* forced preemption. Low overhead, poor responsiveness. - [x] **Pre-emptive** scheduling can be interrupted by timer or higher-priority arrival — *higher overhead*, superior for real-time. - [x] **FCFS**: FIFO order; suffers from *Convoy Effect*. - [x] **SJF**: Optimal average waiting time in non-preemptive case; *starves long jobs*. - [x] **Priority Scheduling**: Pre-emptive variant responds instantly to high-priority arrivals; risks *unbounded priority inversion* — solve with **PIP / PCP**. - [x] **Round Robin**: Time quantum $q$ governs fairness; small $q$ ⇒ high overhead, large $q$ ⇒ degenerates to FCFS. - [x] **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)**: Static priority $\propto 1/T_i$; sufficient test $U \leq n(2^{1/n}-1) \to 0.693$. - [x] **Exact Response-Time Analysis (Joseph-Pandya)**: $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil R_i/T_j \rceil C_j$; find smallest fixed point. Compare with $D_i$. - [x] **Earliest Deadline First (EDF)**: Dynamic priority; **optimal** uniprocessor; schedulability iff $U \leq 1$. - [x] **Least Laxity First (LLF)**: Dynamic; priority $\propto 1/(D_i - t - C_{i,rem})$; used when $D_i \neq T_i$. - [x] **Context-Switch Overhead** $\xi$ must be **included in $C_i$** for any valid hard real-time analysis. - [x] **Hard vs Soft vs Firm Deadlines**: Hard = miss = catastrophe; Soft = miss = degraded QoS; Firm = miss = no value (drop frame). - [x] **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)**: A low-priority task that blocks a high-priority task *temporarily inherits* the highest blocked priority. - [x] **Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP)**: Each resource has a ceiling; task acquiring it runs at the ceiling — *prevents* deadlocks and chained blocking. - [x] **Examples in practice**: FreeRTOS (pre-emptive, configurable), VxWorks (pre-emptive, POSIX), QNX (pre-emptive, microkernel), TinyOS (non-pre-emptive, event-driven), $\mu$C/OS-II (pre-emptive, fixed-priority). - [x] **KTU mnemonic:** *"RMS is Round, RMS is Static, EDF is Earliest, LLF is Laxity."* --- > **End of Module 4 Notes — Task Scheduling Algorithms** > *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020) — Outcome-Based Education Framework*
